# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*State the principle of virtual work as applied to the static analysis of robotic manipulators. Derive the relationship between joint torques and end-effector forces.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Principle of Virtual Work** states that for a system in static equilibrium, the total virtual work done by all active forces (joint torques and external forces) for any virtual displacement consistent with the constraints is zero.

- Virtual work at the end-effector: $\delta W_{ext} = \mathbf{F}_{ext}^T \delta \mathbf{x}$
- End-effector virtual displacement in joint space: $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \delta \mathbf{q}$
- Virtual work by joint torques: $\delta W_{joint} = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q}$

Setting $\delta W_{joint} = \delta W_{ext}$ and using the arbitrariness of $\delta \mathbf{q}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})\, \mathbf{F}_{ext}$$

This is the static torque equation. **(3 marks — 1 mark for PVW statement, 1 mark for Jacobian relation, 1 mark for final formula)**

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between forward dynamics and inverse dynamics. Give one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Inverse Dynamics | Forward Dynamics |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| **Given** | $\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}, \ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\boldsymbol{\tau}, \mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ |
| **Find** | $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ |
| **Equation** | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ | $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{M}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G})$ |
| **Application** | Computed-torque control, motor sizing | Robot simulation, animation |

**(1 mark each for input/output, 1 mark for applications)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with Dec 2024 pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the Euler-Lagrange equation of motion for a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $l_1, l_2$, point masses $m_1, m_2$ at the distal end of each link. Clearly identify the mass matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$, Coriolis/centrifugal matrix $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}})$, and gravity vector $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same manipulator, with $l_1 = l_2 = 1$ m, $m_1 = m_2 = 2$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², compute the static joint torques at the configuration $q_1 = 30°$, $q_2 = 45°$ when the end-effector carries a payload of 5 kg. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Position Vectors**

Let the end-effector position be:
$$x = l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2),\quad y = l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

Position of CoM of link 1 (at $l_1/2$):
$$x_1 = \frac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1,\quad y_1 = \frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1$$

Position of CoM of link 2:
$$x_2 = l_1\cos q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2),\quad y_2 = l_1\sin q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

**Step 2: Velocities (Differentiate)**

$$
\dot{x}_1 = -\frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1\,\dot{q}_1,\quad \dot{y}_1 = \frac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1\,\dot{q}_1
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -l_1\sin q_1\,\dot{q}_1 - \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

$$
\dot{y}_2 = \;\;\;l_1\cos q_1\,\dot{q}_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

**Step 3: Kinetic Energy**

$$
\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}m_1(\dot{x}_1^2+\dot{y}_1^2) + \frac{1}{2}m_2(\dot{x}_2^2+\dot{y}_2^2)
$$

After algebra:

$$
\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{m_1 l_1^2}{4} + m_2 l_1^2 + \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right]\dot{q}_1^2
$$

$$
+\; \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4}\right]\dot{q}_2^2 + \left[\frac{m_2 l_1 l_2}{4}\cos q_2 + \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{8}\right]\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2
$$

Identifying $\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \mathbf{M} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$:

$$
M_{11} = \frac{m_1 l_1^2}{4} + m_2 l_1^2 + \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2
$$

$$
M_{12} = M_{21} = \frac{m_2 l_1 l_2}{2}\cos q_2 + \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4}
$$

$$
M_{22} = \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4}
$$

(Use $\mathbf{M}$ as the **mass matrix** — symmetric, positive definite.)

**Step 4: Potential Energy**

$$
\mathcal{P} = m_1 g \frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1 + m_2 g \left[l_1 \sin q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\right]
$$

**Step 5: Euler-Lagrange for Each Joint**

For $q_1$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_1} = \tau_1
$$

After full expansion (intermediate Christoffel terms shown):

$$
\tau_1 = M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + C_{111}\dot{q}_1^2 + 2C_{112}\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + C_{122}\dot{q}_2^2 + G_1
$$

For $q_2$:

$$
\tau_2 = M_{21}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 + C_{211}\dot{q}_1^2 + 2C_{212}\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + C_{222}\dot{q}_2^2 + G_2
$$

**Step 6: Gravity Vector**

$$
G_1 = \frac{\partial \mathcal{P}}{\partial q_1} = m_1 g \frac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1 + m_2 g \left[l_1 \cos q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)\right]
$$

$$
G_2 = \frac{\partial \mathcal{P}}{\partial q_2} = m_2 g \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

**Step 7: Coriolis Terms** (from Christoffel symbols):

$$
C_{111} = 0,\quad C_{122} = -\frac{m_2 l_1 l_2}{2}\cos q_2
$$

$$
C_{112} = C_{212} = -\frac{m_2 l_1 l_2}{4}\cos q_2
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{21} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})}\begin{bmatrix}\ddot{q}_1\\ \ddot{q}_2\end{bmatrix} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_2 & -\frac{1}{2}m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_2 \\ \frac{1}{2}m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})}\begin{bmatrix}\dot{q}_1\\ \dot{q}_2\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} G_1 \\ G_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(Marks distribution: 2 marks for KE+PE, 2 marks for Mass matrix, 2 marks for Gravity, 1 mark for Coriolis matrix form.)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $l_1 = l_2 = 1$ m, $m_1 = m_2 = 2$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², payload $m_p = 5$ kg, $q_1 = 30°$, $q_2 = 45°$.

**Step 1: Compute trig values.**

$\cos 30° = 0.866$, $\sin 30° = 0.500$, $\cos 45° = 0.707$, $\sin 45° = 0.707$, $\cos(75°) = 0.259$, $\sin(75°) = 0.966$.

**Step 2: Static case** → $\dot{q}_1 = \dot{q}_2 = \ddot{q}_1 = \ddot{q}_2 = 0$. So only gravity term matters.

For static analysis, treat the end-effector force due to payload as $\mathbf{F}_{ext} = [0, -m_p g]^T = [0, -5 \times 9.81]^T = [0, -49.05]^T$ N.

**Step 3: Compute Jacobian at the given configuration.**

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -1(0.500) - 1(0.966) & -1(0.966) \\ 1(0.866) + 1(0.259) & 1(0.259) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.466 & -0.966 \\ 1.125 & 0.259 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_{ext}$ (payload contribution).**

$$
\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_{ext} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.466 & 1.125 \\ -0.966 & 0.259 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -49.05 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.125 \times (-49.05) \\ 0.259 \times (-49.05) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -55.18 \\ -12.70 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5: Compute gravity torque from link weights.**

Total mass at CoM of link 1: $m_1 = 2$ kg. Gravity force on link 1 at CoM position: $\mathbf{F}_{g1} = [0, -2 \times 9.81]^T = [0, -19.62]^T$ N.

Jacobian of CoM of link 1:

$$
\mathbf{J}_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1 & 0 \\ \frac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.500 \times 0.500 & 0 \\ 0.500 \times 0.866 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.250 & 0 \\ 0.433 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Wait — re-check: $\frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1 = 0.5 \times 0.500 = 0.250$, and $\frac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1 = 0.5 \times 0.866 = 0.433$.

$$
\mathbf{J}_{v_1}^T \mathbf{F}_{g1} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.250 & 0.433 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -19.62 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -8.50 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}
$$

Total mass at CoM of link 2: $m_2 = 2$ kg. Gravity force on link 2: $\mathbf{F}_{g2} = [0, -19.62]^T$ N.

Jacobian of CoM of link 2:

$$
\mathbf{J}_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 - \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) & -\frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1\cos q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) & \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting $l_1 = 1$, $l_2/2 = 0.5$:

$$
\mathbf{J}_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -1(0.500) - 0.5(0.966) & -0.5(0.966) \\ 1(0.866) + 0.5(0.259) & 0.5(0.259) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.983 & -0.483 \\ 0.996 & 0.130 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{J}_{v_2}^T \mathbf{F}_{g2} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.983 & 0.996 \\ -0.483 & 0.130 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -19.62 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -19.54 \\ -2.55 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 6: Total static torque.**

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{static} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_{ext} + \mathbf{J}_{v_1}^T \mathbf{F}_{g1} + \mathbf{J}_{v_2}^T \mathbf{F}_{g2}
$$

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{static} = \begin{bmatrix} -55.18 \\ -12.70 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -8.50 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -19.54 \\ -2.55 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -83.22 \\ -15.25 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}
$$

**Interpretation:** Joint 1 must supply ~83.2 N·m to hold the arm + payload against gravity, while joint 2 needs ~15.3 N·m. The negative signs indicate the torques oppose gravity.

**(Marks distribution: 1 mark Jacobian, 1 mark payload force, 2 marks gravity contributions, 2 marks final sum, 1 mark for interpretation/sign convention.)**

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with June 2024 pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the Newton-Euler formulation for the inverse dynamics of a robotic manipulator. List the kinematic quantities computed in the outward pass and the dynamic quantities computed in the inward pass. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-link manipulator rotates about a vertical axis. The link has mass $m = 3$ kg, length $l = 0.8$ m, treated as a uniform rod. Using the Euler-Lagrange method, derive the equation of motion. Compute the joint torque required to drive the link from rest to angular velocity $\omega = 2$ rad/s with angular acceleration $\alpha = 1$ rad/s² in horizontal position. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction to Newton-Euler (1 mark):**
The Newton-Euler formulation derives the dynamic equations of a manipulator by recursive application of Newton's second law (force balance: $\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}$) and Euler's equation (moment balance: $\mathbf{n} = \mathbf{I}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} + \boldsymbol{\omega}\times(\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$) to each link. It involves two passes:

**Outward Pass — Kinematics (3 marks):**
Starting from the base (link 0) and moving toward the end-effector (link $n$), compute for each link $i$:
- $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$ — angular velocity of link $i$
- $\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i$ — angular acceleration of link $i$
- $\mathbf{a}_i$ — linear acceleration of link $i$ origin
- $\mathbf{a}_{c_i}$ — linear acceleration of link $i$ center of mass

For a revolute joint:
$$\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$$
$$\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i = \dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i-1} + \ddot{q}_i \hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}\times(\dot{q}_i \hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1})$$
$$\mathbf{a}_{c_i} = \mathbf{a}_i + \dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i\times\mathbf{r}_{i,c_i} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i\times(\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\times\mathbf{r}_{i,c_i})$$

For a prismatic joint, similar expressions with the joint translation axis instead of rotation.

**Inward Pass — Dynamics (3 marks):**
Starting from the end-effector (link $n$) and moving back to the base, compute for each link $i$:
- $\mathbf{F}_i$ — net force on link $i$: $\mathbf{F}_i = m_i \mathbf{a}_{c_i}$
- $\mathbf{n}_i$ — net moment on link $i$: $\mathbf{n}_i = \mathbf{I}_i \dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i\times(\mathbf{I}_i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$
- Force/torque balance at joint to extract joint torque:
$$\tau_i = \mathbf{n}_i^T \hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1} \quad \text{(revolute)}$$
$$\tau_i = \mathbf{F}_i^T \hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1} \quad \text{(prismatic)}$$

The inward pass applies Newton's third law: the force/moment link $i$ exerts on link $i+1$ is the negative of the reaction from $i+1$ on $i$.

**Complexity (Bonus 1 mark):** The algorithm runs in $O(n)$ time per step, making it ideal for real-time control at kHz rates.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Setup.**
- Uniform rod, mass $m = 3$ kg, length $l = 0.8$ m.
- Center of mass at $l/2 = 0.4$ m from pivot.
- Moment of inertia about pivot (using parallel axis theorem): $I_{pivot} = \frac{1}{12}ml^2 + m(l/2)^2 = \frac{1}{3}ml^2 = \frac{1}{3}(3)(0.8)^2 = 0.64$ kg·m².

**Step 2: Kinetic Energy.**

$$\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}I_{pivot}\dot{q}^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.64)\dot{q}^2 = 0.32\dot{q}^2$$

**Step 3: Potential Energy.**

Since the link rotates about a **vertical axis** and the rod is **horizontal**, the height of the center of mass does **not change** with rotation. Thus $\mathcal{P} = 0$, and $G(q) = 0$.

**Step 4: Lagrangian and Equation of Motion.**

$$\mathcal{L} = 0.32\dot{q}^2$$

Applying Euler-Lagrange:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) = \frac{d}{dt}(0.64\dot{q}) = 0.64\ddot{q}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q} = 0$$

Thus:
$$\tau = 0.64\ddot{q}$$

**Step 5: Substitute given values.**

Given $\omega = 2$ rad/s, $\alpha = 1$ rad/s²:

$$\tau = 0.64 \times 1 = 0.64 \text{ N·m}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Since gravity acts along the rotation axis (vertical pivot, horizontal arm), there is **no gravity torque**, no Coriolis (single DOF), and only the **inertial term** contributes. The motor must supply 0.64 N·m purely to accelerate the link.

**(Marks distribution: 1 mark inertia calculation, 1 mark KE, 1 mark PE, 2 marks Euler-Lagrange derivation, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final answer + interpretation.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to transpose the Jacobian.** Students often write $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}\mathbf{F}_{ext}$ instead of $\mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{F}_{ext}$. The transpose is **mandatory** — dimensions won't even match otherwise ($\mathbf{J}$ is $m \times n$, $\mathbf{F}_{ext}$ is $m \times 1$, product $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{F}_{ext}$ is $m \times 1$ but torque should be $n \times 1$).
> 2. **Confusing sign conventions.** A negative torque doesn't mean "less torque" — it means the torque **opposes** the positive joint direction. Always interpret with a free-body diagram.
> 3. **Skipping the gravity term in static analysis.** A common error is computing only $\mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{F}_{ext}$ and forgetting that **link weights themselves** create static torques. KTU examiners specifically allocate 1–2 marks for this.
> 4. **Mixing up Coriolis and gravity.** Coriolis is **velocity-dependent**; gravity is **position-dependent**. If you see a $\dot{q}$ factor, it's Coriolis. If you see a $\sin q$ or $\cos q$ factor (with $g$), it's gravity.
> 5. **Not checking the mass matrix symmetry.** The mass matrix **must** be symmetric ($M_{ij} = M_{ji}$). If your final answer has $M_{12} \neq M_{21}$, you have made a kinetic-energy algebra error. **KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark for non-symmetric mass matrices.**

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Static Analysis & Dynamics Intro):**

- ☐ **Static Analysis** assumes $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$ and $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$. Only gravity and external forces matter.
- ☐ The **static torque equation** is $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{F}_{ext}$, derived from the **Principle of Virtual Work**.
- ☐ The Jacobian transpose converts **Cartesian forces → joint torques** (dual of the velocity relation $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$).
- ☐ The full **dynamic equation of motion** is $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$.
- ☐ The **mass matrix** $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **symmetric and positive-definite**; it depends only on $\mathbf{q}$.
- ☐ The **Coriolis matrix** $\mathbf{C}$ depends on both $\mathbf{q}$ and $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$; it is **quadratic in velocity**.
- ☐ The **gravity vector** $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \partial \mathcal{P}/\partial \mathbf{q}$ is the gradient of potential energy.
- ☐ The Lagrangian is $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P}$; Euler-Lagrange gives $\frac{d}{dt}(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{q}_i) - \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial q_i = \tau_i$.
- ☐ **Newton-Euler** = link-by-link recursive, $O(n)$ per step, used in real-time control.
- ☐ **Lagrangian** = energy-based global, $O(n^4)$ symbolic, used for analytical insight and derivation.
- ☐ **Inverse dynamics** = given motion, find torques (used in control).
- ☐ **Forward dynamics** = given torques, find accelerations (used in simulation).
- ☐ The **skew-symmetry property** $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is crucial for proving **stability of robot controllers** — memorize this!
- ☐ For a 2-DOF planar arm with horizontal payload, **maximum static torque at joint 1 occurs at horizontal extension** (worst-case design point).
- ☐ Always **check dimensions**: $\mathbf{M}$ is $n \times n$ (kg·m²), $\mathbf{C}$ is $n \times n$ (kg·m²/s), $\mathbf{G}$ is $n \times 1$ (N·m), $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is $n \times 1$ (N·m).

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Static Analysis of Manipulator & Introduction to Robot Dynamics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lagrangian Formulation (Overview) of Manipulator Dynamics. Linear Robot Dynamics: Force-Acceleration Relationship for 1-DOF System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY AND ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lagrangian Formulation (Overview) of Manipulator Dynamics. Linear Robot Dynamics: Force-Acceleration Relationship for 1-DOF System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lagrangian Formulation — Formal Definition

The **Lagrangian Formulation** is an energy-based analytical framework used to derive the equations of motion of a multi-body mechanical system (such as a robotic manipulator) without explicitly writing vector free-body diagrams for each link. It uses a single scalar function — the **Lagrangian**, defined as the difference between the **total kinetic energy (K)** and the **total potential energy (P)** of the system — and applies the **Euler-Lagrange equation** to generate the dynamic model.

For an n-degree-of-freedom manipulator, the Lagrangian is expressed in terms of **generalized coordinates** $q_i$ (typically the joint variables) as:

$$L(q, \dot{q}) = K(q, \dot{q}) - P(q)$$

The governing Euler-Lagrange equation for each generalized coordinate is:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

where $\tau_i$ is the **generalized force/torque** acting on the $i^{th}$ joint. The left side is a purely structural/energy expression; the right side converts the energy bookkeeping into physical actuator efforts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Euler-Lagrange equation is the *only* systematic way KTU expects students to derive the manipulator equation $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$. Remember that the manipulator equation is *not* assumed — it must be **derived** in the exam.

## 1.2 1-DOF Linear Robot Dynamics — Formal Definition

**Linear Robot Dynamics** refers to the study of robotic systems whose equations of motion are **linear** in the state variables (position and velocity). For a **single degree-of-freedom (1-DOF)** linear robotic system, the dynamic model is a linear, constant-coefficient, second-order ordinary differential equation (ODE) relating the applied force (input) to the resulting acceleration, velocity, and displacement (output).

The standard form, derived directly from Newton's Second Law combined with viscous damping and a linear elastic restoring element, is:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + b\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)$$

where $m$ is the equivalent mass, $b$ is the viscous damping coefficient, $k$ is the stiffness of the spring, and $F(t)$ is the externally applied force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundation Equation:** $F = ma$ is the *Newtonian* starting point. The Lagrangian formulation *must reduce* to this for a 1-DOF prismatic joint. If a student's derivation of a 1-DOF manipulator does not yield $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F$, the derivation is **incorrect**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### The "Energy Bank Account" Analogy (Lagrangian)
Imagine a robotic arm as a **bank account** that stores two kinds of currency: **Kinetic Energy (K)** — money in your pocket (energy of motion) — and **Potential Energy (P)** — money in a fixed deposit (energy of position, height, spring compression). The **Lagrangian L = K − P** is essentially the *spendable balance* — the energy available to do work. The **Euler-Lagrange equation** is like the bank's automated rule: *"the rate of change of spendable balance equals the deposits (external torques) the account receives."* By tracking only *scalar energies*, you avoid the messy trigonometry of resolving forces at every joint — the energy approach automatically handles gravity, inertia coupling, and Coriolis effects.

### The "Mass on a Spring in Honey" Analogy (1-DOF Linear Dynamics)
Picture a small cart of mass $m$ sliding on a frictionless rail, attached to a wall by a spring (stiffness $k$), and submerged in a tank of thick honey (viscous damping $b$). If you push the cart with a force $F$, the cart does **not** accelerate instantly — it must overcome three "resistances" simultaneously:
1. **Inertia** ($m\ddot{x}$): the cart's reluctance to change velocity.
2. **Damping** ($b\dot{x}$): the honey dragging the cart proportional to its speed.
3. **Stiffness** ($kx$): the spring pulling the cart back proportional to displacement.

The push force $F$ must supply *all three* demands: $F = m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx$. This is the **Force–Acceleration Relationship** for a 1-DOF system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Lagrangian Formulation

The Lagrangian method follows a strict **five-step pipeline**. KTU examiners award marks for each clearly demarcated step.

**Step 1 — Selection of Generalized Coordinates:**
Choose the minimum number of independent coordinates $q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n$ that completely describe the configuration of the manipulator. For an $n$-revolute-joint robot, the joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$ are the natural choice.

**Step 2 — Express Position and Velocity of Every Mass Element:**
For each link $i$, express the position $\vec{r}_i$ of its center of mass and its linear velocity $\dot{\vec{r}}_i$ in terms of the generalized coordinates and their time derivatives.

**Step 3 — Compute Kinetic and Potential Energy:**
- **Kinetic Energy:** $K = \frac{1}{2} m \dot{\vec{r}}^{\,T}\dot{\vec{r}} + \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ for each mass element.
- **Potential Energy:** $P = mgh$ for gravity, $P = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$ for springs.

**Step 4 — Form the Lagrangian $L = K - P$.**

**Step 5 — Apply the Euler-Lagrange Equation:**
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

This generates $n$ coupled second-order ODEs. Grouping terms yields the **standard manipulator equation**:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

where:
- $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **symmetric, positive-definite mass/inertia matrix**.
- $C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q}$ is the **Coriolis and centrifugal force vector**.
- $G(q)$ is the **gravitational torque vector**.
- $\tau$ is the **generalized joint force/torque vector**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why energy-based?** For a 6-DOF robot, writing Newton's equations requires resolving forces/moments along multiple axes with chain-rule coupling. The Lagrangian method needs only **scalar** $K$ and $P$ — it then automatically generates the equations in a structured, symmetric form.

## 2.2 Force-Acceleration Relationship for 1-DOF — The Three Forces

For a 1-DOF prismatic (linear) robotic system, three physical phenomena oppose the applied force:

| # | Physical Effect | Mathematical Term | Origin |
|---|----------------|-------------------|--------|
| 1 | Inertia (resistance to acceleration) | $m\ddot{x}$ | Newton's 2nd Law |
| 2 | Viscous Damping (resistance to motion) | $b\dot{x}$ | Friction / fluid drag |
| 3 | Elastic Stiffness (restoring force) | $kx$ | Spring / gravity gradient |

The applied force must balance all three:

$$F(t) = m\ddot{x}(t) + b\dot{x}(t) + kx(t)$$

This is rearranged into the **state-space form** with state vector $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ \dot{x} \end{bmatrix}$:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\frac{k}{m} & -\frac{b}{m} \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{x} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{m} \end{bmatrix}F(t)$$

and the **transfer function** (taking $X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x(t)\}$):

$$\frac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{ms^2 + bs + k}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Term | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|----------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Lagrangian | $L = K - P$ | Scalar function of $(q, \dot{q})$ |
| 2 | Euler-Lagrange Equation | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | Generates equations of motion |
| 3 | Generalized Force | $\tau_i = \dfrac{\partial W}{\partial q_i}$ where $W$ is virtual work | Non-conservative actuator effort |
| 4 | Manipulator Equation | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | Compact dynamic model |
| 5 | Mass Matrix Property | $M(q) = M^T(q) > 0$ | Symmetric, positive-definite |
| 6 | Coriolis Matrix | $\dot{M} - 2C$ is skew-symmetric | Passivity property |
| 7 | 1-DOF EOM | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ | Linear second-order ODE |
| 8 | Transfer Function | $\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{ms^2 + bs + k}$ | Laplace-domain input-output model |
| 9 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ rad/s | Undamped oscillation frequency |
| 10 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{mk}}$ | Dimensionless damping |
| 11 | State-Space Matrix A | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\omega_n^2 & -2\zeta\omega_n \end{bmatrix}$ | Linear system matrix |
| 12 | Dimensional Consistency | $[M] = \text{kg},\ [b] = \text{N·s/m},\ [k] = \text{N/m}$ | SI units check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The 1-DOF linear model is the **foundation of robot joint control design**. Every PID/State-Feedback controller in KTU lab work begins with these three parameters $(m, b, k)$. The Lagrangian formulation scales this foundation to multi-DOF arms in an elegant, computationally efficient manner.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Euler-Lagrange Equation (D'Alembert's Principle Form)

**Starting Point — D'Alembert's Principle (virtual work in dynamics):**
For a system in dynamic equilibrium, the sum of inertial forces and applied forces does zero virtual work over a virtual displacement $\delta q_i$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \tau_i - \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) + \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} \right]\delta q_i = 0$$

Since $\delta q_i$ are **independent virtual displacements**, each bracketed coefficient must vanish:

$$\tau_i = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} - \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$$

**Group the Potential Energy Term:**

Because $P = P(q)$ only (no explicit $\dot{q}$ dependence), we recognize:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i} \quad \text{(since } P \text{ is independent of } \dot{q}\text{)}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} - \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\tau_i = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}}$$

This is the **Euler-Lagrange equation of motion**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Standard Manipulator Equation

Let the manipulator have $n$ joints with generalized coordinates $q = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$. The total kinetic energy is a quadratic form in $\dot{q}$:

$$K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$$

where $M(q)$ is the $n \times n$ mass matrix. The potential energy depends only on configuration:

$$P = P(q)$$

Hence $L = K - P = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q} - P(q)$.

**Compute the partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_j$$

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k\dot{q}_j$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k - \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$$

**Substitute into the Euler-Lagrange equation:**

$$\tau_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\left[\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right]\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k + \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$$

**Identify the Coriolis terms** using the Christoffel symbols of the first kind:

$$c_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$$

The element $C_{ij}$ of the Coriolis matrix is:

$$C_{ij}(q, \dot{q}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$$

**Final compact vector-matrix form:**

$$\boxed{M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau}$$

where $G_i(q) = \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$ is the gravity vector.

## 3.3 Derivation of the 1-DOF Force-Acceleration Equation

**Free-Body Diagram of a 1-DOF Prismatic Joint:**
Consider a cart of mass $m$ on a horizontal rail, attached to a fixed wall by a spring of stiffness $k$, moving through a viscous medium with damping $b$, and pushed by an external force $F(t)$. Let $x$ denote the displacement of the cart from its equilibrium (unstretched-spring) position.

**Apply Newton's Second Law along the direction of motion:**

$$\sum F_{external} = m a = m\ddot{x}$$

**Identify the three forces acting on the cart:**

1. Applied external force: $+F(t)$ (assumed positive in $+x$ direction).
2. Spring restoring force: $-kx$ (opposes displacement).
3. Viscous damping force: $-b\dot{x}$ (opposes velocity).

**Sum the forces:**

$$F(t) - b\dot{x}(t) - kx(t) = m\ddot{x}(t)$$

**Rearrange into standard form** (all dependent variables on the left, input on the right):

$$\boxed{m\ddot{x}(t) + b\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Transfer Function

Assume **zero initial conditions**: $x(0) = 0$, $\dot{x}(0) = 0$. Apply the **Laplace transform** $\mathcal{L}\{\cdot\}$ to the equation of motion:

$$\mathcal{L}\{m\ddot{x}\} + \mathcal{L}\{b\dot{x}\} + \mathcal{L}\{kx\} = \mathcal{L}\{F\}$$

Using the standard Laplace identities:

$$ms^2 X(s) + bs X(s) + k X(s) = F(s)$$

**Factor out $X(s)$:**

$$X(s)\left[ms^2 + bs + k\right] = F(s)$$

**Solve for the transfer function:**

$$\boxed{\frac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{ms^2 + bs + k}}$$

This is a **strictly proper, second-order, stable** (provided $m, b, k > 0$) transfer function, and is the canonical starting point for all frequency-domain control design (Bode plot, root locus, PID tuning).

## 3.5 Derivation of the State-Space Representation

Choose the state vector as position and velocity:

$$\mathbf{z}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} z_1 \\ z_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ \dot{x}(t) \end{bmatrix}$$

By definition, $\dot{z}_1 = z_2$. The second equation comes from the 1-DOF EOM:

$$m\dot{z}_2 = F - bz_2 - kz_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{z}_2 = -\frac{k}{m}z_1 - \frac{b}{m}z_2 + \frac{1}{m}F$$

**Stack into matrix form:**

$$\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} \dot{z}_1 \\ \dot{z}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\frac{k}{m} & -\frac{b}{m} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} z_1 \\ z_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{m} \end{bmatrix} F(t)}$$

With output $y = x = z_1$:

$$y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} z_1 \\ z_2 \end{bmatrix} + 0 \cdot F(t)$$

The system matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -k/m & -b/m \end{bmatrix}$ has **eigenvalues** at the poles of the transfer function: the roots of $ms^2 + bs + k = 0$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Kinetic Energy K and Potential Energy P]
    D --> E[Step 4: Form Lagrangian L = K minus P]
    E --> F[Step 5: Apply Euler-Lagrange Equation for each q_i]
    F --> G[Step 6: Group Terms into M, C, G]
    G --> H[Final: M q-double-dot + C q-dot + G = tau]

    I --> J1[Trajectory Planning]
    I --> J2[Computed Torque Control]

```

    E --> F[Displacement x of t]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Style]`
**State the Euler-Lagrange equation for an n-DOF manipulator and identify each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The Euler-Lagrange equation for the $i^{th}$ generalized coordinate $q_i$ of an $n$-DOF manipulator is:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Identification of Terms:**
- $L = K - P$ is the **Lagrangian**, where $K$ is total kinetic energy and $P$ is total potential energy.
- $\dot{q}_i$ is the **generalized velocity** of the $i^{th}$ joint.
- $\tau_i$ is the **generalized force/torque** applied at the $i^{th}$ joint (non-conservative).
- $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$ is the **generalized momentum** conjugate to $q_i$.
- $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right)$ is its **time rate of change**.
- $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}$ captures the **positional sensitivity** of the energy difference.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Similar Style]`
**Write the equation of motion for a 1-DOF linear robotic system and identify the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The equation of motion of a 1-DOF linear robotic system is:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + b\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)$$

**Physical Meaning:**
- $m$ = **equivalent mass** (kg) of the moving link; the term $m\ddot{x}$ is the inertial force.
- $b$ = **viscous damping coefficient** (N·s/m); the term $b\dot{x}$ is the friction/drag force opposing motion.
- $k$ = **stiffness coefficient** (N/m); the term $kx$ is the elastic restoring force.
- $x$ = **displacement** (m), $\dot{x}$ = velocity (m/s), $\ddot{x}$ = acceleration (m/s²).
- $F(t)$ = **externally applied force** (N) — the input/control variable.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the Euler-Lagrange equation starting from D'Alembert's principle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 1-DOF prismatic robotic joint of mass $m = 2$ kg, damping $b = 4$ N·s/m, and stiffness $k = 8$ N/m, find the natural frequency, damping ratio, and the transfer function. Classify the damping. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a): Derivation of Euler-Lagrange Equation

**Step 1 — State D'Alembert's Principle:**

For a system of particles in dynamic equilibrium, the virtual work of the inertial forces plus the active forces vanishes for all virtual displacements consistent with the constraints:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\tau_i - \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) + \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}\right]\delta q_i = 0$$

**Step 2 — Use independence of $\delta q_i$:**

Since the virtual displacements $\delta q_i$ are **independent** (this is the definition of generalized coordinates), each bracket must individually equal zero:

$$\tau_i - \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) + \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the Lagrangian $L = K - P$:**

Observe that:
- $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \dfrac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$ (since $P$ is independent of $\dot{q}$).
- $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \dfrac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} - \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$.

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$\tau_i = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} - \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_i = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}}$$

This is the **Euler-Lagrange equation of the second kind** (or simply, the Lagrangian equation of motion). Each application yields one scalar second-order ODE per generalized coordinate. **(7 Marks Awarded)**

---

#### Solution to A(b): 1-DOF Dynamic Analysis

**Given:** $m = 2$ kg, $b = 4$ N·s/m, $k = 8$ N/m.

**Step 1 — Natural Frequency $\omega_n$:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{8}{2}} = \sqrt{4} = 2 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Damping Ratio $\zeta$:**

$$\zeta = \frac{b}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \frac{4}{2\sqrt{2 \cdot 8}} = \frac{4}{2\sqrt{16}} = \frac{4}{2 \cdot 4} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Transfer Function:**

$$\frac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{ms^2 + bs + k} = \frac{1}{2s^2 + 4s + 8}$$

**Step 4 — Classification of Damping:**

Since $0 < \zeta = 0.5 < 1$, the system is **underdamped** (decaying oscillatory response).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Using the Lagrangian formulation, derive the standard manipulator equation $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$. Clearly identify the mass matrix $M$, Coriolis matrix $C$, and gravity vector $G$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 1-DOF robotic joint has $m = 5$ kg, $b = 10$ N·s/m, $k = 50$ N/m. Find the state-space representation, the eigenvalues of the system matrix, and comment on stability. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a): Derivation of the Manipulator Equation

**Step 1 — Generalized Coordinates:**
For an $n$-DOF manipulator, let $q = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$ be the vector of joint variables.

**Step 2 — Kinetic Energy as a Quadratic Form:**

The total kinetic energy of the manipulator is a scalar quadratic function of $\dot{q}$:

$$K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$$

where $M(q)$ is the $n \times n$ symmetric positive-definite **mass/inertia matrix**, with entries $M_{ij}(q)$.

**Step 3 — Potential Energy:**

The potential energy $P(q)$ depends only on configuration (gravity + spring effects).

**Step 4 — Lagrangian:**

$$L = K - P = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q} - P(q)$$

**Step 5 — Partial Derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_j \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k\dot{q}_j$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k - \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into Euler-Lagrange and Group:**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n}M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\left[\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right]\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k + \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

**Step 7 — Vector-Matrix Form:**

Identify:
- $M(q)$: the $n \times n$ **mass matrix** (symmetric, positive definite).
- $C_{ij} = \sum_k c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$ with $c_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \dfrac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \dfrac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$ — the **Coriolis/centrifugal matrix**.
- $G_i(q) = \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$ — the **gravity vector**.

**Final Equation:**

$$\boxed{M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau}$$

**(7 Marks Awarded)**

---

#### Solution to B(b): State-Space Analysis

**Given:** $m = 5$ kg, $b = 10$ N·s/m, $k = 50$ N/m.

**Step 1 — Equation of Motion:**

$$5\ddot{x} + 10\dot{x} + 50x = F(t)$$

**Step 2 — Define State Vector $z = [x, \dot{x}]^T$:**

$$\dot{z}_1 = z_2, \qquad \dot{z}_2 = \ddot{x} = \frac{1}{5}\left[F - 10z_2 - 50z_1\right] = -10z_1 - 2z_2 + 0.2F$$

**Step 3 — System Matrix $A$:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -10 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad C_{out} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvalues of $A$:**

Solve $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$:

$$\det\!\begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 10 & \lambda + 2 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda + 2) + 10 = \lambda^2 + 2\lambda + 10 = 0$$

Apply the quadratic formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 40}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{-36}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm 6j}{2} = -1 \pm 3j$$

**Step 5 — Stability Comment:**

Both eigenvalues have **negative real part** ($\text{Re}(\lambda) = -1 < 0$). The system is **asymptotically stable**. The imaginary part $\pm 3j$ confirms the response is **underdamped** with damped natural frequency $\omega_d = 3$ rad/s.

**Verification via characteristic equation of EOM:** $5s^2 + 10s + 50 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 + 2s + 10 = 0 \Rightarrow s = -1 \pm 3j$. ✔ Matches.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (3 marks lost):** Students often write the Euler-Lagrange equation as $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}\right) = \tau_i$ — **the order is wrong**! The derivative wrt time must wrap the $\dot{q}$ term, not the $q$ term. Memorize it correctly: **$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$**.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (2 marks lost):** In the 1-DOF problem, students forget that the **applied force $F$ goes on the right-hand side** of the standard form. Writing $F = m\ddot{x} - b\dot{x} - kx$ instead of $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F$ loses the 2 marks allocated for "writing the EOM in standard form".
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (2 marks lost):** The Coriolis matrix $C$ is **not** simply $\dot{M}$. Students who write $C = \dot{M}$ will lose 2 marks. The correct relationship involves Christoffel symbols and the skew-symmetric property $\dot{M} - 2C$ is skew-symmetric.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (1 mark lost):** Stability conclusion must mention **negative real part of eigenvalues** — "stable" without justification gets only partial credit.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Lagrangian** is $L = K - P$, a **scalar** function of generalized coordinates and their time derivatives.
- ✅ The **Euler-Lagrange equation** is $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ — memorize the order!
- ✅ The **manipulator equation** is $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ — it must be **derived**, not assumed.
- ✅ The **mass matrix** $M(q)$ is always **symmetric and positive definite** — this property is used in control design.
- ✅ The **Coriolis matrix** $C(q,\dot{q})$ arises from the $\dot{q}$-dependent (velocity) terms — includes both Coriolis and centrifugal effects.
- ✅ The **gravity vector** $G(q) = \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q}$ depends only on configuration $q$.
- ✅ The **1-DOF EOM** is $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ — the universal linear robotic joint model.
- ✅ The **transfer function** is $\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{ms^2 + bs + k}$ — a second-order, strictly proper system.
- ✅ **Natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and **damping ratio** $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{mk}}$.
- ✅ **State-space** uses $z = [x, \dot{x}]^T$ with $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -k/m & -b/m \end{bmatrix}$.
- ✅ **Stability** requires $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for all eigenvalues of $A$ — guaranteed when $m, b, k > 0$.
- ✅ **Lagrangian vs Newton-Euler**: Lagrangian = closed-form, $O(n^4)$; Newton-Euler = recursive, $O(n)$ — choose by application.
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always state the *units* of $m, b, k$ when writing the EOM — KTU examiners check for dimensional awareness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation of Motion for 2-DOF Robot Arm. Motion Control: Path vs. Trajectory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY AND ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equation of Motion for 2-DOF Robot Arm. Motion Control: Path vs. Trajectory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Equation of Motion (EOM)** of a 2-Degree-of-Freedom (2-DOF) robotic manipulator is a set of coupled, second-order, nonlinear ordinary differential equations that govern the dynamic behavior of the manipulator's links as functions of the joint variables, their first time derivatives (velocities), and second time derivatives (accelerations), subject to externally applied joint torques and the inertial, Coriolis, centrifugal, and gravitational effects of the links.

The generalized dynamic model, in joint space, is universally expressed in the canonical form:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

where $q \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ is the vector of generalized joint coordinates for a 2-DOF arm, $\dot{q}$ and $\ddot{q}$ are the joint velocity and acceleration vectors, $M(q)$ is the symmetric positive-definite **Mass (Inertia) Matrix**, $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ is the **Coriolis and Centrifugal vector**, $G(q)$ is the **Gravitational torque vector**, and $\tau$ is the **vector of generalized joint actuator torques**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The equation-of-motion derivation for a 2-DOF arm is the central evaluative topic in Module 4. Students must be able to derive $M(q)$, $C(q,\dot{q})$, and $G(q)$ explicitly using the **Lagrange–Euler (L–E)** formulation. The Newton–Euler iterative form is supplementary.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Arm" Intuition

Imagine you are holding a fishing rod in one hand and swinging it through the air. To make the tip move fast, you don't just push your shoulder — your elbow joint must also fire in a coordinated way, *because* the rod (Link 2) and your forearm (Link 1) are linked, and the motion of one influences the other. The torques your shoulder and elbow muscles produce at any instant depend on:

1. **How heavy the rod is and where its mass sits** → captured by $M(q)$.
2. **How fast the joints are spinning and whether the rotation is bending the linkage sideways** → captured by $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$.
3. **The downward pull of gravity on the rod and your forearm** → captured by $G(q)$.

A 2-DOF robot arm behaves **identically**. The equation of motion is just the mathematical "muscle coordination recipe" that tells each joint actuator how hard to push at every microsecond to follow a desired motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Recap:** The EOM of a 2-DOF arm is a coupled nonlinear system of two differential equations that map the actuator torques at the shoulder and elbow to the resulting joint accelerations, given the current joint angles, velocities, and gravity. The 2 × 2 mass matrix $M(q)$ is always symmetric and positive definite — a property examiners love to test.

---

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Robotics Engineering

The equation of motion is the *backbone* of every advanced robot controller. Without an explicit dynamic model, you cannot:

- Implement **Computed Torque Control** (model-based inverse dynamics control).
- Design **adaptive or robust controllers** (e.g., Slotine–Li, adaptive inertia estimation).
- Perform **dynamic simulation** in digital twins (Gazebo, MATLAB/Simulink, Adams).
- Predict **joint torques for actuator sizing** in mechanical design.
- Conduct **energy-efficient trajectory optimization** for industrial manipulators (KUKA, ABB, FANUC arms).

In KTU 2024, the topic is also the launchpad for understanding **Trajectory Planning**, which converts a desired spatial **Path** into a time-parameterized **Trajectory** with bounded velocities and accelerations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Lagrange–Euler Formulation — The Standard Derivation Pipeline

For a 2-DOF planar revolute–revolute (RR) arm with link masses $m_1, m_2$, link lengths $l_1, l_2$, and center-of-mass offsets $l_{c1}, l_{c2}$ from each joint, the joint coordinates are $q_1$ (shoulder angle) and $q_2$ (elbow angle). The systematic derivation pipeline is:

**Step 1 — Define generalized coordinates.**
The configuration of the arm is completely described by the joint vector $q = [q_1,\; q_2]^T$.

**Step 2 — Compute the position of each link's center of mass.**
Using forward kinematics, the $(x, y)$ position of the center of mass of link 1 is:
$$x_{c1} = l_{c1}\cos q_1, \qquad y_{c1} = l_{c1}\sin q_1$$
And for link 2:
$$x_{c2} = l_1\cos q_1 + l_{c2}\cos(q_1+q_2)$$
$$y_{c2} = l_1\sin q_1 + l_{c2}\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the kinetic energy of each link.**
The kinetic energy of a rigid link is $T_i = \tfrac{1}{2}m_i v_{ci}^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}I_{ci}\dot{q}_i^2$ (for planar motion with respect to the link's own frame, the angular velocity of link 1 is $\dot{q}_1$ and of link 2 is $\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2$).

**Step 4 — Compute the total potential energy.**
$P = m_1 g\, y_{c1} + m_2 g\, y_{c2}$.

**Step 5 — Form the Lagrangian.**
$L = T - P$.

**Step 6 — Apply the Euler–Lagrange equation** for each generalized coordinate:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i \quad \text{for } i = 1,2$$

**Step 7 — Collect terms** into the standard matrix form $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbolic Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|---------------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | General EOM | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | vector | Coupled nonlinear dynamic model |
| 2 | Mass matrix element $M_{11}$ | $(m_1+m_2)l_{c1}^2 + m_2 l_1^2 + 2m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2 + I_1 + I_2$ | kg·m² | Shoulder inertia seen at joint 1 |
| 3 | Mass matrix element $M_{12}=M_{21}$ | $m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2$ | kg·m² | Coupling inertia between joints |
| 4 | Mass matrix element $M_{22}$ | $m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2$ | kg·m² | Elbow inertia seen at joint 2 |
| 5 | Coriolis term $C_{12}$ entry | $-m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_2$ | N·m·s | Coupling Coriolis coefficient |
| 6 | Coriolis term $C_{21}$ entry | $m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_1$ | N·m·s | Reverse coupling Coriolis coefficient |
| 7 | Gravity term $G_1$ | $(m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g \cos q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2)$ | N·m | Shoulder gravity torque |
| 8 | Gravity term $G_2$ | $m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2)$ | N·m | Elbow gravity torque |
| 9 | Path | Geometric locus in Cartesian/joint space, **time-independent** | — | Pure shape, no timing |
| 10 | Trajectory | Time-parameterized path $q(t)$ or $x(t)$ | — | Path + velocity profile + timing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall — Matrix Symmetry:** The mass matrix $M(q)$ is **always symmetric and positive definite** for any revolute-joint manipulator. A common student error is to write the off-diagonal terms as different functions — they are mathematically equal. The Coriolis matrix $C(q,\dot{q})$ is **not** symmetric and is **not** unique (Christoffel symbol ambiguity).

---

### 2.3 Path vs. Trajectory — The Core Distinction

| Aspect | **Path** | **Trajectory** |
|--------|----------|----------------|
| Definition | A geometric curve connecting start and goal | A path **plus** a time law $q(t)$ or $x(t)$ |
| Time parameter | None | Yes — $t$ is explicit |
| Velocity/Acceleration | Not specified | Fully specified (bounded) |
| Examples | Straight line, circular arc, spline curve | Linear-parabolic, cubic polynomial, quintic polynomial, trapezoidal velocity profile |
| Planning space | Joint space or Cartesian space | Joint space (typical) or Cartesian space (less common) |
| Real-world analogy | A road on a map | A road plus the *speed schedule* a car follows along it |

**Motion Control** therefore has two sub-problems:

1. **Geometric Path Planning** — choose the *shape* of the motion in the appropriate space.
2. **Trajectory Generation** — choose the *time law* along the path so that position, velocity, and acceleration are continuous and bounded.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why distinguish?** An industrial robot following the same geometric path at two different speeds has two distinct trajectories. Path is a *geometric* problem; trajectory is a *dynamic* problem that must respect actuator torque limits, jerk constraints, and cycle-time goals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Lagrange–Euler Derivation for a 2-DOF Planar RR Arm

**Assumptions:** Planar motion in the $xy$-plane, gravity acts along $-y$, all masses are point masses located at the center-of-mass offsets $l_{c1}, l_{c2}$ (or point-mass approximation $I_1 = I_2 = 0$ for simplicity, then add inertias at the end). Both joints are revolute.

#### Step 1 — Forward Kinematics of Center-of-Mass Positions

The position of the center of mass of link 1:
$$x_{c1} = l_{c1}\cos q_1, \qquad y_{c1} = l_{c1}\sin q_1$$

The position of the center of mass of link 2 (end of link 1 is at $(l_1\cos q_1, l_1\sin q_1)$):
$$x_{c2} = l_1\cos q_1 + l_{c2}\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$
$$y_{c2} = l_1\sin q_1 + l_{c2}\sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

#### Step 2 — Velocities of the Centers of Mass

Differentiating with respect to time:
$$\dot{x}_{c1} = -l_{c1}\sin q_1 \,\dot{q}_1, \qquad \dot{y}_{c1} = l_{c1}\cos q_1 \,\dot{q}_1$$

$$\dot{x}_{c2} = -l_1\sin q_1 \,\dot{q}_1 - l_{c2}\sin(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$
$$\dot{y}_{c2} = l_1\cos q_1 \,\dot{q}_1 + l_{c2}\cos(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$

The squared velocity of link 1's CoM:
$$v_{c1}^2 = \dot{x}_{c1}^2 + \dot{y}_{c1}^2 = l_{c1}^2\,\dot{q}_1^2$$

The squared velocity of link 2's CoM:
$$v_{c2}^2 = \dot{x}_{c2}^2 + \dot{y}_{c2}^2$$

Expanding $v_{c2}^2$ (this is the algebra-intensive step most students fumble in exams):
$$v_{c2}^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_{c2}^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2} \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2$$

#### Step 3 — Kinetic Energy of Each Link

For link 1 (including rotational KE about its own CoM):
$$T_1 = \frac{1}{2} m_1 v_{c1}^2 + \frac{1}{2} I_1 \dot{q}_1^2 = \frac{1}{2}(m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1)\dot{q}_1^2$$

For link 2 (rotational KE uses $\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2$):
$$T_2 = \frac{1}{2} m_2 v_{c2}^2 + \frac{1}{2} I_2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2$$

Substituting and grouping:
$$T_2 = \frac{1}{2} m_2 l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}(m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2$$

Total kinetic energy $T = T_1 + T_2$.

#### Step 4 — Potential Energy

$$P = m_1 g\, y_{c1} + m_2 g\, y_{c2} = (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1)g\sin q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g \sin(q_1+q_2)$$

#### Step 5 — Lagrangian

$$L = T - P$$

#### Step 6 — Euler–Lagrange for Joint 1

We need $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1}$, $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right)$, and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_1}$.

From $T$, every $\dot{q}_1$ term contributes. After algebraic collection (the most tedious step):
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1} = \big[(m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1) + m_2 l_1^2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2\big]\dot{q}_1 + \big[m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2\big]\dot{q}_2$$

Differentiating with respect to $t$:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) = M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + \dot{M}_{12}\dot{q}_2$$

where the mass matrix entries are:
$$M_{11} = (m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1) + m_2 l_1^2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2$$
$$M_{12} = m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2$$

The derivative $\dot{M}_{12} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_2$.

Next, the potential energy derivative:
$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial q_1} = (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1)g\cos q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2)$$

The kinetic energy derivative with respect to $q_1$ contributes the centrifugal terms:
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial q_1} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_2\dot{q}_1 - m_2 l_{c2}^2\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - \cdots$$

Carefully gathering all centrifugal/Coriolis contributions from joint 1:
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial q_1} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,(2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \dot{q}_2^2)$$

Therefore, the Euler–Lagrange equation for joint 1:
$$\tau_1 = M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + \big[-m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_2\big]\dot{q}_2 - \big[-m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,(2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \dot{q}_2^2)\big] + G_1$$

After full simplification (the $\dot{q}_2$ coefficient above is the Christoffel $c_{122}$ form), the compact form is:
$$\tau_1 = M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,(2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \dot{q}_2^2) + G_1$$

#### Step 7 — Euler–Lagrange for Joint 2

Carrying out the analogous procedure (omitting repetitive algebra for brevity, but the result is canonical):
$$\tau_2 = M_{21}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1^2 + G_2$$

with:
$$M_{21} = m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2 = M_{12}$$
$$M_{22} = m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2$$
$$G_2 = m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

#### Step 8 — Final Matrix Form

$$\begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{12} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_2 & -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) \\ m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} G_1 \\ G_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the **complete, KTU-ready 2-DOF equation of motion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuator's Observation:** The Coriolis/centrifugal matrix $C(q,\dot{q})$ shown here uses the **specific form from the Christoffel symbols of the first kind**. Other valid forms (using the Levi-Civita skew-symmetric representation) exist, but this is the form expected in KTU 2024 solutions.

---

### 3.2 Trajectory Generation — Joint-Space Cubic Polynomial

Suppose the joint must move from initial angle $q_i$ at $t=0$ to final angle $q_f$ at $t=t_f$, with initial and final velocities both zero. The cubic polynomial ansatz is:
$$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

The four boundary conditions are:
$$q(0) = q_i, \quad q(t_f) = q_f, \quad \dot{q}(0) = 0, \quad \dot{q}(t_f) = 0$$

Solving the linear system:
$$a_0 = q_i, \qquad a_1 = 0$$
$$a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_i)}{t_f^2}, \qquad a_3 = \frac{-2(q_f - q_i)}{t_f^3}$$

Therefore:
$$q(t) = q_i + (q_f - q_i)\left[3\left(\frac{t}{t_f}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{t}{t_f}\right)^3\right]$$

Velocity profile:
$$\dot{q}(t) = \frac{6(q_f - q_i)}{t_f^2}\left[\frac{t}{t_f} - \left(\frac{t}{t_f}\right)^2\right]$$

Acceleration profile:
$$\ddot{q}(t) = \frac{6(q_f - q_i)}{t_f^2}\left[\frac{1}{t_f} - \frac{2t}{t_f^2}\right]$$

The maximum velocity occurs at $t = t_f/2$ and equals $\dot{q}_{\max} = \dfrac{3(q_f - q_i)}{2 t_f}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quintic Polynomial Upgrade:** The cubic polynomial gives a *discontinuous* acceleration at the trajectory endpoints (jumps from $0$ to nonzero at $t=0$ and $t=t_f$). For smoother motion, the quintic polynomial $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ is used, which constrains both endpoints to have zero velocity **and** zero acceleration, and is the **gold standard** in KTU answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 2-DOF RR Manipulator Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base / Ground Frame] -->|Revolute Joint q1| B[Link 1 with mass m1, length l1]
    B -->|Revolute Joint q2| C[Link 2 with mass m2, length l2]
    C --> D[End Effector / Tool Point]
    E[Joint Torque tau1] -->|Actuation| A
    F[Joint Torque tau2] -->|Actuation| B
    G[Gravity Vector g acting downward] --> A
    G --> B
    H[Controller Input qd, qd_dot, qd_ddot] --> I[Inverse Dynamics Solver]
    I -->|Computes| E
    I -->|Computes| F
    J[Sensor Feedback: q, q_dot] --> I
```

### 4.2 Equation-of-Motion Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input: q1, q2, q1_dot, q2_dot, q1_ddot, q2_ddot] --> S1[Compute Mass Matrix M q]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Coriolis Matrix C q, q_dot]
    P1 --> S3[Compute Gravity Vector G q]
    S1 --> A1[M q times q_ddot]
    S2 --> A2[C q, q_dot times q_dot]
    S3 --> A3[G q]
    A1 --> SUM[Vector Sum]
    A2 --> SUM
    A3 --> SUM
    SUM --> R[Output: Joint Torque Vector tau1, tau2]
```

### 4.3 Path vs. Trajectory Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START[Start Pose] --> PATH{Path Type}
    PATH --> P1[Joint Space Linear]
    PATH --> P2[Cartesian Straight Line]
    PATH --> P3[Cartesian Circular Arc]
    PATH --> P4[Spline Curve]
    P1 --> TRAJ{Trajectory Type}
    P2 --> TRAJ
    P3 --> TRAJ
    P4 --> TRAJ
    TRAJ --> T1[Cubic Polynomial in q t]
    TRAJ --> T2[Quintic Polynomial in q t]
    TRAJ --> T3[Trapezoidal Velocity Profile]
    TRAJ --> T4[S-curve with Jerk Limit]
    T1 --> EXEC[Motor Servo Execution]
    T2 --> EXEC
    T3 --> EXEC
    T4 --> EXEC
```

### 4.4 Computed Torque Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QD[Desired Trajectory qd, qd_dot, qd_ddot] --> EOM[Dynamic Model M q, C q q_dot, G q]
    EOM --> TAU[Computed Torque tau]
    TAU --> ARM[2-DOF Robot Arm]
    ARM --> Q[Measured Joint State q, q_dot]
    Q --> FB[Feedback Path]
    FB --> ERR[Subtract from Desired State]
    QD --> ERR
    ERR --> GAIN[Linear Gain Kp, Kv]
    GAIN --> TAU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `P1`, `TAU`, `ARM`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting to ensure successful rendering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
*State the general form of the dynamic equation of motion for a 2-DOF robot manipulator in joint space. Identify each term with units.*

**Model Answer:**
The general form is:
$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$
where:
- $M(q)$: $2 \times 2$ symmetric positive-definite **mass (inertia) matrix**, units **kg·m²**.
- $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$: **Coriolis and centrifugal torque vector**, units **N·m**.
- $G(q)$: **Gravitational torque vector**, units **N·m**.
- $\tau$: **Generalized joint actuator torque vector**, units **N·m**.
- $q, \dot{q}, \ddot{q}$: joint position (rad), velocity (rad/s), and acceleration (rad/s²) vectors.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**
*Differentiate between a **Path** and a **Trajectory** in robot motion control with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**
- A **Path** is a geometric locus of points connecting a start configuration to a goal configuration, defined **without** any time parameter. *Example:* a straight line from $(0.3, 0.2)$ to $(0.5, 0.4)$ in Cartesian space.
- A **Trajectory** is a **time-parameterized** path, i.e., the path together with a velocity/acceleration schedule. *Example:* following the same straight line with a trapezoidal velocity profile, completing the motion in exactly 1.5 seconds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mark Loss Zone 1:** Writing $M(q)$ as non-symmetric. Always verify $M_{12} = M_{21}$. Loss: 1 mark.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 2:** Mixing up joint-space and Cartesian-space notation. Path-planning space and trajectory-generation space are **independent choices**. Loss: 1 mark.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 3:** Forgetting that the Coriolis matrix is **not unique** and writing the wrong sign convention. Loss: 0.5 mark.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 4:** Confusing the gravitational vector with the potential energy derivative. $G_i = \partial P / \partial q_i$, not $\partial P / \partial \dot{q}_i$. Loss: 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 4]**
*Consider a 2-DOF planar RR manipulator with link masses $m_1, m_2$, lengths $l_1, l_2$, and center-of-mass offsets $l_{c1}, l_{c2}$ from the respective joints. The link inertias about their own CoMs are $I_1, I_2$. The manipulator moves in a vertical plane under gravity $g$.*

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion in the standard form $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ using the **Lagrange–Euler** formulation. Clearly state all intermediate steps including the kinetic and potential energy expressions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the case $q_1 = \pi/2$, $q_2 = 0$, $\dot{q}_1 = 1$ rad/s, $\dot{q}_2 = 0.5$ rad/s, $\ddot{q}_1 = \ddot{q}_2 = 0$, compute the numerical value of the **Coriolis and centrifugal torque vector** $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ in terms of $m_2, l_1, l_{c2}$. Take $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$ and $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Step 1 — Forward kinematics of CoMs:
$$x_{c1} = l_{c1}\cos q_1, \quad y_{c1} = l_{c1}\sin q_1$$
$$x_{c2} = l_1\cos q_1 + l_{c2}\cos(q_1+q_2), \quad y_{c2} = l_1\sin q_1 + l_{c2}\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

Step 2 — Squared velocities:
$$v_{c1}^2 = l_{c1}^2 \dot{q}_1^2$$
$$v_{c2}^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_{c2}^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2} \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2$$

Step 3 — Kinetic energy:
$$T_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1)\dot{q}_1^2$$
$$T_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 v_{c2}^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} I_2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2$$
$$T = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[(m_1 l_{c1}^2+I_1) + m_2 l_1^2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2\big]\dot{q}_1^2 + \big[m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2\big]\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(m_2 l_{c2}^2+I_2)\dot{q}_2^2$$

Step 4 — Potential energy:
$$P = (m_1 l_{c1}+m_2 l_1)g\sin q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

Step 5 — Apply Euler–Lagrange for $i = 1, 2$ and collect terms into matrix form. Final result:

$$\begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{12} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -h\,\dot{q}_2 & -h(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) \\ h\,\dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} G_1 \\ G_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

where the shorthand $h = m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2$ is introduced, and:
$$M_{11} = m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2) + I_1 + I_2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2$$
$$M_{12} = m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2$$
$$M_{22} = m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2$$
$$G_1 = (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1)g\cos q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2)$$
$$G_2 = m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2)$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

Given $q_1 = \pi/2$, $q_2 = 0$: $\sin q_2 = 0$, hence $h = m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(0) = 0$.

The Coriolis/centrifugal torque vector is:
$$C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} -h\,\dot{q}_2^2 - h\,\dot{q}_2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) \\ h\,\dot{q}_1^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Physical interpretation:** At the given configuration, the elbow is fully extended along the shoulder axis, so no Coriolis or centrifugal coupling torque is generated regardless of the joint velocities. **Result: $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} = [0,\; 0]^T$ N·m.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain clearly the distinction between **joint-space** and **Cartesian-space** trajectory planning. State **two advantages and one disadvantage** of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single joint must move from $q_i = 10°$ to $q_f = 50°$ in $t_f = 4$ s. Using a **cubic polynomial** trajectory with zero initial and final velocities, derive expressions for $q(t)$, $\dot{q}(t)$, and $\ddot{q}(t)$. Compute the **maximum velocity** and the **time at which it occurs**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

| Aspect | Joint-Space Planning | Cartesian-Space Planning |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Path | Straight line in $q_1$–$q_2$ space | Straight line / curve in $x$–$y$–$z$ end-effector space |
| Computation | Simple: direct polynomial on $q(t)$ | Complex: requires inverse kinematics at every sample |
| Singularity handling | No singularity issues inside joint range | Suffers from kinematic singularities and joint-limit reachability |
| Path accuracy | Lower (end-effector may deviate) | High (end-effector follows geometric path exactly) |

**Joint-space advantages:** (i) computationally cheap, (ii) no inverse-kinematics required, (iii) naturally avoids internal singularities along the trajectory.
**Joint-space disadvantage:** the end-effector path in Cartesian space is unpredictable and may collide with obstacles.

**Cartesian-space advantages:** (i) intuitive for obstacle avoidance, (ii) accurate end-effector path.
**Cartesian-space disadvantages:** requires frequent inverse kinematics; may pass through kinematic singularities or violate joint limits.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

Cubic polynomial: $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$.

Boundary conditions:
$$q(0) = 10° \Rightarrow a_0 = 10°$$
$$\dot{q}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 = 0$$
$$q(4) = 50° \Rightarrow a_0 + 16 a_2 + 64 a_3 = 50°$$
$$\dot{q}(4) = 0 \Rightarrow 8 a_2 + 48 a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = -6 a_3$$

Substituting $a_2 = -6 a_3$ into the third equation:
$$10 + 16(-6 a_3) + 64 a_3 = 50 \Rightarrow 10 - 96 a_3 + 64 a_3 = 50 \Rightarrow -32 a_3 = 40 \Rightarrow a_3 = -1.25°/\text{s}^3$$

Then $a_2 = -6(-1.25) = 7.5°/\text{s}^2$.

Final trajectory:
$$q(t) = 10 + 7.5 t^2 - 1.25 t^3 \quad [°]$$
$$\dot{q}(t) = 15 t - 3.75 t^2 \quad [°/\text{s}]$$
$$\ddot{q}(t) = 15 - 7.5 t \quad [°/\text{s}^2]$$

Maximum velocity: $\dot{q}'(t) = \ddot{q}(t) = 0 \Rightarrow t^* = 2$ s. Substituting:
$$\dot{q}_{\max} = 15(2) - 3.75(4) = 30 - 15 = 15°/\text{s}$$

**Result:** Maximum velocity is $\mathbf{15°/s}$ occurring at $t = 2$ s (mid-trajectory).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **EOM Canonical Form:** $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ — memorize it verbatim.
- **Symmetry of $M(q)$:** $M_{12} = M_{21}$ for all revolute-joint manipulators. Always verify.
- **Positive-Definiteness of $M(q)$:** $M(q) > 0$ for all $q$ in the reachable workspace. This is what makes $M^{-1}(q)$ exist (required for inverse dynamics).
- **Coriolis Matrix Non-Uniqueness:** $C(q,\dot{q})$ depends on the choice of Christoffel symbol formulation; the most common is $c_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial M_{ij}/\partial q_k + \partial M_{ik}/\partial q_j - \partial M_{jk}/\partial q_i)$.
- **Skew-Symmetry Property:** $\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})$ is skew-symmetric, i.e., $x^T(\dot{M} - 2C)x = 0$ for any vector $x$. This is crucial for **passivity-based control** proofs.
- **Gravity Vector Sign:** $G(q) = \partial P / \partial q$ (gradient of potential energy), with $P$ measured with reference to a zero-potential datum.
- **Path = Geometry; Trajectory = Geometry + Time Law.** Trajectory = Path + Velocity/Acceleration profile.
- **Joint-Space Trajectory Planning** is the KTU-default unless explicitly stated otherwise.
- **Cubic Polynomial** ensures $C^0$ (position) and $C^1$ (velocity) continuity.
- **Quintic Polynomial** ensures $C^2$ (acceleration) continuity and is the smoothest KTU-standard choice.
- **Trapezoidal Velocity Profile** has bounded acceleration but unbounded jerk at corners → replaced by S-curve profile in modern industrial robots.
- **Computed Torque Control Law:** $\tau = M(q)\big(\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e\big) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ where $e = q_d - q$. This linearizes the closed-loop dynamics.
- **Units to memorize:** $[M] = \text{kg·m}^2$, $[C] = \text{kg·m}^2/\text{s}$, $[G] = [\tau] = \text{N·m}$, $[q] = \text{rad}$, $[\dot q] = \text{rad/s}$, $[\ddot q] = \text{rad/s}^2$.
- **Common exam trap:** Forgetting to convert degrees to radians (and vice versa) when plugging into the EOM. The EOM is in **radians**; trajectories may be in degrees. Always convert before substituting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joint Space vs. Cartesian Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY AND ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Joint Space vs. Cartesian Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of serial-chain robotic manipulators, the **configuration** of a robot with *n* degrees of freedom (DOF) can be described using two fundamentally different — yet mathematically equivalent — coordinate systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Joint Space (Configuration Space, C-space):**
> The *n*-dimensional vector space spanned by the independent joint variables $q_i$ of the manipulator. Each point in this space uniquely represents one configuration of the robot, expressed as
> $$\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} q_1 & q_2 & \cdots & q_n \end{bmatrix}^T$$
> where $q_i$ denotes either a revolute angle (rad) for a rotary joint or a prismatic displacement (m) for a sliding joint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cartesian Space (Task Space / Operational Space):**
> The 3-D Euclidean space in which the position and orientation of the robot's end-effector are described, typically with respect to the **base frame**. For a generic 6-DOF spatial manipulator the pose vector is
> $$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} p_x & p_y & p_z & \phi & \theta & \psi \end{bmatrix}^T$$
> where $(p_x, p_y, p_z)$ is the position of the tool-tip and $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ is an orientation parameterisation (Euler angles ZYZ / RPY).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car-Driver" Intuition

Imagine you are a passenger giving directions to a driver:

- **Joint Space description** is like describing the motion through the **steering wheel angle, accelerator pedal position, and gear ratio** at every instant. It is the driver's *internal* control language — natural for the machine, but the *path traced on the road* is hidden.
- **Cartesian Space description** is like tracking the car on a **GPS map** (X, Y coordinates and heading). It is the *external* world view — intuitive for a human planning the trip, but the actual wheel/pedal commands required to follow that path must be computed (the *inverse problem*).

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exams:**
> Joint variables are what the *motors* see; Cartesian variables are what the *task* (welding, painting, pick-and-place) demands.

## 1.3 Key Distinction at a Glance

| Aspect | Joint Space | Cartesian Space |
|---|---|---|
| Dimension | $n$ (equal to DOF) | Up to **6** (3 position + 3 orientation) |
| Variables | Angles / displacements | Coordinates + orientation |
| Mapping | $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}, m \le 6$ |
| Solvability | Always (one unique value per joint) | Multiple / no solutions in general |
| Singularity | Singularities hidden | Singularities visible (loss of DOF) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Forward and Inverse Mapping Logic

The two descriptions are related by the **kinematic map** $\mathbf{f}$:

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{q}) \quad \text{(Forward Kinematics)}$$

$$\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{f}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{(Inverse Kinematics)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme:** The 2024 syllabus (Module 4) explicitly tests the **one-to-many** nature of the inverse map. For a 6-DOF PUMA-type arm, a single Cartesian pose can correspond to **up to 8 distinct** valid joint configurations (the famous "elbow-up" vs. "elbow-down" solutions).

## 2.2 Why Two Descriptions? — The Engineering "Why"

| Engineering Need | Preferred Description |
|---|---|
| Servo-motor command generation | **Joint Space** (motors speak angles) |
| Trajectory planning along a straight welding seam | **Cartesian Space** (linear path is trivial) |
| Joint-limit / obstacle avoidance | **Joint Space** (limits are simple bounds on $q_i$) |
| Singular posture detection | **Cartesian Space** (Jacobian becomes rank-deficient) |
| Off-line programming by human | **Cartesian Space** (intuitive XYZ commands) |

## 2.3 Bullet-Style Logic Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Measure the robot:** The manufacturer knows each joint variable $q_i$ from optical encoders or resolvers. This *naturally* yields a Joint Space description.
- **Step 2 — Task specification:** The user, however, wants the end-effector to touch a point in 3-D space. This is fundamentally a **Cartesian specification**.
- **Step 3 — Bridging via FK:** Forward kinematics takes $\mathbf{q}$ and returns the unique Cartesian pose $\mathbf{x}$ using Denavit–Hartenberg (D-H) parameters.
- **Step 4 — Bridging via IK:** Inverse kinematics solves $\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{f}^{-1}(\mathbf{x})$ — this is *non-linear*, *coupled*, and *multi-valued*.
- **Step 5 — Differential bridge (Jacobian):** For velocities, the linearised bridge is
  $$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$
  where $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the **geometric Jacobian**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{q})$ | Forward kinematics (FK) |
| 2 | $\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{f}^{-1}(\mathbf{x})$ | Inverse kinematics (IK) |
| 3 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Differential FK (velocity) |
| 4 | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$ | Static force/torque mapping |
| 5 | $\mathbf{J} = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$ | Jacobian definition |
| 6 | $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0$ | Singularity condition (loss of mobility) |
| 7 | $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})}$ | Yoshikawa's *manipulability* index |
| 8 | $\dot{q}_{i,\max} = \dfrac{\dot{x}_{j,\max}}{\vert J_{ij} \vert}$ | Max joint velocity from Cartesian bound |
| 9 | $x = L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)$ | 2R planar arm: end X |
| 10 | $y = L_1\sin q_1 + L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)$ | 2R planar arm: end Y |

> [!NOTE]
