> All joint-velocity limits derived from Cartesian specifications **must use** the absolute value $\vert J_{ij} \vert$ (not raw $J_{ij}$), because the sign only indicates direction. The KTU 2024 valuation key specifically awards the 1-mark step of taking the modulus.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial welding:** Cartesian linear interpolation (LIN move) — Cartesian space.
- **Palletising:** Joint-interpolated (PTP) moves between bin positions — Joint space.
- **Surgical robotics (Da Vinci):** Master–slave mapping mixes both — surgeon commands in Cartesian, slave motors execute in Joint space.
- **Autonomous mobile manipulators (mobile base + arm):** Base is commanded in Cartesian (SE(2)) while the arm is commanded in Joint space.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — 2R Planar Arm: Joint → Cartesian

Consider a two-link planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$, $L_2$ in the $XY$-plane, base at origin $O_0$. Let joint angles be $q_1$ (shoulder) and $q_2$ (elbow), both measured from the preceding link.

**Step 1 — Locate the elbow (joint 2) using the law of cosines in vector form.**
The position of joint 2 measured from $O_0$ is the vector sum of the first link:

$$\mathbf{p}_2 = L_1\begin{bmatrix}\cos q_1 \\ \sin q_1\end{bmatrix}$$

**Reasoning:** Link 1 makes angle $q_1$ with the global $X$-axis. Its tip therefore lies at horizontal distance $L_1 \cos q_1$ and vertical distance $L_1 \sin q_1$.

**Step 2 — Express the orientation of link 2 in the global frame.**
Link 2 makes an angle $(q_1 + q_2)$ with the global $X$-axis because it is rotated by $q_2$ relative to link 1.

**Step 3 — Write the end-effector position vector.**

The end-effector is at the tip of link 2. Its position is the sum of the elbow position and the link-2 vector:

$$\mathbf{p}_e = \mathbf{p}_2 + L_2\begin{bmatrix}\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ \sin(q_1+q_2)\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Expand into scalar components (this is what the KTU paper expects).**

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\
y &= L_1 \sin q_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **explicit forward map** $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{q})$ for the 2R arm. Two scalar equations, two unknown joint angles — but the map is **non-linear** (sines and cosines of sums).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — The Jacobian (Differential Map)

The Jacobian is the **first-order linearisation** of the FK map. It maps infinitesimal joint motions to infinitesimal Cartesian motions of the end-effector.

**Step 1 — Differentiate $x$ with respect to each joint variable.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} &= -L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} &= -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $y$ with respect to each joint variable.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} &= \;\;L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} &= \;\;L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Assemble into the Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{2\times 2}$.**

$$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ \;\;L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & \;\;L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Use the determinant to detect singularities.**

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \sin q_2$$

**Reasoning:** When $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$, i.e., when $q_2 = 0$ or $q_2 = \pi$, the manipulator is in a **singular configuration** (arm fully stretched or fully folded back). At this configuration the end-effector loses the ability to move in a particular Cartesian direction — even though both joints can still rotate, the *Cartesian* motion in one direction becomes zero.

**Step 5 — Write the velocity relationship.**

$$\begin{bmatrix}\dot{x} \\ \dot{y}\end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\begin{bmatrix}\dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2\end{bmatrix}$$

This is the fundamental relation that **bridges Joint Space velocities and Cartesian Space velocities** — the heart of differential kinematics and the foundation of resolved-rate motion control.

## 3.3 Inverse Kinematics: Cartesian → Joint (Analytical, 2R Arm)

Given a desired Cartesian target $(x_d, y_d)$, the **squared distance** from the base is:

$$r^{2} = x_d^{2} + y_d^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the law of cosines on the triangle formed by $L_1$, $L_2$, and $r$.**

$$r^{2} = L_1^{2} + L_2^{2} - 2L_1 L_2 \cos(\pi - q_2)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(\pi - q_2) = -\cos q_2$:

$$r^{2} = L_1^{2} + L_2^{2} + 2L_1 L_2 \cos q_2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $q_2$ (elbow angle).**

$$q_2 = \arccos\!\left(\frac{r^{2} - L_1^{2} - L_2^{2}}{2L_1 L_2}\right)$$

Note the **two valid solutions** (elbow-up / elbow-down):

$$q_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left(\frac{x_d^{2} + y_d^{2} - L_1^{2} - L_2^{2}}{2L_1 L_2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $q_1$ (shoulder angle).**

$$q_1 = \arctan2(y_d, x_d) - \arctan2\!\left(L_2 \sin q_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos q_2\right)$$

This explicit two-solution structure is the most-favoured KTU 14-mark question pattern.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Criterion | Joint Space | Cartesian Space |
|---|---|---|
| Mapping direction | $\mathbf{q} \rightarrow \mathbf{x}$ (FK) | $\mathbf{x} \rightarrow \mathbf{q}$ (IK) |
| Mathematical nature | Unique, closed-form | Multiple solutions, transcendental |
| Path shape between two points | Non-linear, unpredictable curve | Straight line (LIN) or circle (CIRC) |
| Computational load per update | Very low (1 matrix mult.) | High (IK iterations or trig.) |
| Joint-limit handling | Trivial (clamp $q_i$) | Hard (limits become non-linear constraints in $\mathbf{x}$) |
| Singularities | Not visible in the space itself | Detected by $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ |
| Sensor feedback | Encoders read $q_i$ directly | Need FK to translate |
| Typical KTU mark weightage | 7 marks in 14-mark Q | 7 marks in 14-mark Q |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Joint vs Cartesian Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    jointVars["Joint Space Vector q"]
    fkBlock["Forward Kinematics x equals f of q"]
    cartPose["Cartesian Pose x position and orientation"]
    ikBlock["Inverse Kinematics q equals f inverse of x"]
    jacMat["Jacobian J of q"]
    velMap["Differential Map x dot equals J times q dot"]
    singBlock["Singularity Detector det J equals zero"]
    
    jointVars --> fkBlock
    fkBlock --> cartPose
    cartPose --> ikBlock
    ikBlock --> jointVars
    jointVars --> jacMat
    cartPose --> jacMat
    jacMat --> velMap
    jacMat --> singBlock
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — "Which Space to Use?"

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["New Robot Task Given"]
    qCheck{"Is path shape critical?"}
    pTask["Pick Cartesian Space IK plus LIN or CIRC"]
    jTask["Pick Joint Space PTP move"]
    limitCheck{"Joint limits critical?"}
    jPath["Stay in Joint Space"]
    singCheck{"Singularities must be avoided?"}
    jPath2["Use Joint Space for safety"]
    cartFinal["Use Cartesian Space only when needed"]
    
    startA --> qCheck
    qCheck --> pTask
    qCheck --> jTask
    pTask --> limitCheck
    limitCheck --> jPath
    limitCheck --> singCheck
    singCheck --> jPath2
    singCheck --> cartFinal
```

## 4.3 Schematic — 2R Planar Arm Geometry

```mermaid
graph LR
    baseNode["Base O0 origin"]
    joint1Node["Joint 1 q1 shoulder"]
    joint2Node["Joint 2 q2 elbow"]
    endNode["End Effector P x y"]
    
    baseNode ---|"Link 1 length L1"| joint1Node
    joint1Node ---|"Link 2 length L2"| joint2Node
    joint2Node ---|"Task Frame"| endNode
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Hybrid Control Strategy

```mermaid
graph TD
    userTask["User specifies Cartesian waypoints x desired"]
    ikMod["IK Module solves for q desired"]
    jointCtrl["Joint Servo Controller reads q actual"]
    fkMod["FK Module returns x actual from encoders"]
    errCalc["Error Signal e equals x desired minus x actual"]
    
    userTask --> ikMod
    ikMod --> jointCtrl
    jointCtrl --> fkMod
    fkMod --> errCalc
    errCalc --> jointCtrl
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
Distinguish between **Joint Space** and **Cartesian Space** descriptions of a robotic manipulator. Give one industrial application where each is preferred.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Joint Space | Cartesian Space |
|---|---|---|
| Variables | Joint angles $q_i$ (rad) and displacements (m) | End-effector coordinates $x, y, z$ and orientation $\phi, \theta, \psi$ |
| Dimension | $n$ (equal to DOF) | up to **6** |
| Mapping | $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{q})$ — FK | $\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{f}^{-1}(\mathbf{x})$ — IK |

- *Application — Joint Space:* Palletising where point-to-point (PTP) motion between bins is required, joint limits matter, and the exact path shape is irrelevant.
- *Application — Cartesian Space:* Arc-welding along a **straight seam**, where the tool must trace a linear path between two waypoints in 3-D space.

*(Marker splits: 1 mark definition each, 1 mark for application.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Define the **Jacobian matrix** of a robotic manipulator. State its role in relating joint-space and Cartesian-space velocities.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the matrix of first-order partial derivatives of the forward-kinematic position/orientation vector with respect to the joint variables:

$$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{\partial \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{q})}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$$

It establishes a *linear* (locally valid) relationship between the **joint-velocity vector** $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ and the **end-effector velocity vector** $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ in Cartesian space:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

Additionally, by the principle of virtual work, it also maps Cartesian forces $\mathbf{F}$ to joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{F}$$

*(Marker splits: 1.5 marks for definition, 1 mark velocity relation, 0.5 mark for force relation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Forward Kinematics & Joint-Space Planning*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** For a 2R planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ and joint angles $q_1, q_2$, derive the **forward kinematics** expressing the end-effector position $(x, y)$ in terms of $q_1$ and $q_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Obtain the **Jacobian matrix** of this manipulator and discuss what happens at the configuration $q_2 = 0$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Frame the geometry (1 mark).** 
Place the base frame at $O_0$. Link 1 of length $L_1$ rotates by $q_1$ about the $Z$-axis. Link 2 of length $L_2$ rotates by $q_2$ *relative to* link 1.

**Step 2 — Position of the elbow (joint 2) (2 marks).**

$$\mathbf{p}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} L_1\cos q_1 \\ L_1\sin q_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Orientation of link 2 in the global frame (1 mark).** 
The absolute angle of link 2 is $(q_1 + q_2)$.

**Step 4 — End-effector position vector (2 marks).**

$$\mathbf{p}_e = \mathbf{p}_2 + L_2\begin{bmatrix}\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ \sin(q_1+q_2)\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Final scalar equations (1 mark).**

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) \\
y &= L_1\sin q_1 + L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition of Jacobian (1 mark).**

$$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}\dfrac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial q_2}\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Partial derivatives (3 marks).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} &= -L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} &= -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} &= \;\;L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} &= \;\;L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Assembled Jacobian (1 mark).**

$$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ \;\;L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & \;\;L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Determinant and singularity (2 marks).** Expanding $\det(\mathbf{J})$:

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \sin q_2$$

At $q_2 = 0$ we have $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ — the arm is **fully stretched** (boundary of the workspace). The end-effector instantaneously loses the ability to move along a direction perpendicular to link 1; even if the shoulder motor rotates, the tip moves only along the link-1 axis. This is a **kinematic singularity**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:**
> Students frequently **drop the negative sign** on $\partial x / \partial q_1$ and $\partial x / \partial q_2$ and **miss the singularity discussion**. The 1 mark for "$\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ when $q_2 = 0$" is a guaranteed mark *if* you state the physical meaning. The KTU valuation key also deducts **0.5 mark** for failing to write units of the Jacobian entries (m/rad).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Inverse Kinematics & Cartesian-Space Planning*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **inverse kinematics** of a 2R planar manipulator, showing that for a given end-effector position there are **two distinct solutions** for the elbow angle $q_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Joint-Space** and **Cartesian-Space** trajectory planning. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each, and describe how the **Jacobian inverse** is used in resolved-rate motion control. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Square of the target distance (1 mark).** 
Given desired end-effector position $(x_d, y_d)$:

$$r^{2} = x_d^{2} + y_d^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Law of cosines on the manipulator triangle (2 marks).** 
The squared distance can also be expressed using the angle $(\pi - q_2)$ opposite to the base:

$$r^{2} = L_1^{2} + L_2^{2} - 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(\pi - q_2)$$

**Step 3 — Trigonometric simplification (1 mark).** 
Using $\cos(\pi - q_2) = -\cos q_2$:

$$x_d^{2} + y_d^{2} = L_1^{2} + L_2^{2} + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos q_2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $q_2$ — note the $\pm$ (2 marks).**

$$q_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left(\frac{x_d^{2} + y_d^{2} - L_1^{2} - L_2^{2}}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$$

The **two solutions** correspond to the *elbow-up* and *elbow-down* configurations. Both are mathematically valid; the engineer chooses based on obstacles and joint limits.

**Step 5 — Solve for $q_1$ (1 mark).**

$$q_1 = \arctan 2(y_d, x_d) - \arctan 2\!\left(L_2 \sin q_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos q_2\right)$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Joint-space trajectory (1 mark).** 
Joint-space planning interpolates each joint variable $q_i(t)$ independently (cubic polynomial, quintic spline, or trapezoidal velocity profile). The end-effector path in Cartesian space is **not** a straight line; it is a curve determined by the kinematics.

**Step 2 — Cartesian-space trajectory (1 mark).** 
Cartesian-space planning interpolates the end-effector pose $\mathbf{x}(t)$ along a user-specified path (linear LIN or circular CIRC). At each waypoint the inverse kinematics is invoked to obtain $\mathbf{q}(t)$ for the motors.

**Step 3 — Comparison table (3 marks).**

| Aspect | Joint Space | Cartesian Space |
|---|---|---|
| **Advantage** | Simple, no IK needed; respects joint limits naturally | Intuitive, straight-line paths for welding / gluing |
| **Disadvantage** | Unpredictable Cartesian path; useless for path-critical tasks | IK may fail, may give multiple solutions, singularities possible |

**Step 4 — Resolved-rate motion control (2 marks).** 
Given a desired Cartesian velocity $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d$, joint rates are computed by **pseudo-inverting** the Jacobian:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d$$

where $\mathbf{J}^{+}$ is the Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse (used when $\mathbf{J}$ is non-square or singular). This allows real-time conversion of a Cartesian task-space velocity command into joint-motor velocity commands.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> (1) When solving for $q_2$, **do NOT forget the $\pm$** — the question specifically tests the two-solution nature; missing either sign costs 1 full mark.
> (2) For the comparison, do **not** write vague sentences like "Cartesian is better" — the marker wants a *concrete* advantage (e.g., "linear seam tracing") and a *concrete* disadvantage (e.g., "IK failure near workspace boundary").
> (3) The KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly requires the **$\pm$ sign** *and* the $\arctan 2$ (not $\arctan$) to receive full 1 mark for the $q_1$ expression.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024**

- ✅ **Joint Space** = vector $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, \ldots, q_n]^{T}$ of joint variables (angles in **rad**, distances in **m**).
- ✅ **Cartesian Space** = vector $\mathbf{x} = [p_x, p_y, p_z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^{T}$ of end-effector pose (lengths in **m**, angles in **rad**).
- ✅ **FK is unique**, IK is **non-unique** (up to 8 solutions for a 6R arm; 2 for a 2R arm).
- ✅ Forward map: $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{q})$; Inverse map: $\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{f}^{-1}(\mathbf{x})$.
- ✅ Differential map (Jacobian): $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- ✅ Static map: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{F}$.
- ✅ 2R arm FK: $x = L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)$; $\;y = L_1\sin q_1 + L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)$.
- ✅ 2R arm singularity: $\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \sin q_2 = 0 \Rightarrow q_2 = 0$ (stretched) or $q_2 = \pi$ (folded).
- ✅ **Elbow-up** vs. **Elbow-down** — always two solutions for $q_2$ in the 2R arm.
- ✅ **Joint Space** → simple motor commands, predictable joint limits, unpredictable Cartesian path.
- ✅ **Cartesian Space** → straight-line paths, intuitive, but requires IK and may fail.
- ✅ Resolved-rate control: $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d$.
- ✅ Always use **$\arctan 2(y, x)$** (not $\arctan(y/x)$) to avoid quadrant ambiguity in $q_1$.
- ✅ Singularity → loss of **instantaneous** Cartesian DOF, **not** loss of joint motion.
- ✅ Yoshikawa manipulability $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})}$ is a scalar measure of how "far-from-singular" a configuration is.
- ✅ KTU exam mantra: *“Specify in Cartesian, compute in Joint, control in Joint, feedback in Joint, display in Cartesian.”*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Point-to-Point Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY AND ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Point-to-Point Control # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Point-to-Point (PTP) Control** is a fundamental robotic motion control strategy in which the robot manipulator is commanded to move from one predefined position (point) in its workspace to another predefined position, with **no specification or control of the path, velocity profile, or trajectory** traversed between these two endpoint locations. In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PBPET404 – Mechanics of Machinery and Robotics**, PTP control is classified as the *simplest and most widely deployed* motion control category, primarily used in **pick-and-place operations, loading/unloading tasks, spot welding, drilling, and palletizing applications**. The controller's sole objective is to ensure that each joint (or the end-effector) reaches the **target coordinate accurately and repeatedly**, irrespective of what the joint does in transit. | Parameter | Description | KTU Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | **Start Point** | Initial joint configuration $q_0$ | Radians (rad) or mm | | **End Point** | Target joint configuration $q_f$ | Radians (rad) or mm | | **Path** | **Uncontrolled / Irrelevant** | – | | **Control Variable** | Final position only | – | | **Cycle Time** | Total motion + dwell time | Seconds (s) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Elevator Analogy:** Imagine you enter an elevator on the Ground Floor and press the button for the **5th Floor**. The elevator takes you up — but you have absolutely no control over *how* it gets there, the *speed* profile it uses, or the *path* it follows inside the shaft. You only care that the door opens on the 5th Floor, exactly aligned with the floor level. This is **Point-to-Point Control**. The elevator (robot) is not concerned with what happens between the start and end positions. **Another Real-World Analogy — The Postal Letter:** Think of a courier service. The courier guarantees the letter will reach the **destination address** (target point) intact and on time. However, the courier is free to choose *any route*, *any vehicle*, and *any timing* as long as the parcel arrives. PTP robots behave identically: the controller only enforces the **arrival condition**, not the journey. ## 1.3 Salient Features of PTP Control > [!NOTE] > **Key Characteristics — Must Remember for KTU Board Exams:** > 1. The **end-effector position** is the only controlled variable. > 2. **Path between points is not specified** and may vary cycle-to-cycle. > 3. **Velocity profile is uncontrolled** (or pre-programmed in servo systems). > 4. Simpler controller architecture → **lower cost** and **easier programming**. > 5. Uses **bang-bang control** (non-servo) or **trapezoidal velocity profile** (servo-based). > 6. Also called **"Stop-to-Stop"** or **"Limited Sequence"** control in older literature. ## 1.4 Historical / Industrial Context PTP control emerged as the **first generation of industrial robot control** (Unimate, 1961). It dominated **75% of early industrial robot installations** in the 1970s-80s and remains the workhorse for **high-speed pick-and-place** cells in the FMCG (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods) and pharmaceutical industries. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of PTP Control Systems PTP control is broadly classified into **two major architectures** based on the feedback mechanism employed: ### 2.1.1 Non-Servo (Open-Loop) PTP Control — "Bang-Bang Control" > [!IMPORTANT] > In **non-servo PTP systems**, the actuator (typically a **stepper motor** or **pneumatic/hydraulic cylinder with limit switches**) is commanded to move a *fixed number of increments* without any feedback sensor to verify the actual position reached. **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bullets):** - The controller sends a **fixed number of pulses** to the stepper motor driver. - Each pulse rotates the motor by a precise **step angle** $\theta_s$. - The mechanical linkage (lead screw, rack-and-pinion) converts rotation into linear motion. - The system **assumes** the load has reached the target — there is **no verification**. - **End-of-travel is detected** using **mechanical limit switches** or **proximity sensors**. **Advantages:** - ✅ Extremely **simple and inexpensive** hardware - ✅ **No feedback sensors** required (cost saving) - ✅ **High reliability** for light loads - ✅ **Easy to program** — count the pulses **Disadvantages:** - ❌ **No error correction** — missed steps are permanent errors - ❌ **Poor accuracy** under varying loads - ❌ **Limited payload capacity** - ❌ **Open-loop instability** under dynamic loading ### 2.1.2 Servo (Closed-Loop) PTP Control > [!IMPORTANT] > In **servo-based PTP control**, **position feedback sensors** (encoders, resolvers, LVDTs) continuously report the actual joint position to the controller. The controller **compares actual vs. commanded** position and **drives the actuator** to minimize the error $e(t) = q_{desired}(t) - q_{actual}(t)$. **Operational Logic:** - The controller outputs a **velocity command** to each joint servo. - The **trapezoidal velocity profile** is the most common: **Acceleration → Constant Velocity → Deceleration**. - The system decelerates as it approaches the target point to ensure **accurate stopping**. - Feedback from **encoders** (typical resolution: 1000–10,000 pulses/revolution) provides **real-time position verification**. **Advantages:** - ✅ **High accuracy** (sub-millimeter possible) - ✅ **Handles variable loads** gracefully - ✅ **Programmable velocity profiles** - ✅ **Suitable for heavy payloads** (up to 1000+ kg) **Disadvantages:** - ❌ **Complex and expensive** hardware - ❌ Requires **tuning of PID gains** (stability issue) - ❌ Susceptible to **encoder noise and drift** ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains **all essential formulas** required for solving KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problems on Point-to-Point Control. **Memorize these for the ESE (End Semester Examination).** | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Engineering Meaning | SI Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Stepper Motor Step Angle** | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_{steps}}$ | Angular displacement per pulse | degrees (°) | | 2 | **Linear Resolution per Step** | $\Delta x = \dfrac{P \cdot \theta_s}{360°} = \dfrac{P}{N_{steps}}$ | Smallest linear increment | metres (m) | | 3 | **Total Linear Position** | $x = s \cdot \Delta x = \dfrac{s \cdot P}{N_{steps}}$ | Position after $s$ pulses | metres (m) | | 4 | **Pulses Required for Distance $L$** | $s = \dfrac{L \cdot N_{steps}}{P}$ | Number of command pulses | dimensionless | | 5 | **Joint Angular Resolution** | $\Delta q = \dfrac{2\pi}{N_{steps}}$ | Smallest angular increment | radians (rad) | | 6 | **Positioning Error (Open-Loop)** | $e_p = \Delta x \cdot s_{lost}$ | Error from missed steps | metres (m) | | 7 | **Trapezoidal Velocity Profile – Total Time** | $T_{total} = t_a + t_c + t_d$ | Total move time | seconds (s) | | 8 | **Acceleration Time** | $t_a = \dfrac{V_{max}}{a_{max}}$ | Time to reach max velocity | seconds (s) | | 9 | **Distance During Acceleration** | $d_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot a_{max} \cdot t_a^2$ | Distance covered while accelerating | metres (m) | | 10 | **Cycle Time (PTP)** | $T_{cycle} = T_{move} + T_{dwell}$ | Time per pick-and-place cycle | seconds (s) | | 11 | **Gear Reduction Ratio** | $G = \dfrac{\theta_{motor}}{\theta_{joint}}$ | Stepper-to-joint ratio | dimensionless | | 12 | **Joint Resolution (with gearbox)** | $\Delta q_{joint} = \dfrac{\theta_s}{G}$ | Effective joint resolution | radians (rad) | | 13 | **Servo Steady-State Error** | $e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$ | Final positioning error | metres (m) or rad | | 14 | **Throughput (Parts/Hour)** | $\eta = \dfrac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$ | Production rate | parts/hour | Where the symbols are defined as: - $N_{steps}$ = total number of steps per motor revolution - $P$ = lead screw pitch (linear distance per revolution) - $s$ = number of pulses sent - $L$ = commanded linear distance - $s_{lost}$ = number of missed pulses - $V_{max}$ = maximum (cruise) velocity - $a_{max}$ = maximum acceleration - $T_{dwell}$ = dwell time (pause) at the endpoint ## 2.3 Path Planning in PTP Control > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU Board Exams — Critical Distinction:** > In **pure PTP control**, NO path planning is performed. The robot simply commands each joint to move from start angle $q_0^{(i)}$ to end angle $q_f^{(i)}$ simultaneously. The **Cartesian path of the end-effector is unpredictable** unless the kinematics are coincidentally aligned. > > However, in **modern PTP controllers**, a **coordinated joint motion** scheme is used: all joints start and stop at the **same time**, producing a *predictable* but still *uncontrolled* straight-line-ish path in joint space. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications | Industry | Typical PTP Task | Why PTP is Sufficient | |---|---|---| | **Automotive Spot Welding** | Move weld gun to fixed point on car body | Path between welds is irrelevant | | **PCB Assembly** | Pick component → Place on board | Only start and end positions matter | | **Packaging** | Pick box from conveyor → Place in carton | No trajectory control needed | | **Machine Tending (CNC)** | Load raw blank → Unload finished part | Pure transfer motion | | **Pharmaceutical** | Vial filling station transfer | Hygiene and position accuracy only | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stepper Motor Angular Resolution > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup:** A **hybrid permanent-magnet stepper motor** has **200 rotor teeth** and is driven in **full-step mode** with a **2-phase stator** excitation. Derive the fundamental step angle $\theta_s$ and the linear resolution when coupled to a **lead screw of pitch 5 mm**. ### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Relation For a stepper motor, the number of steps per revolution $N_{steps}$ equals the number of stable detent positions in one full mechanical rotation. For a hybrid stepper with $N_t$ rotor teeth driven in full-step mode with $N_{phase}$ phases excited sequentially, the number of steps is: $$ N_{steps} = N_t \times N_{phase} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the Given Numerical Values Given $N_t = 200$ teeth and $N_{phase} = 2$ (full-step, 2-phase-on): $$ N_{steps} = 200 \times 2 = 400 \text{ steps/revolution} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Angular Step Size $$ \theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_{steps}} = \frac{360°}{400} = 0.9° \text{ per step} $$ Converting to radians: $$ \theta_s = 0.9° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 0.01571 \text{ rad/step} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert to Linear Resolution via Lead Screw For a lead screw of pitch $P$ (linear advance per full revolution): $$ \Delta x = \frac{P}{N_{steps}} = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{400} = 0.0125 \text{ mm/step} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Result:** The PTP positioning system has a **fundamental resolution of 12.5 µm per pulse**. This is the smallest achievable linear increment — a critical specification for board exam questions. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pulse Count for a Commanded Displacement > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup:** The same PTP system must move a payload by a **total linear distance of $L = 250$ mm**. Compute the number of pulses $s$ to be sent to the stepper motor driver. ### Step 1 — State the Governing Equation The number of pulses required for linear displacement $L$ is: $$ s = \frac{L \cdot N_{steps}}{P} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the Known Quantities $$ s = \frac{250 \text{ mm} \times 400}{5 \text{ mm}} $$ ### Step 3 — Evaluate the Numerator $$ s = \frac{100{,}000}{5} $$ ### Step 4 — Final Result $$ \boxed{s = 20{,}000 \text{ pulses}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** The controller must output a 20 kHz burst (if the move is to be completed in 1 second) to drive the load to the target. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Trapezoidal Velocity Profile for Servo PTP > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup:** A servo-driven PTP robot joint must move through a **total angular displacement of $\theta_{total} = 90° = \pi/2$ rad**. The maximum allowable angular velocity is $\omega_{max} = 1.5$ rad/s and the maximum angular acceleration is $\alpha_{max} = 3$ rad/s². Compute the **total motion time** $T_{total}$ and the **distance covered** during each phase. ### Step 1 — Compute the Acceleration Time $t_a$ Assuming the trapezoidal profile is **symmetric** (equal accel and decel times): $$ t_a = \frac{\omega_{max}}{\alpha_{max}} = \frac{1.5}{3} = 0.5 \text{ s} $$ By symmetry: $$ t_d = t_a = 0.5 \text{ s} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Distance Travelled During Acceleration $$ \theta_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \alpha_{max} \cdot t_a^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times (0.5)^2 $$ $$ \theta_a = 1.5 \times 0.25 = 0.375 \text{ rad} $$ By symmetry: $$ \theta_d = \theta_a = 0.375 \text{ rad} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Remaining Distance for Constant-Velocity Phase $$ \theta_c = \theta_{total} - \theta_a - \theta_d = \frac{\pi}{2} - 0.375 - 0.375 $$ $$ \theta_c = 1.5708 - 0.75 = 0.8208 \text{ rad} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Constant-Velocity Time $t_c$ $$ t_c = \frac{\theta_c}{\omega_{max}} = \frac{0.8208}{1.5} = 0.5472 \text{ s} $$ ### Step 5 — Total Motion Time $$ T_{total} = t_a + t_c + t_d = 0.5 + 0.5472 + 0.5 $$ $$ \boxed{T_{total} = 1.5472 \text{ s}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** The trapezoidal profile is the **default velocity profile** for servo-based PTP control. A pure triangular profile (no cruise phase) is used when the move distance is short. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cycle Time and Throughput Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup:** For a pick-and-place PTP robot, the motion time computed above is $T_{move} = 1.5472$ s. The dwell time at the placement point is $T_{dwell} = 0.5$ s (for gripper release and retraction). Compute the **cycle time** and the **hourly throughput**. ### Step 1 — Cycle Time $$ T_{cycle} = T_{move} + T_{dwell} = 1.5472 + 0.5 = 2.0472 \text{ s} $$ ### Step 2 — Throughput in Parts per Hour $$ \eta = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{2.0472} = 1758.5 \text{ parts/hour} $$ ### Step 3 — Sanity Check $$ \boxed{\eta \approx 1758 \text{ parts/hour} \approx 29.3 \text{ parts/minute}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This throughput figure is typical for a **medium-speed PTP packaging robot**. High-speed delta robots can exceed 120 cycles/min (i.e., 7200 parts/hour). --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Open-Loop Position Error Due to Missed Steps > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup:** Under a heavy transient load, the stepper motor **misses 8 pulses** during a 20,000-pulse move (from the earlier example). Compute the **positioning error** $e_p$ in millimetres. ### Step 1 — Error Formula $$ e_p = s_{lost} \times \Delta x $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute Values $$ e_p = 8 \times 0.0125 \text{ mm} $$ ### Step 3 — Final Result $$ \boxed{e_p = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 100 \text{ \mu m}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Critical Board Exam Insight:** Open-loop PTP systems have **no mechanism to detect or correct missed steps**. A single lost pulse permanently offsets the system — this is the primary reason high-precision applications use **servo (closed-loop) PTP control**. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PTP vs. CP Control > [!IMPORTANT] > **This comparison table is a high-frequency KTU question — both 3-mark and 14-mark variants have appeared in past papers.** | S.No. | Parameter | **Point-to-Point (PTP)** | **Continuous Path (CP)** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Path Control** | Uncontrolled | Fully controlled | | 2 | **Velocity Profile** | Optional (often open-loop) | Mandatory (precise) | | 3 | **Number of Points** | 2 per move (start, end) | Many (interpolated) | | 4 | **Memory Required** | Low | Very high | | 5 | **Controller Complexity** | Simple | Complex | | 6 | **Cost** | Low | High | | 7 | **Typical Applications** | Pick-and-place, spot welding | Spray painting, arc welding, polishing | | 8 | **Accuracy** | Position accuracy only | Path accuracy + position accuracy | | 9 | **Sensors** | Limit switches / Encoders | Continuous encoders + resolvers | | 10 | **Examples** | SCARA, Cartesian gantry | 6-axis articulated paint robot | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Open-Loop (Non-Servo) PTP Control Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **signal and power flow** in a non-servo PTP system. The defining feature is the **absence of any feedback path** from the joint position to the controller. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[USER PROGRAM] --> B[CONTROLLER CPU] B --> C[PULSE GENERATOR] C --> D[STEPER MOTOR DRIVER] D --> E[STEPER MOTOR] E --> F[LEAD SCREW / RACK PINION] F --> G[ROBOT JOINT / SLIDER] G --> H[LIMIT SWITCH AT TARGET] H -.NO FEEDBACK.-> B G --> I[END EFFECTOR AT TARGET POINT] classDef ctrl fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A07000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef act fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#003F7F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mech fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sensor fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#7F0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,C ctrl class D,E act class F,G,I mech class H sensor ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **solid arrows** represent the **forward command path**. - The **dotted red arrow** from the limit switch to the controller signifies that feedback is **not used for real-time correction** — it is used **only for end-of-motion confirmation** and safety interlock. - The system **trusts the pulse count** blindly. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop (Servo) PTP Control Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **continuous feedback loop** in a servo-based PTP system. The **PID controller** computes the error signal and drives the actuator to minimize it. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[USER PROGRAM TARGET qf] --> B[COMMAND POSITION qd] B --> C[PID CONTROLLER] C --> D[SERVO DRIVER / AMPLIFIER] D --> E[DC / AC SERVO MOTOR] E --> F[GEARBOX] F --> G[ROBOT JOINT] G --> H[END EFFECTOR] G --> I[ENCODER / RESOLVER] I --> J[FEEDBACK SIGNAL qa] J --> K[SUMMING JUNCTION] K --> B K -- error signal e = qd minus qa --> C classDef input fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ctrl fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef act fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mech fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fb fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A input class B,C,K ctrl class D,E act class F,G,H mech class I,J fb ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **summing junction (K)** computes $e(t) = q_d(t) - q_a(t)$. - The **PID controller (C)** outputs a control voltage proportional to $e(t)$ plus its integral and derivative. - The **encoder (I)** provides **continuous position feedback** at high resolution (typically 4096–65,536 counts per revolution). - The **closed loop** enables **real-time error correction** and **disturbance rejection**. --- ## 4.3 Trapezoidal Velocity Profile — Conceptual Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START OF MOVE] --> B[PHASE 1: ACCELERATION
omega rises from 0 to omega max] B --> C[PHASE 2: CRUISE
omega constant at omega max] C --> D[PHASE 3: DECELERATION
omega falls from omega max to 0] D --> E[END OF MOVE AT TARGET POINT] E --> F[DWELL TIME tdwell] F --> G[NEXT PTP MOVE BEGINS] classDef phase fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef term fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,E,G term class B,C,D,F phase ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU Board Exams — Drawing Tip:** When asked to draw the velocity profile, always label: (1) the **acceleration slope** $= \alpha_{max}$, (2) the **cruise plateau** at $\omega_{max}$, (3) the **deceleration slope** $= -\alpha_{max}$, and (4) the **areas under the curve** that equal **distance travelled**. --- ## 4.4 PTP vs. CP — Conceptual Architecture Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PTP[POINT TO POINT CONTROL] A1[POINT A] --> A2[UNCONTROLLED PATH] --> A3[POINT B] end subgraph CP[CONTINUOUS PATH CONTROL] B1[POINT A] --> B2[INTERPOLATED PATH 1] --> B3[POINT B] --> B4[INTERPOLATED PATH 2] --> B5[POINT C] end classDef ptpnode fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cpnode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A1,A3 ptpnode class A2 ptpnode class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 cpnode ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** PTP treats each move as **two isolated points** connected by an *uncontrolled void*. CP control fills that void with **hundreds of intermediate waypoints** (interpolated at 50–1000 Hz) to ensure the **Cartesian path** follows a defined geometry (line, circle, spline). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** 2 questions are expected from this module topic in Part A. Each carries **3 marks** and requires a **concise 3–4 sentence answer** with a labelled diagram wherever applicable. ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]` **Define Point-to-Point (PTP) control in robotics. List any four salient features.** **Model Answer:** > **Point-to-Point (PTP) control** is a motion control strategy in which a robot is commanded to move from a **starting position to a target position** without any specification of the **path, velocity, or trajectory** between the two points. The controller's only objective is to ensure that the end-effector **accurately reaches the target coordinate**. **Salient Features:** 1. **Position-only control** — velocity and path are uncontrolled. 2. **Simpler hardware and software** compared to Continuous Path (CP) control. 3. Uses either **bang-bang (open-loop)** or **trapezoidal-velocity (closed-loop servo)** schemes. 4. **Lower cost** and **higher cycle speed** for repetitive pick-and-place tasks. 5. Typical applications include **spot welding, drilling, palletizing, and machine tending**. *(Self-explanatory diagram: two points connected by an arbitrary wavy line with the note "Path Uncontrolled".)* --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]` **Distinguish between open-loop (non-servo) and closed-loop (servo) Point-to-Point control systems.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | **Open-Loop (Non-Servo) PTP** | **Closed-Loop (Servo) PTP** | |---|---|---| | **Feedback** | None (or end-of-travel only) | Continuous position feedback via encoders | | **Actuator** | Stepper motor / pneumatic cylinder | DC/AC servo motor | | **Accuracy** | Limited by step size; degrades with load | High; sub-millimetre achievable | | **Error Correction** | None — missed steps cause permanent error | Real-time correction via PID controller | | **Cost & Complexity** | Low | High | | **Payload Capability** | Light | Heavy | | **Typical Use** | Light pick-and-place, PCB assembly | Heavy palletizing, CNC tending | **Concluding Statement:** Open-loop PTP is chosen for **low-cost, light-duty, high-speed** tasks, while closed-loop PTP is preferred when **accuracy, payload, and reliability** are paramount. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**: sub-part (a) and sub-part (b). Students must answer **either Question A OR Question B** (internal choice). The depth expected is **derivation + diagram + numerical + discussion**. --- ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **open-loop (non-servo) Point-to-Point control system** for a stepper-motor-driven Cartesian robot. Derive the expressions for **(i) step angle, (ii) linear resolution, and (iii) number of pulses required for a given displacement**. [7 Marks] **(b)** A stepper motor with **200 rotor teeth** is operated in **full-step 2-phase mode** and is coupled to a lead screw of **pitch 6 mm**. The motor is commanded to drive a linear slide by **480 mm**. Compute: 1. The **number of steps per revolution** of the motor. 2. The **angular step size** in degrees and radians. 3. The **linear resolution** in mm. 4. The **total number of pulses** to be issued. 5. The **resulting positioning error** if **12 pulses are lost** due to a transient overload. [7 Marks] --- ### ✦ Model Solution — Question A #### Solution to (a): **Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes):** The non-servo PTP system consists of: - **User Program / Teach Pendant** → issues the target coordinates. - **Microprocessor-based Controller** → computes the pulse count. - **Pulse Generator & Driver** → delivers current-amplified pulses to the stepper motor. - **Stepper Motor** → rotates by a precise step angle $\theta_s$ per pulse. - **Lead Screw** → converts rotation to linear motion. - **Linear Slide / Cartesian Axis** → carries the end-effector. - **Limit Switches** → confirm arrival at endpoints (no real-time correction). **Derivation of (i) Step Angle:** For a hybrid stepper with $N_t$ rotor teeth driven in full-step with $N_{phase}$ sequential excitations: $$ N_{steps} = N_t \times N_{phase} $$ Therefore, the step angle in degrees is: $$ \theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_{steps}} = \frac{360°}{N_t \cdot N_{phase}} $$ **Derivation of (ii) Linear Resolution:** If the lead screw has pitch $P$ (linear advance per revolution), the linear travel per step is: $$ \Delta x = \frac{P}{N_{steps}} = \frac{P}{N_t \cdot N_{phase}} $$ **Derivation of (iii) Pulses for Displacement $L$:** $$ s = \frac{L}{\Delta x} = \frac{L \cdot N_{steps}}{P} = \frac{L \cdot N_t \cdot N_{phase}}{P} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Explanation:** The controller simply outputs $s$ pulses, and the system assumes the slide has moved exactly $L$. There is **no closed-loop verification** during the motion. --- #### Solution to (b): **Given Data:** - $N_t = 200$ rotor teeth - $N_{phase} = 2$ (full-step, 2-phase) - Lead screw pitch $P = 6$ mm - Commanded displacement $L = 480$ mm - Lost pulses $s_{lost} = 12$ **Step 1 — Steps per Revolution:** $$ N_{steps} = N_t \times N_{phase} = 200 \times 2 = 400 \text{ steps/rev} $$ **Step 2 — Angular Step Size:** $$ \theta_s = \frac{360°}{400} = 0.9° \text{ per step} $$ $$ \theta_s = 0.9° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 0.015708 \text{ rad/step} $$ **Step 3 — Linear Resolution:** $$ \Delta x = \frac{P}{N_{steps}} = \frac{6 \text{ mm}}{400} = 0.015 \text{ mm/step} $$ **Step 4 — Total Pulses Required:** $$ s = \frac{L \cdot N_{steps}}{P} = \frac{480 \times 400}{6} = \frac{192{,}000}{6} = 32{,}000 \text{ pulses} $$ **Step 5 — Positioning Error from Lost Pulses:** $$ e_p = s_{lost} \times \Delta x = 12 \times 0.015 \text{ mm} = 0.18 \text{ mm} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Tabulated Answer for Valuation:** | Quantity | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Steps per revolution | $N_{steps}$ | **400 steps/rev** | | Angular step size | $\theta_s$ | **0.9° = 0.01571 rad** | | Linear resolution | $\Delta x$ | **0.015 mm/step** | | Total pulses | $s$ | **32,000 pulses** | | Positioning error | $e_p$ | **0.18 mm** | --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **closed-loop (servo) Point-to-Point control system** with a labelled block diagram. Discuss the role of the **PID controller** and the **trapezoidal velocity profile** in achieving accurate PTP motion. [7 Marks] **(b)** A servo-driven PTP robot joint must execute a move of **$\theta_{total} = 120°$** with the following constraints: - Maximum angular velocity $\omega_{max} = 2$ rad/s - Maximum angular acceleration $\alpha_{max} = 4$ rad/s² - Dwell time at target $T_{dwell} = 0.4$ s Compute: 1. The **acceleration time** $t_a$ and **deceleration time** $t_d$. 2. The **angular distance** covered during acceleration and deceleration. 3. Whether the **trapezoidal profile is feasible** (i.e., whether a constant-velocity phase exists). If yes, compute the **cruise time** $t_c$ and the **total motion time** $T_{total}$. 4. The **cycle time** and the **hourly throughput** in parts/hour. [7 Marks] --- ### ✦ Model Solution — Question B #### Solution to (a): **Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes):** The closed-loop PTP system comprises: - **Command Generator** → produces the desired position profile $q_d(t)$. - **Summing Junction** → computes the position error $e(t) = q_d(t) - q_a(t)$. - **PID Controller** → outputs a control signal $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) \, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$. - **Servo Amplifier / Driver** → boosts the control signal to drive the motor. - **Servo Motor + Gearbox** → actuates the robot joint. - **Encoder / Resolver** → measures actual joint position $q_a(t)$ and feeds it back to the summing junction. **Role of the PID Controller:** - **Proportional term ($K_p$):** Provides the **primary corrective action** proportional to the instantaneous error. - **Integral term ($K_i$):** Eliminates **steady-state error** by accumulating past errors. - **Derivative term ($K_d$):** Provides **damping** by anticipating future error based on its rate of change. **Role of the Trapezoidal Velocity Profile:** - Ensures the motor does not exceed $\omega_{max}$ or $\alpha_{max}$ (prevents mechanical shock). - The **acceleration phase** ramps velocity from 0 to $\omega_{max}$ over time $t_a = \omega_{max} / \alpha_{max}$. - The **cruise phase** maintains $\omega_{max}$ for the bulk of the distance. - The **deceleration phase** symmetrically ramps velocity from $\omega_{max}$ to 0, ensuring **smooth and accurate stopping** at the target point. - This profile **minimizes cycle time** while respecting **actuator and payload constraints**. --- #### Solution to (b): **Given Data:** - $\theta_{total} = 120° = 120 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 2.0944$ rad - $\omega_{max} = 2$ rad/s - $\alpha_{max} = 4$ rad/s² - $T_{dwell} = 0.4$ s **Step 1 — Acceleration and Deceleration Times:** $$ t_a = t_d = \frac{\omega_{max}}{\alpha_{max}} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5 \text{ s} $$ **Step 2 — Angular Distance During Acceleration (and Deceleration):** $$ \theta_a = \theta_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \alpha_{max} \cdot t_a^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times (0.5)^2 $$ $$ \theta_a = 2 \times 0.25 = 0.5 \text{ rad} $$ **Step 3 — Feasibility of Trapezoidal Profile:** Total distance covered during accel + decel phases: $$ \theta_a + \theta_d = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ rad} $$ Since $\theta_a + \theta_d = 1.0$ rad **<** $\theta_{total} = 2.0944$ rad, the **trapezoidal profile is feasible** and a cruise phase exists. **Cruise distance:** $$ \theta_c = \theta_{total} - \theta_a - \theta_d = 2.0944 - 1.0 = 1.0944 \text{ rad} $$ **Cruise time:** $$ t_c = \frac{\theta_c}{\omega_{max}} = \frac{1.0944}{2} = 0.5472 \text{ s} $$ **Total motion time:** $$ T_{total} = t_a + t_c + t_d = 0.5 + 0.5472 + 0.5 = 1.5472 \text{ s} $$ **Step 4 — Cycle Time and Throughput:** $$ T_{cycle} = T_{total} + T_{dwell} = 1.5472 + 0.4 = 1.9472 \text{ s} $$ $$ \eta = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{1.9472} = 1848.8 \text{ parts/hour} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Tabulated Answer for Valuation:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | $t_a = t_d$ | **0.5 s** | | $\theta_a = \theta_d$ | **0.5 rad** | | Trapezoidal profile feasible? | **Yes** | | Cruise distance $\theta_c$ | **1.0944 rad** | | Cruise time $t_c$ | **0.5472 s** | | Total motion time $T_{total}$ | **1.5472 s** | | Cycle time $T_{cycle}$ | **1.9472 s** | | Throughput $\eta$ | **≈ 1849 parts/hour** | --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Examiner's Note (Valuation Key Insights) — Read Carefully Before Exam:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–1 mark):** Students frequently write $\theta_s = 0.9°$ and forget to convert to radians when used in rad/s equations. Always state both. > 2. **Mixing $N_t$ and $N_{steps}$ (–1 mark):** The "number of rotor teeth" is **NOT** the same as "steps per revolution." Use the formula $N_{steps} = N_t \times N_{phase}$ explicitly. > 3. **Forgetting the Gear Ratio (–2 marks):** If a gearbox is mentioned in the problem, the joint resolution is $\Delta q_{joint} = \theta_s / G$, **not** $\theta_s$ directly. Many students miss this and lose substantial marks. > 4. **Path vs. Position Confusion (–2 marks):** PTP does **NOT** control the path. If a question asks "what is the path of the end-effector in PTP?", the answer is **"uncontrolled and not specified"** — not "straight line." > 5. **Servo vs. Stepper Identification (–1 mark):** Open-loop PTP → **stepper motor**; Closed-loop PTP → **servo motor**. Reversing these loses a mark. > 6. **Block Diagram Labelling (–1 to –2 marks):** Every block in the diagram **must be labelled** with the component name. An unlabelled block is considered incomplete. > 7. **Trapezoidal Profile Total Area Check:** If $\theta_a + \theta_d > \theta_{total}$, the profile degenerates into a **triangular profile** (no cruise phase). Always verify before computing $t_c$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Read 5 minutes before the exam.** ### 🔑 Core Definitions - **PTP Control:** Move from point A to point B; **path is uncontrolled**. - **CP Control:** Move from point A to point B; **path is fully controlled** via interpolation. - **Non-Servo PTP:** Open-loop, stepper motor, no feedback — uses **bang-bang control**. - **Servo PTP:** Closed-loop, servo motor, encoder feedback — uses **trapezoidal velocity profile**. ### 🔑 Critical Formulas to Memorize - $\theta_s = 360° / N_{steps}$ - $\Delta x = P / N_{steps}$ - $s = L \cdot N_{steps} / P$ - $T_{cycle} = T_{move} + T_{dwell}$ - $\eta = 3600 / T_{cycle}$ parts/hour - $t_a = \omega_{max} / \alpha_{max}$ - $\theta_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha_{max} t_a^2$ ### 🔑 Decision Rule: Trapezoidal vs. Triangular Profile - If $\theta_{total} > 2 \theta_a$ → **Trapezoidal** (cruise phase exists). - If $\theta_{total} \leq 2 \theta_a$ → **Triangular** (no cruise phase; $t_c = 0$). ### 🔑 Applications Mnemonic — **"P-S-D-P-M"** - **P**alletizing - **S**pot welding - **D**rilling / PCB assembly - **P**ick-and-place - **M**achine tending ### 🔑 Key Comparison Axes (for 14-mark questions) 1. Path control 2. Velocity profile 3. Feedback type 4. Cost and complexity 5. Typical applications 6. Accuracy metric ### 🔑 Industrial Robot Classifications Using PTP - **Cartesian (Gantry) Robots** — predominantly PTP. - **SCARA Robots** — PTP for vertical pick-and-place, CP for in-plane paths. - **Cylindrical and Spherical Robots** — early PTP-dominant designs. ### 🔑 One-Line Takeaway > *"PTP control cares **where** the robot ends up, **not how** it gets there."*

topics.title: Trajectory Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY AND ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trajectory Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Trajectory Generation** is the process of computing a time-based sequence of intermediate configurations (positions, velocities, and accelerations) of a robotic manipulator that causes it to move from an initial pose to a final pose while satisfying prescribed kinematic constraints and smoothness criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Path vs Trajectory — The Critical Distinction**
> - A **Path** is a purely *geometric* curve in space — it is a locus of points with no time information.
> - A **Trajectory** is a *time-parameterized* path — every point on the path is associated with a specific time instant $t$.
> - Mathematically: $\text{Trajectory} = \text{Path} + \text{Time Law (Timing Function)}$

## 1.2 Joint-Space vs Cartesian-Space Trajectories

A robot arm can be commanded either in its **joint space** (actuator coordinates $q_i$) or in its **Cartesian/Task space** (end-effector pose $X, Y, Z, \phi, \theta, \psi$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Joint-Space Trajectory** — Interpolates the joint variables $\theta_1(t), \theta_2(t), \ldots, \theta_n(t)$ between initial and final joint configurations using a scalar timing law. The end-effector path between the two configurations is **not** a straight line, but the joint motions are smooth, predictable, and free of kinematic singularities along the route.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cartesian-Space Trajectory** — Interpolates the end-effector pose along a desired geometric path (e.g., a straight line, a circular arc). This requires **inverse kinematics** to be solved at many sample points, which is computationally expensive but gives a clean, predictable tool-path for tasks like welding or gluing.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The GPS + Cruise Control Analogy

Imagine you are driving from your home to the office using a GPS:

- **Path Planning** is the route the GPS draws on the map (e.g., "Take the second right, then go straight for 2 km"). The route has a shape but no schedule.
- **Trajectory Generation** is the speedometer and cruise-control system layered on top: "Reach 40 km/h in 5 s, maintain 40 km/h, then decelerate to 0 km/h in 3 s at the destination."
- The car must **respect the speed limits, accelerate smoothly without jerking, and stop exactly at the gate** — these are the kinematic boundary conditions.

A robot trajectory is the same idea: the *path* is the route, and the *timing law* is the speed profile applied on top.

## 1.4 Classification of Motion Commands

| Motion Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **PTP (Point-to-Point)** | Only start and end poses specified; intermediate path is unconstrained | Material handling, pick-and-place |
| **CP (Continuous Path)** | Intermediate points are also specified; path shape matters | Spray painting, welding, cutting |
| **Multi-Point (Via Points)** | Sequence of via points that the robot must pass through | Assembly, inspection routes |

## 1.5 Kinematic Constraints Used in Trajectory Generation

A good trajectory is **C²-continuous** (continuous position, velocity, and acceleration). The most common constraints are:

- $q(t_0) = q_0$ — initial position
- $q(t_f) = q_f$ — final position
- $\dot{q}(t_0) = \dot{q}_0$ — initial velocity (usually 0)
- $\dot{q}(t_f) = \dot{q}_f$ — final velocity (usually 0)
- $\ddot{q}(t_0) = \ddot{q}_0$ — initial acceleration (often 0)
- $\ddot{q}(t_f) = \ddot{q}_f$ — final acceleration (often 0)

> [!TIP]
> Each constraint eliminates one degree of freedom in the polynomial. A 3rd-order (cubic) polynomial has 4 coefficients → handles 4 constraints. A 5th-order (quintic) polynomial has 6 coefficients → handles all 6 constraints above.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Trajectory Generation Pipeline (Logical Flow)

1. **Specify the start and goal configurations** in joint space (after IK, if the user gave a Cartesian goal).
2. **Choose a path description**: direct interpolation in joint space, or a Cartesian curve (line, circle) sampled densely.
3. **Choose a timing law / interpolation scheme** (cubic, quintic, trapezoidal, splines).
4. **Apply boundary conditions** on position, velocity, and (optionally) acceleration.
5. **Solve the linear system** for the polynomial coefficients.
6. **Sample the trajectory** at a fixed rate (e.g., every 10 ms) and feed the commands to the low-level joint servo controllers.
7. **Verify smoothness** — ensure no velocity or acceleration discontinuities that would excite structural vibrations.

## 2.2 Why Are Polynomials Used?

Polynomials are the workhorse of trajectory generation because:

- They are **infinitely differentiable** in closed form.
- They allow **analytical enforcement of boundary conditions** by solving a linear system.
- They are **trivially sampled** at any time instant.
- Higher-degree polynomials give **more degrees of freedom** for additional constraints (via points, acceleration continuity).

## 2.3 Method 1 — Cubic Polynomial Trajectories (The KTU Workhorse)

A cubic polynomial in time $t$ for a single joint variable is:

$$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

With the four boundary conditions $q(0)=q_0$, $q(t_f)=q_f$, $\dot{q}(0)=0$, $\dot{q}(t_f)=0$, the closed-form coefficients are:

$$a_0 = q_0, \quad a_1 = 0, \quad a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{2}}, \quad a_3 = \frac{-2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{3}}$$

**Engineering Utility:** Used in PTP motion of SCARA and small 6-DOF arms where the start and end velocities are zero. The acceleration is a smooth ramp; the velocity profile is a smooth bump (parabolic at the ends, flat in the middle).

## 2.4 Method 2 — Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSPB / Trapezoidal Velocity)

To approximate a **constant-velocity** profile with smooth acceleration at the start and end, the trajectory is built from three phases:

- **Phase 1 (Blend region, $0 \le t \le t_b$):** Parabolic acceleration. Position grows as $t^2$, velocity as $t$.
- **Phase 2 (Linear region, $t_b \le t \le t_f - t_b$):** Constant velocity $V$. Position grows linearly.
- **Phase 3 (Blend region, $t_f - t_b \le t \le t_f$):** Parabolic deceleration. Velocity drops to 0.

The standard formulas use a constant blend acceleration magnitude $A$:

$$V = \frac{q_f - q_0}{t_f - t_b}, \qquad t_b = \frac{t_f}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{t_f^{2} - \frac{4(q_f - q_0)}{A}}, \qquad A = \frac{V}{t_b}$$

**Engineering Utility:** Faster than cubic for long moves because the robot spends more time at peak velocity. Heavily used in industrial PLC-based motion controllers.

## 2.5 Method 3 — Quintic Polynomial Trajectories (Smooth Acceleration)

A fifth-degree polynomial has 6 coefficients and is used when **acceleration** must be prescribed at both ends:

$$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$$

With six boundary conditions ($q_0, q_f, \dot{q}_0, \dot{q}_f, \ddot{q}_0, \ddot{q}_f$), the coefficients are:

$$a_0 = q_0, \qquad a_1 = \dot{q}_0, \qquad a_2 = \frac{\ddot{q}_0}{2}$$

$$a_3 = \frac{1}{2 t_f^{3}} \left[ 20(q_f - q_0) - (8\dot{q}_f + 12\dot{q}_0)t_f - (3\ddot{q}_0 - \ddot{q}_f)t_f^{2} \right]$$

$$a_4 = \frac{1}{2 t_f^{4}} \left[ 30(q_0 - q_f) + (14\dot{q}_f + 16\dot{q}_0)t_f + (3\ddot{q}_0 - 2\ddot{q}_f)t_f^{2} \right]$$

$$a_5 = \frac{1}{2 t_f^{5}} \left[ 12(q_f - q_0) - 6(\dot{q}_f + \dot{q}_0)t_f - (\ddot{q}_0 - \ddot{q}_f)t_f^{2} \right]$$

**Engineering Utility:** Used for high-payload arms and humanoid robots where jerky motion (acceleration discontinuity) would damage gearboxes or excite structural vibrations.

## 2.6 Method 4 — Cartesian Straight-Line Trajectory

A straight-line trajectory in task space is parameterized by $u \in [0,1]$:

$$\mathbf{p}(u) = \mathbf{p}_i + u (\mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_i), \qquad u = \frac{t}{t_f}$$

At each sample time, the joint commands are recovered by **inverse kinematics**:

$$\mathbf{q}(t) = \text{IK}^{-1}\!\left(\mathbf{p}(t)\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Singularity Warning:** If the straight line in Cartesian space passes through a wrist singularity, the IK may give infinite joint velocities. The trajectory planner must detect this and either deviate the path or switch to joint-space mode.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Coefficients / Boundary Conditions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cubic polynomial form | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | 4 coefficients, 4 boundary conditions | rad (joint) |
| 2 | Cubic $a_2$ coefficient | $a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{2}}$ | Position at $t=0, t_f$ | rad/s² |
| 3 | Cubic $a_3$ coefficient | $a_3 = \frac{-2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{3}}$ | Velocity at $t=0, t_f$ | rad/s³ |
| 4 | Cubic $\dot{q}(t)$ | $\dot{q}(t) = 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$ | First derivative of $q(t)$ | rad/s |
| 5 | Cubic $\ddot{q}(t)$ | $\ddot{q}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$ | Second derivative of $q(t)$ | rad/s² |
| 6 | Quintic form | $q(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{5} a_k t^k$ | 6 coefficients, 6 boundary conditions | rad |
| 7 | Quintic $a_3$ | $\frac{1}{2 t_f^{3}}[20\Delta q - (8\dot{q}_f+12\dot{q}_0)t_f - (3\ddot{q}_0-\ddot{q}_f)t_f^2]$ | $\Delta q = q_f - q_0$ | rad/s³ |
| 8 | Quintic $a_5$ | $\frac{1}{2 t_f^{5}}[12\Delta q - 6(\dot{q}_f+\dot{q}_0)t_f - (\ddot{q}_0-\ddot{q}_f)t_f^2]$ | Acceleration continuity | rad/s⁵ |
| 9 | LSPB blend time | $t_b = \frac{t_f}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{t_f^{2} - \frac{4 \Delta q}{A}}$ | $A$ = constant blend acceleration | s |
| 10 | LSPB peak velocity | $V = \frac{\Delta q}{t_f - t_b}$ | Constant in linear segment | rad/s |
| 11 | Cartesian linear | $\mathbf{p}(u) = \mathbf{p}_i + u(\mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_i)$ | $u \in [0,1]$, $u = t/t_f$ | m |
| 12 | Cartesian circular | $\mathbf{p}(u) = \mathbf{c} + r(\cos\phi(u), \sin\phi(u))$ | $\phi(u) = \phi_i + u(\phi_f-\phi_i)$ | m, rad |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Trajectory Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive welding** | Cartesian straight line | Predictable torch path, constant travel speed for uniform bead |
| **PCB pick-and-place** | Joint-space PTP cubic | High speed, path shape irrelevant, no IK overhead |
| **Humanoid walking** | Quintic spline | Jerk minimization reduces actuator wear and energy use |
| **CNC machining** | LSPB / S-curve | Constant feed-rate in the cut, rapid transit between cuts |
| **Surgical robot (Da Vinci)** | Quintic | Jerk-limited motion protects patient tissue |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Cubic Polynomial Coefficients (4 Boundary Conditions)

### Statement
Given a single joint variable $q(t)$ that must move from $q_0$ to $q_f$ in time $t_f$ with zero initial and final velocity, derive the coefficients of the cubic polynomial $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$.

### Step 1: Write the polynomial and its first derivative

$$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

$$\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

### Step 2: Apply boundary condition 1 — Initial position

$$q(0) = a_0 + a_1 (0) + a_2 (0)^2 + a_3 (0)^3 = a_0 = q_0$$

Therefore $a_0 = q_0$.

### Step 3: Apply boundary condition 2 — Initial velocity is zero

$$\dot{q}(0) = a_1 + 2 a_2 (0) + 3 a_3 (0)^2 = a_1 = 0$$

Therefore $a_1 = 0$.

### Step 4: Apply boundary condition 3 — Final position

$$q(t_f) = a_0 + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^{2} + a_3 t_f^{3} = q_f$$

Substitute $a_0 = q_0$ and $a_1 = 0$:

$$q_0 + a_2 t_f^{2} + a_3 t_f^{3} = q_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 t_f^{2} + a_3 t_f^{3} = q_f - q_0 \quad \cdots (I)$$

### Step 5: Apply boundary condition 4 — Final velocity is zero

$$\dot{q}(t_f) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^{2} = 0$$

Substitute $a_1 = 0$:

$$2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^{2} = 0 \quad \cdots (II)$$

### Step 6: Solve the 2×2 linear system for $a_2$ and $a_3$

From equation (II):

$$2 a_2 t_f = -3 a_3 t_f^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 = -\frac{3 a_3 t_f}{2}$$

Substitute into (I):

$$\left(-\frac{3 a_3 t_f}{2}\right) t_f^{2} + a_3 t_f^{3} = q_f - q_0$$

$$-\frac{3 a_3 t_f^{3}}{2} + a_3 t_f^{3} = q_f - q_0$$

$$-\frac{a_3 t_f^{3}}{2} = q_f - q_0$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{2 (q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{3}}$$

And then:

$$a_2 = -\frac{3}{2} t_f \cdot \left[ -\frac{2 (q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{3}} \right] = \frac{3 (q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{2}}$$

### Step 7: Final compact form

$$\boxed{\,q(t) = q_0 + \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{2}} t^{2} - \frac{2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{3}} t^{3}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Velocity and Acceleration of the Cubic Trajectory

Using the derived coefficients:

$$\dot{q}(t) = \frac{6 (q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{2}} t - \frac{6 (q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{3}} t^{2} = \frac{6 \Delta q}{t_f^{2}} t \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_f}\right)$$

$$\ddot{q}(t) = \frac{6 (q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{2}} - \frac{12 (q_f - q_0)}{t_f^{3}} t = \frac{6 \Delta q}{t_f^{2}} \left(1 - \frac{2t}{t_f}\right)$$

**Observations:**

- The acceleration is **linear in time**: it is $\dfrac{6 \Delta q}{t_f^{2}}$ at $t=0$, decreases to 0 at $t = t_f/2$, and is $-\dfrac{6 \Delta q}{t_f^{2}}$ at $t = t_f$.
- The velocity profile is a **smooth bump** (parabolic near the ends, peaked in the middle). It is zero at both endpoints, satisfying the boundary conditions.
- The trajectory is $C^1$ continuous (position and velocity continuous) but **not** $C^2$ (the acceleration has a kink at the midpoint of a long move, in the form of a discontinuity of the second derivative when multiple cubic segments are joined — which is why quintic splines are preferred for very smooth motion).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A single joint of a robot must move from $\theta_0 = 10°$ to $\theta_f = 50°$ in $t_f = 4$ s. Initial and final velocities are zero. Find (a) the cubic trajectory $\theta(t)$, (b) the time at which peak velocity occurs, and (c) the maximum velocity.

### Step (a): Compute the coefficients

Convert to consistent units (we will keep degrees; ratios are unitless):

$$\Delta \theta = \theta_f - \theta_0 = 50° - 10° = 40°$$

$$a_0 = \theta_0 = 10°$$

$$a_1 = 0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3 \times 40°}{4^{2}} = \frac{120°}{16} = 7.5°/\text{s}^{2}$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{2 \times 40°}{4^{3}} = -\frac{80°}{64} = -1.25°/\text{s}^{3}$$

Therefore:

$$\theta(t) = 10° + 7.5 t^{2} - 1.25 t^{3} \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le 4 \text{ s}$$

### Step (b): Time of peak velocity

Peak velocity occurs when acceleration crosses zero:

$$\ddot{\theta}(t) = 15 t - 7.5 t^{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t(15 - 7.5 t) = 0$$

$$t = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad t = \frac{15}{7.5} = 2 \text{ s}$$

So peak velocity occurs at $t = 2$ s (the midpoint, as expected for symmetric cubic).

### Step (c): Maximum velocity

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = 15 t - 3.75 t^{2}$$

$$\dot{\theta}(2) = 15(2) - 3.75(4) = 30 - 15 = 15°/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\max} = 15°/\text{s} \text{ occurring at } t = 2 \text{ s}}$$

### Verification of final conditions

$$\theta(4) = 10 + 7.5(16) - 1.25(64) = 10 + 120 - 80 = 50° \checkmark$$

$$\dot{\theta}(4) = 15(4) - 3.75(16) = 60 - 60 = 0°/\text{s} \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation C — LSPB (Trapezoidal) Velocity Profile — Blend Time

For a trajectory with constant acceleration magnitude $A$ over the blend region of duration $t_b$, the velocity gain during blend is:

$$V = A \, t_b$$

The position change during blend is the area under the velocity triangle:

$$\Delta q_{\text{blend}} = \frac{1}{2} V t_b = \frac{1}{2} A t_b^{2}$$

Since there are two symmetric blend regions and one linear region:

$$\Delta q = 2 \left(\frac{1}{2} A t_b^{2}\right) + V (t_f - 2 t_b)$$

$$= A t_b^{2} + A t_b (t_f - 2 t_b) = A t_b t_f - A t_b^{2}$$

Setting this equal to $\Delta q$ and solving the quadratic in $t_b$:

$$A t_b^{2} - A t_f t_b + \Delta q = 0$$

$$t_b = \frac{A t_f \pm \sqrt{A^{2} t_f^{2} - 4 A \Delta q}}{2 A} = \frac{t_f}{2} \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{t_f^{2} - \frac{4 \Delta q}{A}}$$

Choosing the smaller root (so that the linear region exists):

$$\boxed{\,t_b = \frac{t_f}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{t_f^{2} - \frac{4 \Delta q}{A}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feasibility Condition:** For the trapezoidal profile to exist, we must have $t_f^{2} \geq \dfrac{4 \Delta q}{A}$. If $A$ is too small, no trapezoidal profile is possible for the given $t_f$, and the trajectory must be a pure parabolic (triangular) profile instead.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Trajectory Generation Pipeline

The block diagram below shows how a path specification is processed through the trajectory planner and finally delivered to the low-level joint controllers. This is the most important control-flow picture for KTU viva and theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[USER TASK SPECIFICATION] --> A1[Initial Pose q0]
    A0 --> A2[Final Pose qf]
    A0 --> A3[Path Type Choice]
    A1 --> B1[Joint Space Planner]
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A3 --> C1[Cartesian Space Planner]
    B1 --> B2[Select Interpolation Method]
    B2 --> B3{Method?}
    B3 -->|Cubic| P1[Apply 4 Boundary Conditions]
    B3 -->|Quintic| P2[Apply 6 Boundary Conditions]
    B3 -->|LSPB| P3[Apply 2 Velocity + Accel Bounds]
    P1 --> D1[Solve Linear System for Coeffs]
    P2 --> D1
    P3 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Sample Trajectory at Fixed Time Step]
    E1 --> E2[Generate q t, q dot t, q ddot t]
    E2 --> F1[Send to Joint Servo Controllers]
    F1 --> G1[Robot Joint Motion Executes]
    C1 --> C2[Path Discretisation into Waypoints]
    C2 --> C3[Inverse Kinematics at Each Waypoint]
    C3 --> B1
```

## 4.2 Velocity Profiles of the Three Methods (Schematic Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1[Cubic Polynomial]
        V1[Velocity bumps smoothly from 0 to peak and back to 0]
    end
    subgraph M2[Quintic Polynomial]
        V2[Velocity rises with zero initial jerk, smooth bump, zero final jerk]
    end
    subgraph M3[LSPB Trapezoidal]
        V3[Constant acceleration ramp, constant velocity plateau, constant deceleration ramp]
    end
    M1 --> COMPARE{Profile Comparison}
    M2 --> COMPARE
    M3 --> COMPARE
    COMPARE --> OUT1[Cubic is C1 continuous]
    COMPARE --> OUT2[Quintic is C2 continuous jerk minimised]
    COMPARE --> OUT3[LSPB is fastest for long moves but acceleration is discontinuous at blend points]
```

## 4.3 Joint-Space vs Cartesian-Space Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the path shape important for the task?}
    Q1 -->|Yes example welding gluing| QC[Use Cartesian Space Trajectory]
    Q1 -->|No example pick and place| QJ[Use Joint Space Trajectory]
    QC --> Q2{Does the path cross a singularity?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| QS[Switch to Joint Space near singularity]
    Q2 -->|No| QP[Sample straight line and apply inverse kinematics at each waypoint]
    QJ --> Q3{Is jerk minimisation required?}
    Q3 -->|Yes humanoid high precision| QQ[Use Quintic Polynomial]
    Q3 -->|No industrial pick and place| QC2[Use Cubic Polynomial]
```

## 4.4 Trajectory Coordinate Transformation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Task Space Path p t] --> IK[Inverse Kinematics Module]
    IK --> Q0[Joint Space Trajectory q t]
    Q0 --> J1[Servo of Joint 1]
    Q0 --> J2[Servo of Joint 2]
    Q0 --> Jn[Servo of Joint n]
    J1 --> R[Robot End Effector Motion in Real World]
    J2 --> R
    Jn --> R
```
