topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY AND ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Position/Force Control System. Overview of Robot Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hybrid Position/Force Control System

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Position/Force Control** is an advanced robotic control architecture that simultaneously regulates **position** along unconstrained (free) task-space directions and **force** along constrained (constrained) task-space directions. It was formally established by **Raibert and Craig (1981)** as an extension of Mason's compliance frame concept, and it forms the backbone of modern compliant manipulation tasks in robotics.

Mathematically, a hybrid controller partitions the task space into two complementary subspaces using a **diagonal selection matrix** $S$ (with entries 0 or 1), where one subspace is regulated by a position servo loop and the other by a force/ torque servo loop.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Writing on a Whiteboard:** When you write the letter "A" on a whiteboard, your hand must **trace an exact path** on the board's surface (position control in the $X$–$Y$ plane), while simultaneously pressing the marker against the board with a **specific, constant pressure** (force control along the $Z$-axis, perpendicular to the board). You never command a force in $X$ or $Y$ (that would be meaningless), and you never command a position in $Z$ (the marker naturally follows the surface). This is exactly what a hybrid controller does: it splits the task into "where to move" and "how hard to push."

### Why Hybrid Control? — The Physical Motivation

Pure **position control** is unsuitable when the robot's end-effector is in **mechanical contact** with the environment (e.g., polishing, deburring, assembly, wiping a window). A stiff position servo will:
- Build up enormous internal forces,
- Saturate joint torques,
- Damage the workpiece or the robot.

Conversely, pure **force control** is unsuitable for free-space motion because the robot would drift under any small load. The hybrid controller elegantly resolves this contradiction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In any contact task, the environment naturally imposes constraints. Along the **constrained directions**, position cannot vary freely (you cannot push the marker *through* the board), so we control **force**. Along the **unconstrained (free) directions**, force cannot be meaningfully defined, so we control **position**. The selection matrix $S$ encodes exactly this partition.

### Natural and Artificial Constraints — Mason's Foundation

Matthew T. Mason (1981) introduced the notion of a **compliance frame** (also called a **constraint frame**) $\{C\}$ attached to the task. In this frame, constraints decompose into:

| Constraint Type | Direction of Free Position (along which) | Direction of Constrained Force (along which) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Natural Constraints** | Environment dictates — robot **cannot** choose | Environment dictates — robot **cannot** choose |
| **Artificial Constraints** | Robot **chooses** position via controller | Robot **chooses** force via controller |

This gives the canonical **2 × 6 table** for a generic task (3 translations + 3 rotations). The diagonal pattern is unmistakable: position-controlled axes are position-constrained naturally, and force-controlled axes are force-constrained naturally.

---

## 1.2 Overview of Robot Programming

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Programming** encompasses the set of methods, languages, and tools used to specify a robot's desired motion, interaction with the environment, and decision logic. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies these into **on-line (lead-through, teach pendant)** and **off-line (textual languages, graphical simulation)** paradigms, culminating in modern **task-level / skill-based** programming.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Teaching a Child vs. Giving a Recipe:** You can teach a child to sweep the floor by **physically guiding their hand** (this is *lead-through programming*). Alternatively, you can hand them a written recipe that says "sweep toward the dustpan, apply medium pressure" (this is *offline programming*). Both work, but the recipe is more repeatable, transferable, and editable. Modern industrial robotics follows the same trade-off.

### The Programming Hierarchy

Programming methods sit on a hierarchy of abstraction, from lowest to highest:

1. **Joint-level / Teach-pendant programming** — operator walks the robot through each joint angle.
2. **Lead-through programming** — operator physically grabs the arm and traces the path.
3. **Textual (high-level) programming** — languages like **VAL, AML, AL, RAPID, KRL**.
4. **Graphical / Offline programming** — 3D simulation environments (RobotStudio, RoboDK, Visual Components).
5. **Task-level / Skill-based programming** — declaring *what* the task is, not *how* to do it.
6. **Programming by Demonstration (PbD) / Imitation learning** — robot infers the skill from human demonstration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 explicitly emphasizes the **first three methods** (lead-through, teach pendant, and offline) along with a survey of **robot programming languages** (VAL, AML, AL). These form the core of Part A and Part B questions in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Raibert–Craig Hybrid Control Architecture

### 2.1.1 Structural Decomposition

The Raibert–Craig architecture (1981) is built on three coordinate frames:

| Frame | Symbol | Origin / Orientation | Role |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Base frame** | $\{B\}$ | Fixed to robot base | Where the robot lives |
| **End-effector / Tool frame** | $\{E\}$ | Attached to the tool | The "hand" |
| **Compliance / Task frame** | $\{C\}$ | Attached to the task geometry | Where constraints are defined |

The hybrid controller uses two servo loops **operating in parallel**, one for position and one for force, and merges their outputs at the joint level.

### 2.1.2 Selection Matrix $S$

The selection matrix $S$ is a $6 \times 6$ **diagonal** matrix where each diagonal entry is either 0 or 1:

$$S = \text{diag}(s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5, s_6), \quad s_i \in \{0, 1\}$$

By convention:
- $s_i = 1$  →  the $i$-th task-space axis is **position-controlled**.
- $s_i = 0$  →  the $i$-th task-space axis is **force-controlled**.

The complement matrix $\bar{S} = I - S$ activates the force-control branch on the complementary axes. This guarantees that **position and force control are mutually exclusive along any single task-space axis** — a foundational design rule of the Raibert–Craig scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orthogonality Guarantee:** Because $S$ and $\bar{S}$ are diagonal and complementary, the commanded position error and commanded force error cannot fight each other on the same axis. This is the *defining* feature of hybrid control.

### 2.1.3 The Hybrid Control Law (Raibert–Craig, 1981)

Let us denote the task-space position and force vectors as $p \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ and $f \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$, and their desired values as $p_d$ and $f_d$. The position-control branch computes a position correction $\Delta p$, while the force-control branch computes a force correction $\Delta f$. The two are combined in the **task space** and then projected to **joint space** via the Jacobian $J(q)$:

$$\tau = J^T(q) \left[ K_{pp}\,(p_d - p) + K_{pi} \int (p_d - p)\,dt - K_{fp}\,(f_d - f) - K_{fi} \int (f_d - f)\,dt \right]$$

In practice, the position branch acts *only* where $S$ is the identity, and the force branch acts *only* where $\bar{S}$ is the identity:

$$\tau = J^T(q) \left[ S \cdot K_{pp}\,(p_d - p) + S \cdot K_{pi} \int (p_d - p)\,dt + \bar{S} \cdot K_{fp}\,(f_d - f) + \bar{S} \cdot K_{fi} \int (f_d - f)\,dt \right]$$

This is the equation that KTU examiners most frequently test.

### 2.2 Mason's Natural / Artificial Constraint Table (Canonical)

For a **canonical task** (e.g., a peg-in-hole aligned with the $Z$-axis of $\{C\}$):

| Axis | Natural Position Constraint | Natural Force Constraint | Artificial Position Constraint | Artificial Force Constraint |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $X$ | Position free | Force = 0 | Set by user | — |
| $Y$ | Position free | Force = 0 | Set by user | — |
| $Z$ | Position = 0 | Force free | — | Set by user |
| $\theta_X$ | Angle free | Moment = 0 | Set by user | — |
| $\theta_Y$ | Angle free | Moment = 0 | Set by user | — |
| $\theta_Z$ | Angle = 0 | Moment free | — | Set by user |

The user (i.e., the programmer / control engineer) fills in the **artificial constraints** column to fully specify the task.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Position Directions | Force Directions | Tool |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Deburring a cast edge** | $X$, $Y$ (trace edge) | $Z$ (push with force) | Rotary cutter |
| **Polishing a free-form surface** | $X$, $Y$ (scan surface) | $Z$, $\theta_X$, $\theta_Y$ (press + align) | Buffing wheel |
| **Wiping a window** | $X$, $Y$ (cover area) | $Z$ (constant normal force) | Squeegee |
| **Door opening** | Arc in $X$–$Y$ | $Z$ (pull against hinges) | Handle gripper |
| **Peg-in-hole assembly** | $X$, $Y$ (search spiral) | $Z$ (insertion force) | Gripper |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The **automotive industry** uses hybrid position/force control for **robotic polishing, sanding, and wheel-assembly** tasks. The compliance frame is typically computed automatically from the **CAD model of the workpiece** in modern off-line programming systems.

---

## 2.4 Robot Programming — Conceptual Pillars

### 2.4.1 Lead-Through Programming (Manual / Walk-Through)

The operator physically **grabs the end-effector** (or a handle) and guides the robot through the desired path. A **digital recorder** samples joint positions at a fixed rate (typically **30–60 Hz** for modern KUKA and FANUC controllers, historically as low as **10 Hz** on the original Unimate). On playback, the controller interpolates between sampled points.

**Advantages:**
- No programming skill required.
- Intuitive for path-heavy tasks (painting, gluing, welding).
- Rapid prototyping of new paths.

**Disadvantages:**
- Tedious for high-precision positioning.
- Cannot use sensor feedback during teach.
- Playback uses *open-loop* joint commands — no force adaptation.

### 2.4.2 Teach-Pendant Programming

The operator uses a **handheld pendant** (a keypad + joystick) to jog each joint (or the Cartesian frame) into a desired pose, then presses a **"Record"** button. The pose is stored in a textual program as a **motion command** (e.g., `MOVE P[1]`, `LIN P[2]`, `CIRC P[3]`).

**Motion primitives taught:**

| Command | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| `MOVE / PTP` | Point-to-Point (joint-interpolated) |
| `LIN` | Linear in Cartesian space |
| `CIRC` | Circular arc through three points |
| `SPLINE` | Smooth polynomial blend |

### 2.4.3 Offline Programming (OLP)

The program is created in a **3D simulation environment** (e.g., ABB RobotStudio, KUKA.Sim, FANUC ROBOGUIDE, RoboDK) on a separate PC, then **downloaded** to the controller. The cell layout is built from **CAD models** of the workpiece, fixtures, and obstacles.

**Advantages:** No production downtime, parametric programs, ability to verify reachability and collision-free paths in software.

### 2.4.4 Robot Programming Languages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus Languages:** **VAL (Unimation, 1979)**, **AL (Stanford, 1974)**, and **AML (IBM, 1979)** are explicitly named in the KTU Module 4 syllabus. Modern equivalents include **RAPID (ABB)**, **KRL (KUKA)**, **KAREL (FANUC)**, and **URScript (Universal Robots)**.

| Language | Origin | Key Innovation | Era |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **VAL** | Unimation (1979) | First commercial robot language; `MOVE`, `HERE`, `WHERE` | 1st gen |
| **AL** | Stanford AI Lab (1974) | Force / motion commands, `MOVE barm TO goal` | Research |
| **AML** | IBM (1979) | Sophisticated data structures, $A$ notation | 1st gen |
| **RAPID** | ABB (1994) | Modular procedures, interrupts, multi-tasking | Modern |
| **KRL** | KUKA (1990s) | Inline forms + expert-level KRL scripts | Modern |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **all** the high-yield formulas for Module 4. Memorize the selection-matrix relations and the control-law structure.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Selection matrix | $S = \text{diag}(s_1,\ldots,s_6)$, $s_i \in \{0,1\}$ | $s_i=1$ → position; $s_i=0$ → force |
| 2 | Complement matrix | $\bar{S} = I - S$ | Activates force branch |
| 3 | Position error | $e_p = p_d - p$ | $\mathbb{R}^{6}$ vector, in $\{C\}$ |
| 4 | Force error | $e_f = f_d - f$ | $\mathbb{R}^{6}$ vector, in $\{C\}$ |
| 5 | Hybrid control law (joint torque) | $\tau = J^T \left[ S\,K_{pp}\,e_p + S\,K_{pi}\int e_p + \bar{S}\,K_{fp}\,e_f + \bar{S}\,K_{fi}\int e_f \right]$ | Newton-metres [N·m] |
| 6 | Jacobian transpose mapping | $f_{\text{task}} \rightarrow \tau = J^T f$ | Maps task force to joint torque |
| 7 | Natural constraint (free) | Position unconstrained, force = 0 |  |
| 8 | Natural constraint (rigid) | Position = 0, force free |  |
| 9 | Artificial constraint (position) | User sets $p_d$ on free axes |  |
| 10 | Artificial constraint (force) | User sets $f_d$ on rigid axes |  |
| 11 | Lead-through sample rate | $f_s = 30\text{–}60$ Hz | Modern digital recorders |
| 12 | Task-space dimension | $n = 6$ (3 translation + 3 rotation) | Standard 6-DOF task vector |
| 13 | Compliance frame origin | $p_C = p_E - R_E^C\,d_{\text{tip}}$ | Where $\{C\}$ sits on task |
| 14 | Teach-pendant pose count | $N \geq 3$ for arcs, $\geq 2$ for lines | Minimum waypoints |
| 15 | Hybrid controller stability (sufficient) | $K_{pp} > 0$, $K_{pi} > 0$, $K_{fp} > 0$, $K_{fi} > 0$ | All gains strictly positive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Reminder:** $J^T$ (transpose, *not* inverse) is used because we are mapping **task-space force** to **joint-space torque** (static equivalence). The Jacobian *inverse* is for mapping velocities, not forces. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hybrid Control Law (Static Equivalence)

This derivation is the most-asked Part B derivation in KTU Module 4.

### Step 1 — Define the Task-Space Variables

We work in the **compliance frame** $\{C\}$. The end-effector pose relative to $\{C\}$ is a $6 \times 1$ vector $p$ comprising three translations $(x, y, z)$ and three rotations $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$. Similarly, the wrench applied by the environment is $f = [f_x, f_y, f_z, m_x, m_y, m_z]^T$.

### Step 2 — Identify the Control Subspaces

The selection matrix $S$ activates the position-control branch on free axes:

$$S = \text{diag}(s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5, s_6)$$

The complement $\bar{S}$ activates the force-control branch on constrained axes:

$$\bar{S} = I_{6 \times 6} - S$$

**Conversion Logic:** A diagonal entry $s_i$ of $1$ means "this axis is commanded by position." Its complement $\bar{s}_i = 1 - s_i = 0$, so the force branch is **silent** on that axis. Conversely, $s_i = 0$ silences the position branch and enables force. The two branches are **complementary** and **non-interfering**.

### Step 3 — Form the Position-Loop Output

The position controller is a standard **PI (Proportional + Integral)** servo. Its output (a corrective motion in $\{C\}$) is:

$$\Delta p = K_{pp}\,(p_d - p) + K_{pi} \int_{0}^{t} (p_d - p)\,d\tau$$

Multiplying by $S$ restricts this correction to position-controlled axes only:

$$\Delta p_{\text{active}} = S \cdot \Delta p$$

### Step 4 — Form the Force-Loop Output

The force controller is also a **PI** servo, but its output is a **velocity/position adjustment** that biases the robot *toward* the desired contact force. By Mason's law of cause and effect, the force error is mapped through an admittance-type gain:

$$\Delta f = K_{fp}\,(f_d - f) + K_{fi} \int_{0}^{t} (f_d - f)\,d\tau$$

Multiplying by $\bar{S}$ restricts it to force-controlled axes:

$$\Delta f_{\text{active}} = \bar{S} \cdot \Delta f$$

### Step 5 — Combine in Task Space

The two corrections are **summed** in the task frame (not joint frame!) to form the net task-space adjustment:

$$\Delta u = S\,K_{pp}\,e_p + S\,K_{pi}\int e_p + \bar{S}\,K_{fp}\,e_f + \bar{S}\,K_{fi}\int e_f$$

**Conversion Logic:** Because $S\bar{S} = \bar{S}S = 0$ (they act on disjoint axes), the two PI outputs cannot conflict on any single axis. The sum is meaningful in $\{C\}$.

### Step 6 — Map to Joint Torques via Jacobian Transpose

The final step is to convert the task-space correction into joint torques. Using the **Principle of Virtual Work**, a wrench $f$ at the end-effector produces a generalized joint torque:

$$\tau = J^T(q)\,f$$

where $J(q)$ is the **geometric Jacobian** of the manipulator. Substituting our combined correction:

$$\boxed{\;\tau = J^T(q) \left[ S\,K_{pp}\,(p_d - p) + S\,K_{pi}\int (p_d - p)\,dt + \bar{S}\,K_{fp}\,(f_d - f) + \bar{S}\,K_{fi}\int (f_d - f)\,dt \right]\;}$$

This is the **canonical Raibert–Craig hybrid control law**. Every KTU Part B question on this topic eventually reduces to writing and applying this equation.

### Step 7 — Dimension Check

| Symbol | Dimension | SI Unit |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $K_{pp}$ | diagonal gain | [1/s²] (position stiffness) |
| $K_{pi}$ | diagonal gain | [1/s³] (position integrator) |
| $K_{fp}$ | diagonal gain | [m/(N·s)] (force admittance) |
| $K_{fi}$ | diagonal gain | [m/(N·s²)] (force integrator) |
| $e_p$ | length / angle | [m] or [rad] |
| $e_f$ | force / moment | [N] or [N·m] |
| $J^T$ | dimensionless mapping | — |
| $\tau$ | torque | [N·m] |

The units multiply consistently: $[K_{pp}] \cdot [e_p] \cdot [J^T] = \text{N·m}$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Selection Matrix Construction

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (KTU Practice, Module 4):** A 6-DOF robot is to perform a **deburring task on a flat horizontal surface**. The compliance frame $\{C\}$ has its $Z$-axis perpendicular to the surface. Construct the selection matrix $S$ for this task. Write down the corresponding Raibert–Craig control law.

### Step 1 — Identify Free and Constrained Axes

| Axis | Free or Constrained? | Control Type |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $X$ (in-plane) | Free | Position |
| $Y$ (in-plane) | Free | Position |
| $Z$ (normal) | Constrained by surface | Force |
| $\theta_X$ (roll) | Free | Position |
| $\theta_Y$ (pitch) | Free | Position |
| $\theta_Z$ (yaw) | Free | Position |

### Step 2 — Construct $S$

By our convention, $s_i = 1$ for position-controlled axes and $s_i = 0$ for force-controlled axes:

$$S = \text{diag}(1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1)$$

The complement is:

$$\bar{S} = I - S = \text{diag}(0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0)$$

Notice that $\bar{S}$ has exactly **one non-zero entry**, $s_3 = 1$, corresponding to the $Z$-axis force control. All other axes are position-controlled.

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Hybrid Control Law

$$\tau = J^T(q) \left[ \text{diag}(1,1,0,1,1,1)\,K_{pp}\,(p_d - p) + \text{diag}(0,0,1,0,0,0)\,K_{fp}\,(f_d - f) + \text{ integral terms} \right]$$

The first term regulates **all position axes** except $Z$. The second term regulates **force along $Z$** only. Position and force are **decoupled** by the diagonal nature of $S$ and $\bar{S}$. ✓

### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation

- The robot's $X$–$Y$ position traces the **edge profile** of the casting.
- The end-effector maintains a **constant downward force** $f_{d,z}$ on the surface.
- The tool's **orientation** is held fixed to keep the burr cutter perpendicular to the surface.

This matches the canonical deburring task taught in industrial robotics labs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Task-Space Velocity Mapping (for completeness)

Some KTU questions ask for the kinematic mapping. If the end-effector's twist (linear + angular velocity) is $v = [\dot{p};\, \omega]$, the joint velocities are:

$$\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\,v$$

For the hybrid controller, the commanded twist is itself a mixture:

$$v_{\text{cmd}} = S\,v_p + \bar{S}\,v_f$$

where $v_p = K_{pp}\,e_p + K_{pi}\int e_p$ (position branch) and $v_f$ is the admittance output of the force branch. The joint velocities are then $\dot{q} = J^{-1}\,v_{\text{cmd}}$, and these are integrated to obtain the reference joint trajectory sent to the **inner joint servo loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus is explicit that the **outer loop is in task space** and the **inner loop is in joint space**. The Jacobian $J$ is the bridge. Always show this two-loop structure in your exam answer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Programming Methods

This comparison matrix is a high-yield recap for Part A 3-mark questions.

| Criterion | Lead-Through | Teach Pendant | Offline Programming |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Skill required** | Operator only | Basic pendant literacy | Robotics engineer |
| **Production downtime** | Yes (cell is teaching) | Yes (cell is teaching) | **No** (PC-based) |
| **Precision** | Low–medium | High | High |
| **Sensor integration** | None at teach | Limited | Full (vision, force) |
| **CAD integration** | None | None | Native |
| **Parametric / scalable** | No | Limited | **Yes** (variables) |
| **Path complexity** | Best for free curves | Best for line/arc | Best for complex paths |
| **Historical era** | 1960s (Unimate) | 1970s+ | 1990s+ |
| **Example system** | Original Unimate | KUKA smartPAD | ABB RobotStudio |

> **Examiners Love This Table:** A common 7-mark Part B question is *"Compare lead-through, teach-pendant, and offline programming with examples."* Use this matrix as the backbone of your answer and **add at least one real-world example** (e.g., FANUC welding cell, KUKA paint shop).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Raibert–Craig Hybrid Controller — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the full closed-loop architecture of the Raibert–Craig hybrid controller. The position loop and force loop operate in parallel, both in the **task (compliance) frame** $\{C\}$, and their outputs are merged at the **Jacobian transpose** stage before being sent to the **inner joint servo** stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER["USER COMMAND"]
        Pd["p_d : Desired Position<br/>(6x1 in frame C)"]
        Fd["f_d : Desired Force<br/>(6x1 in frame C)"]
    end

    subgraph POS_LOOP["POSITION LOOP (Free Axes)"]
        PeSum["e_p = p_d - p"]
        KpP["K_pp : Proportional Gain"]
        KiP["K_pi : Integral Gain"]
        IntP["Integrator over time"]
        SBlock["S : Selection Matrix<br/>(diagonal, s_i = 1)"]
    end

    subgraph FRCE_LOOP["FORCE LOOP (Constrained Axes)"]
        FeSum["e_f = f_d - f"]
        KpF["K_fp : Force-Proportional Gain"]
        KiF["K_fi : Force-Integral Gain"]
        IntF["Integrator over time"]
        SBarBlock["S-bar = I - S : Complement Matrix<br/>(diagonal, s-bar_i = 1)"]
    end

    subgraph MERGE["TASK-SPACE MERGE"]
        SumNode["Sum Node :<br/>u = S.K_pp.e_p + S.K_pi.int_e_p +<br/>S-bar.K_fp.e_f + S-bar.K_fi.int_e_f"]
    end

    subgraph MAP["JOINT-SPACE MAPPING"]
        Jt["J transpose : 6x6 Geometric Jacobian Transpose"]
        TauOut["tau : Joint Torque Vector (Nx1)"]
    end

    subgraph ROBOT["ROBOT & ENVIRONMENT"]
        JointLoop["Inner Joint Servo"]
        Manip["6-DOF Manipulator"]
        Env["Environment / Workpiece<br/>(f contact force, p pose)"]
    end

    Pd --> PeSum
    PeSum --> KpP
    PeSum --> IntP
    IntP --> KiP
    KpP --> SBlock
    KiP --> SBlock

    Fd --> FeSum
    FeSum --> KpF
    FeSum --> IntF
    IntF --> KiF
    KpF --> SBarBlock
    KiF --> SBarBlock

    SBlock --> SumNode
    SBarBlock --> SumNode

    SumNode --> Jt
    Jt --> TauOut
    TauOut --> JointLoop
    JointLoop --> Manip
    Manip --> Env
    Env -->|f measurement| FeSum
    Env -->|p measurement| PeSum
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two PI controllers (position and force) operate in parallel. The **selection matrices** $S$ and $\bar{S}$ act as **switches** that route each controller's output to its dedicated task-space axes. The sum is converted to joint torques via $J^T$, applied to the manipulator, and the cycle repeats. The **outer loop is in $\{C\}$** and the **inner loop is in joint space**.

The following Mermaid flow chart shows the decision logic a robotics engineer uses to pick the right programming method for a given task.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define hybrid position/force control. Why is it necessary for contact tasks? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Hybrid position/force control** is a robotic control scheme in which the **task space** is partitioned into two complementary subspaces by a **diagonal selection matrix** $S$ — one subspace is regulated by a **position servo** (PI controller tracking a desired position $p_d$), and the other is regulated by a **force servo** (PI controller tracking a desired contact force $f_d$). The two branches operate in parallel, and their outputs are combined in the task space and projected to joint space via the Jacobian transpose $J^T$.

> It is **necessary for contact tasks** (e.g., deburring, polishing, assembly) because:
> 1. **Pure position control** saturates joint torques and damages the workpiece when the end-effector is in rigid contact with the environment.
> 2. **Pure force control** causes drift and instability in unconstrained (free) directions.
> 3. Hybrid control resolves this by **controlling position where the environment imposes no constraint and controlling force where the environment constrains motion** — the natural complementarity of the two quantities in mechanical contact.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between lead-through programming and teach-pendant programming. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Aspect | Lead-Through | Teach Pendant |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **How path is taught** | Operator physically grabs the arm and moves it along the path | Operator uses a handheld keypad to jog each joint to discrete poses |
> | **Storage** | Joint angles sampled at a fixed rate (e.g., 60 Hz) into a point buffer | Discrete Cartesian or joint poses stored as numbered points |
> | **Best for** | Continuous free-form paths (painting, welding seams) | High-precision line / arc moves (assembly, pick-and-place) |
> | **Sensor feedback** | None at teach time | Limited; conditional branch instructions possible |
> | **Era / use** | Older 1970s–80s cells, simple paint robots | Modern universal industrial use (FANUC, ABB, KUKA) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions in Module 4 carry **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Module internal choice means students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Below we present both alternatives.

---

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Raibert–Craig hybrid position/force control architecture**. Define the selection matrix and show how it decouples position and force control. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** A 6-DOF robot performs a **door-opening task**. The door's hinge axis is aligned with the $Y$-axis of the compliance frame $\{C\}$. The robot's end-effector must apply a pulling force of **20 N** along the $-Z$ direction (perpendicular to the door) and move along an arc in the $X$–$Y$ plane. **(i)** Construct the selection matrix $S$. **(ii)** Identify which axes are naturally position-constrained and which are naturally force-constrained. **(iii)** Write down the Raibert–Craig control torque expression for this task. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Step 1 — Architecture overview.** The Raibert–Craig controller has **two parallel PI loops** in the task (compliance) frame $\{C\}$ — one for position, one for force. Each loop produces a task-space correction. The two corrections are summed and mapped to joint torques by the **Jacobian transpose** $J^T(q)$. The inner loop is a conventional joint servo.

**Step 2 — The position loop.** The position error is $e_p = p_d - p$. The position PI controller produces a corrective motion in $\{C\}$:

$$\Delta p = K_{pp}\,e_p + K_{pi} \int e_p\,dt$$

**Step 3 — The force loop.** The force error is $e_f = f_d - f$. The force PI controller produces a corrective action via admittance gains:

$$\Delta f_{\text{adm}} = K_{fp}\,e_f + K_{fi}\int e_f\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Selection matrix and decoupling.** The $6 \times 6$ diagonal matrix

$$S = \text{diag}(s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5, s_6), \quad s_i \in \{0,1\}$$

multiplies the position correction. Its complement $\bar{S} = I - S$ multiplies the force correction. Because $S\bar{S} = 0$, the two corrections act on **disjoint subspaces** and cannot fight each other on the same axis — this is the **decoupling property**.

**Step 5 — Full control law.**

$$\tau = J^T(q) \left[ S\,K_{pp}\,e_p + S\,K_{pi}\int e_p\,dt + \bar{S}\,K_{fp}\,e_f + \bar{S}\,K_{fi}\int e_f\,dt \right]$$

**Step 6 — Block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram).** The block diagram shows the parallel PI loops, selection matrices, sum node, Jacobian transpose, and the inner joint servo feeding the 6-DOF manipulator.

*(Marking: 2 marks for the law, 2 marks for selection matrix and complementarity, 2 marks for the block diagram, 1 mark for the two-loop structure description. Total 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Step 1 — Identify axes.** The compliance frame $\{C\}$ has the $Y$-axis along the door hinge.

| Axis | Role | Natural Position | Natural Force |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $X$ | Free, traces the arc | Free | Force = 0 |
| $Y$ | Along the hinge | Free | Force = 0 |
| $Z$ | Perpendicular to door | Constrained (= 0) | Force free |
| $\theta_X$ | Tool roll (free) | Free | Moment = 0 |
| $\theta_Y$ | Twist about hinge | Free | Moment = 0 |
| $\theta_Z$ | Yaw (free) | Free | Moment = 0 |

**Step 2 — Construct the selection matrix.** Position-controlled axes (free) get $s_i = 1$; force-controlled axes (constrained) get $s_i = 0$. Only $Z$ is force-controlled.

$$S = \text{diag}(1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1)$$

The complement is:

$$\bar{S} = I - S = \text{diag}(0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0)$$

**Step 3 — Desired force vector.** The user specifies $f_d = [0, 0, -20, 0, 0, 0]^T$ N (pulling toward the robot, in $-Z$).

**Step 4 — Raibert–Craig control law for this task.**

$$\tau = J^T(q) \left[ \text{diag}(1,1,0,1,1,1)\,K_{pp}\,(p_d - p) + \text{diag}(0,0,1,0,0,0)\,K_{fp}\,(f_d - f) + \text{integral terms} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.** The robot traces the door's arc in the $X$–$Y$ plane (position control) while maintaining a $-20$ N pull (force control along $Z$). The orientation is held fixed (position control of all three rotations). The selection matrix guarantees that no single axis receives conflicting commands.

*(Marking: 2 marks selection matrix, 2 marks natural-constraint identification, 3 marks control law and substitution. Total 7 marks.)*

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q.B (a)** Explain **Mason's natural and artificial constraints** for hybrid control. Construct the constraint table for a **peg-in-hole assembly task** in which the peg's axis is aligned with the $Z$-axis of $\{C\}$. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Discuss the various methods of **robot programming**. Compare **lead-through**, **teach-pendant**, and **offline programming** with industrial examples. List any two **robot programming languages** and their salient features. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Step 1 — Mason's framework.** For any contact task, Mason defined a **compliance frame** $\{C\}$ attached to the task. The environment imposes **natural constraints** (independent of the controller), and the user specifies **artificial constraints** (through the controller) to fully determine the task. Constraints are expressed as either **position** or **force** along each of the six task-space axes.

**Step 2 — Natural constraints (environment-imposed).** For a peg-in-hole along $Z$:
- Along $X$, $Y$: position is free, force = 0 (no contact surface).
- Along $Z$: position is constrained to 0 (rigid contact), force is free.
- About $\theta_X$, $\theta_Y$: angle is free, moment = 0.
- About $\theta_Z$: angle is constrained to 0, moment is free.

**Step 3 — Artificial constraints (user-set).** The user defines:
- $p_d^{(X)}$, $p_d^{(Y)}$ — search pattern (spiral / Lissajous).
- $p_d^{(\theta_X)}$, $p_d^{(\theta_Y)}$ — alignment of peg.
- $f_d^{(Z)} = +F_{\text{insert}}$ — insertion force.
- $m_d^{(\theta_Z)} = 0$ — no twisting moment.

**Step 4 — Constraint table.**

| Axis | Natural Position | Natural Force | Artificial Position | Artificial Force |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $X$ | Free | $= 0$ | $p_d^{(X)}$ set by user | — |
| $Y$ | Free | $= 0$ | $p_d^{(Y)}$ set by user | — |
| $Z$ | $= 0$ | Free | — | $f_d^{(Z)} = F_{\text{insert}}$ |
| $\theta_X$ | Free | $= 0$ | $p_d^{(\theta_X)}$ set | — |
| $\theta_Y$ | Free | $= 0$ | $p_d^{(\theta_Y)}$ set | — |
| $\theta_Z$ | $= 0$ | Free | — | $m_d^{(\theta_Z)} = 0$ |

**Step 5 — Selection matrix.** $S = \text{diag}(1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0)$.

*(Marking: 2 marks for Mason's framework, 2 marks for natural constraints identification, 2 marks for artificial constraints, 1 mark for the table. Total 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Step 1 — Overview.** Robot programming methods span a hierarchy from low-level joint motion specification to high-level task-level declarations. The three principal industrial methods are:

- **Lead-through programming** — operator physically guides the arm.
- **Teach-pendant programming** — operator uses a handheld keypad to record poses.
- **Offline programming (OLP)** — programs are written in a 3D simulation environment on a PC.

**Step 2 — Lead-through.** The operator grabs the end-effector (or a handle) and traces the desired path. Joint positions are sampled by the controller at a fixed rate (typically **30–60 Hz**). On playback, the controller interpolates the joint trajectory. **Example:** Original Unimate robots on General Motors welding lines (1960s–70s). **Best for:** Continuous free-form paths (paint, glue, weld seams). **Drawback:** No sensor feedback at teach time, tedious for precision.

**Step 3 — Teach pendant.** The operator uses a **handheld pendant** with jog keys and a joystick. Discrete poses are recorded as numbered points. Motion primitives include `MOVE/PTP` (joint-interpolated), `LIN` (Cartesian linear), `CIRC` (arc through three points), and `SPLINE` (smooth blend). **Example:** KUKA smartPAD on KR AGILUS, ABB FlexPendant on IRB series. **Best for:** Pick-and-place, simple line/arc moves, high precision.

**Step 4 — Offline programming.** Programs are authored in a 3D simulation environment (**ABB RobotStudio, KUKA.Sim, FANUC ROBOGUIDE, RoboDK**) using **CAD models** of the cell. The resulting program is downloaded to the controller. **Example:** Automotive body-in-white welding cells (BMW Spartanburg, Tesla Gigafactory). **Best for:** Complex paths, large cells, no production downtime, parametric / scalable programs.

**Step 5 — Two robot programming languages.**

**(i) VAL (Unimation, 1979):** The first commercial robot language. Commands include `MOVE destination`, `HERE` (store current pose), `WHERE` (query pose), and `SPEED ratio` (0.1 to 1.0). Variable speed, conditional branching, and simple I/O were supported. It was the de-facto language of PUMA and Unimate robots.

**(ii) AL (Stanford AI Lab, 1974):** A research language introducing the **affixment** abstraction (tool-to-gripper attachment) and explicit **force/motion commands**, e.g., `MOVE barm TO goal` and `MOVE barm TO goal WITH FORCE = f`. AL is the **theoretical ancestor** of all hybrid-control-aware languages; its syntax directly inspired Raibert–Craig's hybrid architecture.

**(iii) [Bonus] AML (IBM, 1979):** A powerful commercial language with rich data structures, algebraic $A$-notation for expressions, and well-defined sensor variables. Used on IBM's RS-1 and Sankyo robots.

**Step 6 — Conclusion.** Modern industrial practice favors a **combination** of teach-pendant (for fine calibration) and offline programming (for production programs). PbD and task-level programming are emerging research directions, but lead-through, teach-pendant, and OLP remain the workhorses of 2024 industry.

*(Marking: 2 marks lead-through, 2 marks teach-pendant, 2 marks OLP, 1 mark for each of two languages. Total 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 4)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the complement matrix.** Many students write $S$ but forget that force control uses $\bar{S} = I - S$. Without this, the position and force branches are not properly decoupled, and the answer loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $J^T$ with $J^{-1}$.** $J^T$ maps **task-space force to joint torque** (static equivalence via virtual work). $J^{-1}$ maps **task-space velocity to joint velocity** (kinematic). Examiners deduct 1 mark for mixing these up.
> 3. **Skipping the integral terms.** The full hybrid control law has **both P and I** terms in each branch. Writing only the P term loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Programming-methods question should have examples.** A 7-mark Part B on programming methods **without** an industrial example is treated as incomplete. Always cite at least one real system (KUKA, FANUC, ABB, etc.).
> 5. **Mixing VAL and AL.** VAL is the **Unimation** commercial language; AL is the **Stanford AI Lab** research language. Conflating the two origins is a common factual error costing 1 mark.
> 6. **Diagonal-only selection matrix.** A common mistake is to use a non-diagonal $S$. The original Raibert–Craig architecture **requires** $S$ to be diagonal, otherwise the position and force subspaces are coupled and the design decouples. Use only diagonal entries.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before the Exam**

### Hybrid Position/Force Control

- ☐ **Hybrid control** = position control on free axes + force control on constrained axes, in parallel.
- ☐ Originator: **Raibert & Craig (1981)**; foundation: **Mason's compliance frame (1981)**.
- ☐ **Selection matrix** $S = \text{diag}(s_1, \ldots, s_6)$, $s_i \in \{0, 1\}$; complement $\bar{S} = I - S$.
- ☐ **Convention:** $s_i = 1$ → position; $s_i = 0$ → force.
- ☐ **Diagonal-only** $S$ for guaranteed decoupling.
- ☐ Full control law: $\tau = J^T [S K_{pp} e_p + S K_{pi} \int e_p + \bar{S} K_{fp} e_f + \bar{S} K_{fi} \int e_f]$.
- ☐ **Jacobian transpose** ($J^T$, not $J^{-1}$) for force-to-torque mapping (virtual work).
- ☐ **Natural constraints** come from the environment; **artificial constraints** come from the user.
- ☐ Constraint table is a $2 \times 6$ (or $4 \times 6$) structure per axis: position-free ↔ force-=0; position-=0 ↔ force-free.
- ☐ Applications: deburring, polishing, peg-in-hole, door opening, wiping.
- ☐ Selection matrix for a flat-surface deburring task: $S = \text{diag}(1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1)$.
- ☐ Selection matrix for peg-in-hole along $Z$: $S = \text{diag}(1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0)$.
- ☐ All four gains $K_{pp}, K_{pi}, K_{fp}, K_{fi}$ must be **strictly positive diagonal** for stability.

### Robot Programming

- ☐ **Lead-through** = grab arm, walk through path; sample at 30–60 Hz.
- ☐ **Teach pendant** = jog keys + record poses; commands: `MOVE`, `LIN`, `CIRC`, `SPLINE`.
- ☐ **Offline (OLP)** = 3D simulation on PC; no downtime; e.g., RobotStudio, KUKA.Sim, RoboDK.
- ☐ **Task-level** = declare goal, not motion (emerging).
- ☐ **PbD** = robot infers from human demonstration (research).
- ☐ **VAL** = Unimation 1979; commands `MOVE`, `HERE`, `WHERE`, `SPEED`.
- ☐ **AL** = Stanford 1974; introduced affixments and force/motion syntax.
- ☐ **AML** = IBM 1979; rich data structures and $A$-notation.
- ☐ Modern industrial languages: **RAPID** (ABB), **KRL** (KUKA), **KAREL** (FANUC), **URScript** (UR).
- ☐ Programming hierarchy: joint → lead-through → teach pendant → textual → OLP → task-level → PbD.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- ☐ Don't use $J^{-1}$ for force mapping.
- ☐ Don't omit the integral term in PI controllers.
- ☐ Don't forget $\bar{S}$ when describing the force branch.
- ☐ Don't confuse VAL and AL origins.
- ☐ Don't give OLP answer without a real industrial example.
- ☐ Don't draw a non-diagonal selection matrix.
- ☐ Don't forget the units: $e_p$ in m/rad, $e_f$ in N/N·m, $\tau$ in N·m.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scope for Mobile Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY AND ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scope for Mobile Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Mobile Robot** is defined as an automatic machine that is capable of locomotion across a given environment, equipped with an onboard power source, sensing system, control architecture, and decision-making intelligence, enabling it to perform assigned tasks without being physically constrained to a fixed base.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)** syllabus framework, the *Scope for Mobile Robots* encompasses the spectrum of environments in which mobile robots operate, the application domains they serve, the mechanical configurations they adopt, and the technological trajectory governing their future development.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The scope of mobile robots is not limited to a single industry. It spans structured industrial floors, unstructured disaster zones, aquatic depths, aerial altitudes, planetary surfaces, and human-proximate domestic spaces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Swiss Army Knife on wheels** — but instead of blades and screwdrivers, it carries cameras, LIDAR sensors, GPS modules, and microprocessors. A mobile robot is essentially a *thinking machine that can physically move through the world* and adapt to it.

Think of a *delivery boy on a bicycle* navigating through traffic using maps, eyes, and judgment. A mobile robot performs the same task, but replaces the human with:
- **Wheels/legs/propellers** for locomotion (instead of legs/tyres)
- **Sensors** (LIDAR, cameras, ultrasonic) instead of eyes
- **Algorithms (SLAM, path planning)** instead of human judgment
- **Microcontrollers/Processors** instead of the brain

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** *Mobility + Perception + Autonomy = Mobile Robot.* Remove any one of these three pillars, and the system collapses into either a stationary manipulator, a remote-controlled toy, or a sensor package.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Mobile Robotics

The following standardized parameters are universally used to benchmark mobile robots:

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent movements a robot can perform.
- **Payload Capacity:** Measured in **kilograms (kg)** or **newtons (N)**.
- **Operational Range:** Distance covered per charge cycle, in **meters (m)** or **kilometers (km)**.
- **Degrees of Autonomy (DoA):** Classified into **6 levels (0–5)** as per the SAE J3016 inspired framework.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Typically rated in **hours (h)** for industrial units.
- **Localization Accuracy:** Measured in **millimeters (mm)** for indoor and **meters (m)** for outdoor GPS-based systems.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Mobile Robot Scope

| Pillar | Engineering Domain | Representative Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Locomotion** | Mechanical Engineering | Wheels, tracks, legs, rotors, thrusters |
| **Perception** | Electronics & Computer Science | LIDAR, IMU, cameras, GPS |
| **Decision Intelligence** | AI & Control Systems | SLAM, path planning, neural networks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Mobile Robots by Environment

The scope of mobile robots is best understood by categorizing them according to the **operational medium** they navigate. Each environment imposes unique mechanical, sensory, and computational demands.

#### 2.1.1 Ground (Terrestrial) Mobile Robots
These operate on solid surfaces. They are further divided into:

- **Wheeled Mobile Robots (WMR):** The most common configuration. Kinematic models use the **differential drive** or **Ackermann steering** principle. Highly efficient on flat, structured surfaces such as factory floors and warehouses.
- **Tracked Mobile Robots:** Use continuous tracks (similar to tanks) for high traction on rough, uneven, or soft terrain. Used in military and disaster response.
- **Legged Mobile Robots:** Mimic biological locomotion. Walking gaits are classified as **static walking** (always stable) and **dynamic walking** (uses momentum). Examples include quadrupeds (four legs) and hexapods (six legs).
- **Hybrid Robots:** Combine wheels and legs for adaptability.

#### 2.1.2 Aerial Mobile Robots (UAVs / Drones)
- **Multi-rotor Drones:** Quadcopters, hexacopters, octocopters — offer vertical takeoff and landing (VTOL).
- **Fixed-wing UAVs:** Higher endurance and range, but require runways or launch systems.
- **Hybrid VTOL:** Combine hovering capability with efficient forward flight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Aerodynamic Principle:** Lift force $L = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 S C_L$ where $\rho$ is air density, $v$ is velocity, $S$ is wing area, and $C_L$ is the lift coefficient. This is the foundational equation for aerial robot design.

#### 2.1.3 Aquatic Mobile Robots
- **Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs):** Operate submerged, used for oceanographic surveys, pipeline inspection, and naval reconnaissance.
- **Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs):** Tethered to a surface vessel, used in deep-sea exploration.
- **Autonomous Surface Vehicles (ASVs):** Operate on water surfaces for hydrographic mapping and environmental monitoring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buoyancy Principle:** An aquatic robot achieves neutral buoyancy when $F_{buoyancy} = \rho_{water} \cdot V \cdot g = m_{robot} \cdot g$. This equilibrium is critical for energy-efficient AUV operation.

#### 2.1.4 Space Mobile Robots
- **Planetary Rovers:** NASA's *Perseverance* and *Curiosity* are landmark examples operating on Mars.
- **Free-Flying Robots:** Operate in microgravity aboard the International Space Station (ISS), e.g., the *Astrobee* robot.
- **Landing/Exploration Probes:** Combine mobility with scientific instrumentation.

### 2.2 Classification by Application Domain

The **scope of mobile robots** extends across virtually every modern industry. Below is a structured taxonomy aligned with KTU 2024 expectations:

#### 2.2.1 Industrial Scope
- **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs):** Transport materials in warehouses and manufacturing plants. Operate along magnetic strips, wires, or vision-based markers.
- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs):** A more advanced evolution of AGVs that dynamically map and navigate environments using SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping).
- **Last-Mile Delivery Robots:** Used by companies like Amazon (Scout) and Starship Technologies for urban parcel delivery.

#### 2.2.2 Medical and Healthcare Scope
- **Surgical Robots:** Da Vinci system (teleoperated, semi-mobile).
- **Hospital Service Robots:** Transport medications, meals, and supplies within hospitals.
- **Rehabilitation Robots:** Exoskeletons that assist patient mobility (e.g., ReWalk).
- **Disinfection Robots:** UV-C light emitting units deployed in hospitals post-COVID.

#### 2.2.3 Defense and Security Scope
- **Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs):** For bomb disposal (e.g., DRDO Daksh), reconnaissance, and combat support.
- **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs):** For surveillance, target acquisition, and tactical strikes.
- **Unmanned Underwater Vehicles (UUVs):** For mine detection and harbor protection.

#### 2.2.4 Agriculture Scope
- **Autonomous Tractors and Harvesters:** GPS-guided field operations.
- **Agricultural Drones:** Crop monitoring, pesticide spraying, soil analysis.
- **Precision Weeding Robots:** e.g., *John Deere's* autonomous solutions and *Small Robot Company*.

#### 2.2.5 Domestic and Service Scope
- **Vacuum Cleaning Robots:** iRobot Roomba — one of the most commercially successful mobile robots globally.
- **Lawn Mowing Robots:** Husqvarna Automower.
- **Companion/Elderly Care Robots:** Assist in social interaction and health monitoring.
- **Hospitality Robots:** Used in hotels for room service (e.g., Henn-na Hotel, Japan).

#### 2.2.6 Disaster Response and Hazardous Environments
- **Search and Rescue (SAR) Robots:** Navigate collapsed buildings and rubble.
- **Nuclear Decommissioning Robots:** Used in Fukushima Daiichi cleanup.
- **Firefighting Robots:** e.g., *Tafbot* and various US Navy shipboard units.
- **Mining Robots:** Autonomous haul trucks in open-pit mines (e.g., Komatsu FrontRunner).

#### 2.2.7 Space Exploration Scope
- **Planetary Rovers:** Perseverance, Zhurong (China), Pragyan (India - Chandrayaan-3).
- **In-orbit Servicing Robots:** Satellite refueling and repair.
- **Asteroid Mining Concepts:** Future scope for resource extraction.

### 2.3 Key Engineering Challenges in Mobile Robot Scope

The expansion of mobile robot scope is constrained by several technical challenges that students must understand for examination purposes:

1. **Localization and Mapping:** The robot must answer *"Where am I?"* — addressed through SLAM algorithms.
2. **Path Planning:** Computing an optimal, collision-free trajectory from start to goal.
3. **Energy Management:** Battery technology limits operational endurance; typical Li-ion energy density is **250 Wh/kg**.
4. **Obstacle Avoidance:** Real-time reaction to dynamic and static obstacles.
5. **Communication Latency:** Critical for teleoperated and swarm systems.
6. **Environmental Robustness:** Operating in dust, water, extreme temperatures, and radiation.
7. **Ethical and Legal Frameworks:** Regulatory acceptance in public spaces.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Differential Drive Kinematics | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | Wheeled robot linear velocity |
| 2 | Differential Drive Angular Velocity | $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | WMR turning rate |
| 3 | Ackermann Steering Geometry | $\cot(\delta_o) - \cot(\delta_i) = \frac{W}{L}$ | Car-like robot steering |
| 4 | Aerodynamic Lift | $L = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 S C_L$ | UAV design |
| 5 | Buoyant Force | $F_B = \rho V g$ | AUV floatation |
| 6 | Battery Endurance | $t = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{total}}$ | Operating time estimation |
| 7 | SLAM Uncertainty Bound | $\sigma_{pos} \propto \sqrt{d}$ | Localization drift |
| 8 | Static Walking Stability | CoG projection lies within support polygon | Legged robot gait |
| 9 | Tracked Robot Traction | $F_{tract} = \mu W$ | Off-road mobility |
| 10 | Degrees of Autonomy (DoA) | $DoA \in [0, 5]$ | Autonomy classification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Symbols:** In the table above, $r$ = wheel radius, $\omega_R, \omega_L$ = right/left wheel angular velocities, $L$ = wheelbase, $W$ = track width, $\delta_o, \delta_i$ = outer/inner steering angles, $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $E_{bat}$ = battery energy in joules, $P_{total}$ = total power consumption in watts.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The scope of mobile robots directly impacts:
- **Manufacturing 4.0 and Smart Factories:** AMRs replace conveyor belts with flexible material handling.
- **Smart Cities:** Autonomous vehicles, delivery robots, and surveillance drones form the urban mobility fabric.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Reduces chemical usage by up to **90%** through targeted spraying.
- **Healthcare Delivery:** Hospital logistics robots reduce nurse workload by approximately **30%**.
- **Planetary Science:** Mobile robots are the only viable means of in-situ exploration of other celestial bodies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Differential Drive Kinematic Model

The differential drive is the **most common wheeled mobile robot configuration** and is a high-yield topic for KTU examinations. The derivation proceeds as follows:

**Given:** A two-wheeled robot with wheels of radius $r$ separated by axle length $L$. The right and left wheels rotate with angular velocities $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$ respectively.

**Step 1: Linear velocity of each wheel**

The linear velocity at the contact point of each wheel with the ground is given by the standard rolling relation:

$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$$
$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L$$

**Step 2: Linear velocity of the robot center**

The robot's center moves at the average of the two wheel velocities, since the center lies on the axle midway between the wheels:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

**Step 3: Angular velocity of the robot**

The robot rotates about a point (Instantaneous Center of Curvature, ICC) when the wheels turn at different speeds. The angular velocity of the robot is determined by the difference in wheel velocities divided by the axle length:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

**Step 4: Special Case Analysis**

- **Case 1: Straight Line Motion:** $\omega_R = \omega_L \Rightarrow \omega = 0$, robot moves straight.
- **Case 2: In-Place Rotation:** $\omega_R = -\omega_L \Rightarrow v = 0$, robot rotates about its own center.
- **Case 3: Curved Path:** Any other combination produces a circular arc with radius:

$$R_{ICC} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$$

**Step 5: Complete Kinematic State Vector**

The robot's motion in the global frame $(x, y, \theta)$ is governed by:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos(\theta) \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin(\theta) \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **forward kinematic model** of a differential drive mobile robot — a mandatory topic for KTU 2024 examinations.

### 3.2 Derivation: Ackermann Steering Geometry

The Ackermann steering principle ensures that all wheels of a car-like robot follow concentric circles about a common ICC, eliminating tire slip.

**Given:** A four-wheeled vehicle with wheelbase $L$ (distance between front and rear axles) and track width $W$ (distance between left and right wheels).

**Step 1: Geometric Constraint**

For the inner and outer front wheels to have the same ICC, the following condition must hold:

$$\cot(\delta_i) = \frac{R_{ICC} - \frac{W}{2}}{L}$$
$$\cot(\delta_o) = \frac{R_{ICC} + \frac{W}{2}}{L}$$

**Step 2: Subtracting the Two Equations**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cot(\delta_o) - \cot(\delta_i) &= \frac{R_{ICC} + \frac{W}{2}}{L} - \frac{R_{ICC} - \frac{W}{2}}{L} \\
&= \frac{W}{L}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Ackermann condition**:

$$\cot(\delta_o) - \cot(\delta_i) = \frac{W}{L}$$

**Step 3: Practical Implication**

The inner wheel must turn at a **larger angle** than the outer wheel. In practice, this is achieved by a trapezoidal steering linkage.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Locomotion Systems

The following matrix provides a KTU-ready analytical comparison of mobile robot locomotion systems:

| Criterion | Wheeled | Tracked | Legged | Aerial | Aquatic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Efficiency** | High | Medium | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Terrain Adaptability** | Low | High | Very High | N/A (Aerial) | N/A (Aquatic) |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | High | High |
| **Speed (typical)** | 1–10 m/s | 0.5–3 m/s | 0.3–1.5 m/s | 5–30 m/s | 0.5–5 m/s |
| **Payload Capacity** | High | Very High | Low | Low | Medium |
| **Control Complexity** | Low | Medium | Very High | High | High |
| **Outdoor Suitability** | Limited | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Indoor Suitability** | Excellent | Limited | Limited | Limited | Not applicable |
| **Cost (relative)** | Low | Medium | High | High | High |
| **Example Systems** | AGV, AMR | PackBot, Daksh | Spot, ASIMO | Quadcopter, Predator | AUV, ROV |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Examiners frequently ask for a comparison table in 14-mark questions. Memorize the trade-offs between locomotion types — it is a guaranteed high-scoring response.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Mobile Robot Operational Pipeline

The following diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a generic autonomous mobile robot, showing the interaction between sensing, perception, decision-making, and actuation subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environmental Inputs] --> B[Sensor Suite]
    B --> C[Perception Module]
    C --> D[SLAM Engine]
    D --> E[World Map Database]
    C --> F[Object Detection]
    F --> G[Obstacle Registry]
    E --> H[Path Planning Engine]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Motion Controller]
    I --> J[Actuator Drivers]
    J --> K[Wheel Motors / Rotor / Thruster]
    K --> L[Physical Movement]
    L --> M[Updated Environment]
    M --> B
    H --> N[Mission Goal]
    N --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The cycle demonstrates the closed-loop feedback nature of autonomous mobile robots. The sensor suite continuously perceives the environment, the SLAM engine updates the world map, the path planner computes trajectories, and the actuators execute motion — with the new environment state fed back into sensors.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mobile Robot Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PWR[Power Subsystem]
        P1[Battery Pack]
        P2[Power Distribution Board]
        P3[Voltage Regulators]
        P1 --> P2 --> P3
    end

    subgraph SEN[Sensing Subsystem]
        S1[LIDAR Sensor]
        S2[Cameras and IMU]
        S3[GPS Module]
        S4[Ultrasonic Array]
    end

    subgraph CNS[Central Computing]
        C1[Onboard Processor]
        C2[SLAM Algorithm]
        C3[Path Planning Module]
        C4[Behavior Controller]
        C1 --> C2
        C1 --> C3
        C1 --> C4
    end

    subgraph ACT[Actuation Subsystem]
        M1[Motor Controllers]
        M2[Drive Motors]
        M3[Steering Actuator]
        M1 --> M2
        M1 --> M3
    end

    subgraph COM[Communication]
        T1[Wireless Transceiver]
        T2[Teleoperation Link]
        T1 --> T2
    end

    P3 --> C1
    SEN --> C1
    C4 --> M1
    C4 --> T1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The four subsystems — **Power, Sensing, Computing, and Actuation** — along with the **Communication** link, form the canonical architecture of any autonomous mobile robot. The Power subsystem is often the most limiting factor in practical deployment.

### 4.3 Mermaid Tree Diagram: Scope Classification of Mobile Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Mobile Robots] --> G[Ground Robots]
    ROOT --> A[Aerial Robots]
    ROOT --> AQ[Aquatic Robots]
    ROOT --> S[Space Robots]

    G --> G1[Wheeled - AGV and AMR]
    G --> G2[Tracked - Military UGV]
    G --> G3[Legged - Quadruped and Hexapod]
    G --> G4[Hybrid - Wheel Leg]

    A --> A1[Multirotor - Quadcopter]
    A --> A2[Fixed Wing - Surveillance UAV]
    A --> A3[Hybrid VTOL]

    AQ --> AQ1[AUV - Underwater Survey]
    AQ --> AQ2[ROV - Tethered Deep Sea]
    AQ --> AQ3[ASV - Surface Vessel]

    S --> S1[Planetary Rover]
    S --> S2[Free Flyer - ISS Astrobee]
    S --> S3[Lander Probe]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Mobile Robot Mission Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator
    participant RC as Robot Controller
    participant SL as SLAM Engine
    participant PP as Path Planner
    participant MC as Motion Controller
    participant ENV as Environment

    OP->>RC: Issue Mission Goal
    RC->>SL: Initialize Localization
    SL-->>RC: Initial Pose Estimate
    RC->>PP: Request Path to Goal
    PP->>SL: Query Current Map
    SL-->>PP: Map and Pose Data
    PP-->>RC: Planned Trajectory
    RC->>MC: Send Velocity Commands
    MC->>ENV: Execute Motion
    ENV-->>SL: Sensor Feedback Updates
    SL-->>PP: Updated Map and Pose
    PP-->>MC: Replanned Trajectory if Needed
    MC->>ENV: Continue Motion
    MC-->>OP: Mission Status Report
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Value:** This sequence diagram is a frequent KTU 14-mark question style. Students are often asked to *draw a block diagram of an autonomous mobile robot system* — the above serves as a complete template.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
*Define the term "Mobile Robot" and list any four types of mobile robots based on their operational environment.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Mobile Robot** is an autonomous or semi-autonomous machine capable of moving through its environment using onboard propulsion and sensing systems, without being restricted to a fixed location.

The four types of mobile robots based on operational environment are:

1. **Ground Mobile Robots** — operate on solid surfaces (wheeled, tracked, legged).
2. **Aerial Mobile Robots (UAVs)** — operate in the atmospheric airspace.
3. **Aquatic Mobile Robots** — operate in water (AUVs, ROVs, ASVs).
4. **Space Mobile Robots** — operate in extraterrestrial environments or in orbit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing and briefly explaining the types.

#### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
*Differentiate between an AGV and an AMR with a suitable example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) | AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot) |
|---|---|---|
| Navigation | Follows fixed paths (magnetic strips, wires, QR codes) | Uses SLAM and dynamic path planning |
| Flexibility | Low — requires infrastructure changes | High — adapts to environment changes |
| Intelligence | Limited onboard decision-making | Advanced AI-based perception and planning |
| Example | Factory tow vehicle in automotive assembly | Amazon Kiva robots in fulfillment centers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme (3 marks):** 1 mark for AGV, 1 mark for AMR, 1 mark for example and differentiation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer **one full question** (both sub-parts) from the internal choice pair.

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various types of mobile robots based on locomotion. Compare wheeled, tracked, and legged locomotion with respect to terrain adaptability, energy efficiency, and mechanical complexity.

**(b) [7 Marks)** With a neat block diagram, describe the major subsystems of an autonomous mobile robot. Explain the role of the SLAM engine in mobile robot navigation.

##### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Mobile robots achieve locomotion through various mechanisms depending on the terrain and task. The three primary ground locomotion types are wheeled, tracked, and legged.

**Wheeled Locomotion (2 marks):**
Wheeled mobile robots are the most widely used due to their **simplicity, efficiency, and reliability**. Common configurations include differential drive, Ackermann steering, and omnidirectional (Mecanum or Swedish) wheels. They excel on flat, hard surfaces such as factory floors and paved roads. However, their performance degrades significantly on rough, uneven, or soft terrain.

**Tracked Locomotion (2 marks):**
Tracked robots use continuous belt tracks, distributing the robot's weight over a large surface area. This provides **high traction and stability** on rough terrain, sand, mud, and rubble. Examples include military UGVs like the DRDO Daksh. The disadvantages include higher energy consumption and mechanical wear.

**Legged Locomotion (1.5 marks):**
Legged robots mimic biological systems, using discrete contact points with the ground. They offer **superior terrain adaptability** — capable of stepping over obstacles, climbing stairs, and traversing irregular surfaces. However, they are mechanically complex, require sophisticated gait control algorithms, and consume more energy. Examples include Boston Dynamics' Spot (quadruped) and Cassie (bipedal).

**Comparison (0.5 mark):** A summary table as in Section 3.3 above.

##### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2. The major subsystems are:

1. **Power Subsystem:** Battery pack, power distribution board, voltage regulators.
2. **Sensing Subsystem:** LIDAR, cameras, IMU, GPS, ultrasonic sensors.
3. **Central Computing Subsystem:** Onboard processor running SLAM, path planning, and behavior control.
4. **Actuation Subsystem:** Motor controllers, drive motors, and steering actuators.
5. **Communication Subsystem:** Wireless transceiver for teleoperation and data transfer.

**SLAM Engine Role (4 marks):**

**SLAM** stands for **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping**. It addresses two fundamental problems simultaneously:

- **Localization:** *Where is the robot right now?*
- **Mapping:** *What does the surrounding environment look like?*

The SLAM algorithm uses sensor data (typically LIDAR point clouds and wheel odometry) to:
1. Build a **2D or 3D occupancy grid map** of the environment in real-time.
2. Estimate the robot's **pose (x, y, θ)** within that map.
3. **Correct odometry drift** using sensor loop closures — recognizing previously visited locations.

**Mathematical Foundation:** SLAM uses probabilistic frameworks such as the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF-SLAM)** or **particle filter-based approaches (FastSLAM)**. The robot's pose uncertainty is modeled as a Gaussian distribution, and landmark observations are fused using Bayesian inference.

**Engineering Significance:** Without SLAM, a mobile robot cannot operate autonomously in an unknown environment. SLAM is the *perceptual backbone* of modern mobile robotics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SLAM with simple GPS navigation:** SLAM works indoors where GPS is unavailable.
> 2. **Forgetting the loop closure concept:** Many students omit this critical mechanism.
> 3. **Omitting the block diagram in part (b):** The diagram carries **at least 2–3 marks** in the marking scheme.
> 4. **Writing only definitions without engineering examples:** KTU examiners expect real-world system references.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the various application domains of mobile robots. Give at least two specific examples from each domain.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the forward kinematic equations of a differential drive mobile robot. Explain the two special cases of motion.

##### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Application Domains (7 marks, approximately 1 mark per major domain):**

1. **Industrial Applications:** AGVs transport materials in automotive assembly lines; AMRs like Amazon Kiva robots move inventory shelves in warehouses.
2. **Medical Applications:** Da Vinci surgical system (semi-mobile teleoperated robot); hospital service robots delivering medications.
3. **Defense Applications:** DRDO Daksh for bomb disposal; MQ-9 Reaper UAV for surveillance and strike missions.
4. **Agricultural Applications:** John Deere autonomous tractors for plowing and harvesting; DJI Agras drones for pesticide spraying.
5. **Domestic Applications:** iRobot Roomba for vacuum cleaning; Husqvarna Automower for lawn mowing.
6. **Space Applications:** NASA's Perseverance rover for Mars exploration; ISRO's Pragyan rover (Chandrayaan-3) for lunar surface analysis.
7. **Disaster Response:** PackBot for search and rescue in collapsed buildings; underwater ROVs for flood rescue operations.

**Conclusion:** The scope of mobile robots continues to expand as sensor technology, AI algorithms, and battery technology advance.

##### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. Key points to include:

- Define the differential drive configuration (axle length $L$, wheel radius $r$).
- Derive $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$.
- Derive $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$.
- Present the complete state vector:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\cos(\theta) \\
\dot{y} &= \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}\sin(\theta) \\
\dot{\theta} &= \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
\end{aligned}$$

- **Special Case 1:** $\omega_R = \omega_L$ → straight line motion ($v \neq 0$, $\omega = 0$).
- **Special Case 2:** $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ → in-place rotation ($v = 0$, $\omega \neq 0$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Kinematics Derivation:**
> 1. **Skipping the geometric reasoning:** Students who jump directly to the final formula without showing why the center moves at the average velocity lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the global frame transformation:** The $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ projection onto the $x$ and $y$ axes is essential for full marks.
> 3. **Not stating units:** $\omega$ must be in **rad/s** and $v$ in **m/s**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Scope for Mobile Robots**

- ✅ A **mobile robot** is an autonomous machine capable of locomotion, perception, and decision-making without a fixed base.
- ✅ The **three pillars** of mobile robotics are **Locomotion, Perception, and Autonomy**.
- ✅ **Locomotion types:** Wheeled (efficient, structured), Tracked (rough terrain), Legged (highly adaptive, complex), Aerial (UAVs), Aquatic (AUVs/ROVs/ASVs), and Space (rovers).
- ✅ **AGV vs AMR:** AGVs follow fixed paths; AMRs use SLAM for dynamic navigation.
- ✅ **Differential Drive Equations:** $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ and $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$.
- ✅ **Ackermann Condition:** $\cot(\delta_o) - \cot(\delta_i) = \frac{W}{L}$ — required to avoid tire slip in car-like robots.
- ✅ **SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) is the perceptual backbone of autonomous mobile robots.
- ✅ **Application sectors:** Industrial, Medical, Defense, Agriculture, Domestic, Space, and Disaster Response.
- ✅ **Key challenges:** Localization, path planning, energy management, obstacle avoidance, and communication latency.
- ✅ **Future scope:** AI integration, swarm robotics, soft robotics, and human-robot collaboration (Cobots).
- ✅ **Standard autonomy levels:** SAE-inspired 0–5 scale; full autonomy (Level 5) remains an active research frontier.
- ✅ **Energy metric:** Typical Li-ion energy density is approximately **250 Wh/kg**.
- ✅ **Lift equation (UAVs):** $L = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 S C_L$.
- ✅ **Buoyancy equation (AUVs):** $F_B = \rho V g$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** For the 14-mark questions, always begin with a **brief introduction (2–3 lines)**, present a **diagram or table** wherever applicable, support with **real-world examples**, and conclude with a **1–2 line summary**. This structure aligns with the KTU 2024 marking scheme and consistently scores 12+ marks.
