# MODULE 4: AI/ML IN ROBOTICS & PROJECT TOPICS IN MECHANISM DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Need and Application of AI and ML in Robotics — Formal Definition

**Artificial Intelligence (AI)** in robotics refers to the simulation of human cognitive processes — perception, reasoning, learning, and decision-making — embedded within a robotic system so that it can execute tasks autonomously in dynamic, unstructured environments.

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a subset of AI that enables the robot to **learn patterns and improve task performance from data** without being explicitly programmed for every scenario. The principal ML paradigms applied to robotics are:

- **Supervised Learning** — learning from labelled input–output pairs.
- **Unsupervised Learning** — discovering latent structure in unlabelled data.
- **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** — learning an optimal control policy via a reward signal (central to robotic manipulation, walking, and grasping).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 4 emphasises *why* classical, model-based control is insufficient for modern robots, and *how* AI/ML augments perception, planning, and control. The project topics below are the prescribed framework for the semester's mini-project / design activity.

### 1.2 Why Robots Need AI/ML — The Core Motivation

Classical industrial robots are blind, deaf, and rigid:

- They depend on **fixed trajectories** and **pre-programmed positions**.
- They cannot adapt to **variability in part geometry, lighting, surface friction, or human presence**.
- They require **re-teaching** for every new product.

Modern applications — surgical robots, autonomous mobile robots (AMRs), collaborative robots (cobots), and humanoids — operate in **uncertain, partially observable, multi-modal environments** that cannot be captured by closed-form kinematics alone.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Robot as a Newborn Child"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a classical robot as a **trained factory worker** who has memorised a fixed drill — fast, accurate, but useless outside the factory. An AI/ML-enabled robot is like a **newborn child**: it perceives the world through sensors (eyes, ears, skin), learns by trial-and-error, gradually builds a model of cause-and-effect, and ultimately performs tasks it was never explicitly taught. Reinforcement learning is exactly the **"praise and scold"** mechanism a parent uses to train a toddler.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom | DOF | integer | Manipulator configuration |
| Pose repeatability | $\rho$ | $\pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.1$ mm | Industrial arms |
| Payload | $m_p$ | **3 – 1000 kg** | Material handling |
| Inference latency | $\tau_{inf}$ | **1 – 50 ms** | Real-time control |
| Mean Average Precision (detection) | mAP | 0.5 – 0.9 | Vision-based grasping |
| Reward discount factor (RL) | $\gamma$ | **0.9 – 0.99** | RL convergence |

### 1.5 Application Domains of AI/ML in Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Applications:**
> 1. **Computer Vision for Grasping** — CNNs (YOLO, ResNet) detect object pose.
> 2. **SLAM (Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping)** — used in AMRs; combines EKF with neural networks.
> 3. **Reinforcement Learning for Locomotion** — humanoid/quadruped walking policies.
> 4. **Imitation Learning** — Learning from Demonstration (LfD) for assembly.
> 5. **Predictive Maintenance** — LSTM networks on joint torque/vibration time-series.
> 6. **Natural Language Interaction** — LLMs integrated with ROS 2 for voice-controlled cobots.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Stack: How AI/ML Fits Inside a Robot

The modern intelligent robot is built as a layered architecture. Each layer consumes the output of the one below:

- **Layer 1 — Physical Hardware:** Actuators (servo motors, BLDC), reducers (harmonic drives, cycloidal), and sensors (encoders, IMU, force/torque, RGB-D cameras, LiDAR).
- **Layer 2 — Low-Level Control:** PID / field-oriented control loops running at **1 – 10 kHz**.
- **Layer 3 — Kinematics & Dynamics Solvers:** Forward/Inverse kinematics, Jacobian-based velocity mapping, inverse dynamics.
- **Layer 4 — Perception & State Estimation:** Visual odometry, object detection (CNN), occupancy grid mapping.
- **Layer 5 — Planning:** Path planning (A*, RRT*), motion planning (CHOMP, STOMP), task planning (PDDL).
- **Layer 6 — Learning & Reasoning (AI/ML):** RL policies, behaviour cloning, LLM-based task planning.
- **Layer 7 — Human–Robot Interaction:** Speech, gesture, safety monitoring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **why** behind this layering: each layer operates at a different time-scale. Low-level control is real-time (kHz); planning is mid-level (10–100 Hz); AI reasoning is slow (Hz or less). Decoupling them keeps the system stable.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars Where AI/ML Replaces/Enhances Classical Methods

| Classical (Model-Based) | AI/ML-Enhanced | Why AI/ML Wins |
|---|---|---|
| Template matching for object detection | CNN (YOLO, Mask R-CNN) | Robust to lighting, occlusion, scale |
| Hard-coded manipulation sequences | RL-trained grasp policies | Adapts to novel objects |
| Geometric SLAM (EKF, particle filter) | Deep SLAM (e.g., ORB-SLAM3 + DROID-SLAM) | Handles texture-less scenes |
| Hand-tuned PID gains | Auto-tuned via Bayesian optimisation | Faster commissioning |
| Predefined trajectory playback | Imitation / LfD | Reduces programming time by **80 %** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Module 4

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulae are the **high-yield equations** students must memorise for ESE and continuous evaluation. All are KTU-syllabus-aligned.

### 2.3.1 Four-Bar Linkage — Grashof's Law & Velocity Analysis

$$
\text{Grashof Condition: } s + l \le p + q
$$

where $s$ = shortest, $l$ = longest, $p, q$ = other two links.

### 2.3.2 Quick-Return Mechanism (Whitworth / Crank-Shaper) — Instantaneous Centre Method

The number of instantaneous centres in a four-bar mechanism:

$$
N_{IC} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = 6
$$

Velocity of point $B$ on the coupler using IC $I_{13}$:

$$
v_B = \omega_{12} \cdot \overline{I_{12} I_{13}}
$$

Quick-return ratio:

$$
Q = \frac{T_{cut}}{T_{ret}} = \frac{180^{\circ} + 2\phi}{180^{\circ} - 2\phi}
$$

where $2\phi$ is the crank angle corresponding to the cutting stroke.

### 2.3.3 Cam Mechanism — Synthesis for Given Motion

Displacement of follower for **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**:

$$
y(\theta) = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]
$$

Velocity:

$$
v(\theta) = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \beta}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)
$$

Acceleration:

$$
a(\theta) = \frac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2 \beta^{2}}\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)
$$

### 2.3.4 Pantograph — Scaling Ratio

$$
\frac{OC}{OA} = \frac{OD}{OB} = k \quad (\text{scale factor})
$$

Output displacement $y$:

$$
y = k \cdot x
$$

### 2.3.5 Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) Parameters — Robot Kinematics

Homogeneous transformation between adjacent links:

$$
A_i = \text{Rot}_{z,\theta_i} \cdot \text{Trans}_{z,d_i} \cdot \text{Trans}_{x,a_i} \cdot \text{Rot}_{x,\alpha_i}
$$

$$
A_i = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_i & -s\theta_i c\alpha_i & s\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i c\theta_i \\ s\theta_i & c\theta_i c\alpha_i & -c\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i s\theta_i \\ 0 & s\alpha_i & c\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

End-effector pose:

$$
T_n = A_1 A_2 A_3 \cdots A_n
$$

### 2.3.6 Forward Kinematics of 2-DOF Planar Arm

$$
x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

### 2.3.7 Inverse Kinematics of 2-DOF Arm

$$
\theta_2 = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{L_2 \sin\theta_2}{L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2}\right)
$$

### 2.3.8 Jacobian (Planar 2R)

$$
J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s\theta_1 - L_2 s(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2 s(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1 c\theta_1 + L_2 c(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2 c(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

End-effector velocity:

$$
\dot{x} = J(\theta)\,\dot{\theta}
$$

### 2.3.9 Reinforcement Learning — Bellman Equation

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s)\!\left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) V^{\pi}(s') \right]
$$

### 2.3.10 Gripper Design — Gripping Force

For a two-finger parallel gripper on a cylindrical object (coefficient of friction $\mu$, mass $m$, safety factor $n$):

$$
F_{grip} = \frac{n \cdot m \cdot g}{2 \mu}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AI/ML** allows robots to leave the structured factory floor and enter **warehouses, hospitals, and roads** — markets projected at over **\$150 billion by 2030**.
- **Mechanism design projects** (four-bar, cam, pantograph) are the **physical backbone** of every robot — without sound linkage design, even the smartest AI cannot reach a target.
- **DH parameters** are the **universal language** of robot kinematics used by ROS, MATLAB Robotics Toolbox, and every CAD/CAM platform.
- The **gripper** is often the **bottleneck** of a pick-and-place cell — proper force analysis is non-negotiable.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Planar Robot Arm

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a planar RR manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ in the $xy$-plane. Joint angles are $\theta_1$ (shoulder) and $\theta_2$ (elbow), both measured from the preceding link.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Position of elbow joint $E$ in the base frame:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 \\
E_y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Absolute angle of link 2** is the sum of the joint angles:

$$
\theta_{abs} = \theta_1 + \theta_2
$$

**Step 3 — Position of end-effector $P$** (adding the second link vector):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_x &= E_x + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
P_y &= E_y + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compact matrix form using DH convention:**

$$
A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 & L_1 c_1 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 & L_1 s_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & L_2 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & L_2 s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
T_2 = A_1 A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_{12} & -s_{12} & 0 & L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} \\ s_{12} & c_{12} & 0 & L_1 s_1 + L_2 s_{12} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ and $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$.

**Step 5 — Reading the position vector** from the 4th column confirms the closed-form result.

> **Conversion logic:** Each $A_i$ represents one rigid link. Multiplying them accumulates all rotations and translations, so the 4th column of $T_n$ is the end-effector position expressed in the base frame.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Kinematics of the 2-DOF Arm

Given target $(x, y)$, find $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$.

**Step 1 — Square and add the two FK equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} \\
y &= L_1 s_1 + L_2 s_{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

Squaring and adding:

$$
x^2 + y^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 (c_1 c_{12} + s_1 s_{12})
$$

Using the cosine difference identity $c_1 c_{12} + s_1 s_{12} = \cos\theta_2$:

$$
x^2 + y^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\theta_2$:**

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \pm \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_1$:**

Take the ratio $y/x$ and apply $\tan^{-1}$:

$$
\tan\theta_1 = \frac{y(L_1 + L_2 c_2) - x L_2 s_2}{x(L_1 + L_2 c_2) + y L_2 s_2}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \text{atan2}\!\left(y(L_1 + L_2 c_2) - x L_2 s_2,\; x(L_1 + L_2 c_2) + y L_2 s_2\right)
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Quick-Return Ratio for a Crank-Shaper Mechanism

Let the cutting stroke span crank angle $2\phi$ and the return stroke span $360^{\circ} - 2\phi$ (uniform crank speed $\omega$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cut} &= \frac{2\phi}{\omega} \\
T_{ret} &= \frac{360^{\circ} - 2\phi}{\omega} \\
Q &= \frac{T_{cut}}{T_{ret}} = \frac{2\phi}{360^{\circ} - 2\phi}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the question expresses the cutting angle as $180^{\circ} + 2\phi$ and return as $180^{\circ} - 2\phi$ (Whitworth):

$$
Q = \frac{180^{\circ} + 2\phi}{180^{\circ} - 2\phi}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Instantaneous Centre Method (Four-Bar)

Number of ICs for $n$ links:

$$
N_{IC} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

For $n = 4$: $N_{IC} = 6$. Three are known (at the pin joints), three are unknown. Apply **Kennedy's theorem**: any three bodies sharing a common IC must have their relative ICs collinear. This yields the Aronhold–Kennedy equations that locate $I_{13}, I_{14}, I_{24}$.

Once $I_{13}$ is located, the velocity of $B$ (on the coupler) is:

$$
v_B = \omega_{12} \cdot \overline{I_{12} I_{13}}
$$

This is the **classic KTU-asked method** for the Quick-Return Velocity Analysis question.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Cam Follower (SHM) — Velocity & Acceleration Peaks

From the displacement equation:

$$
y(\theta) = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]
$$

Differentiate w.r.t. time using $\dot{\theta} = \omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v(\theta) &= \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{dy}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \beta}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right) \\
a(\theta) &= \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2 \beta^{2}}\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Peak values:**

$$
v_{max} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \beta}, \qquad a_{max} = \frac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2 \beta^{2}}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The peak occurs when the trigonometric argument reaches $90^{\circ}$ (velocity) or $0^{\circ}$ (acceleration). The factor $h/2$ arises because the SHM stroke must go from $0$ to $h$ in the angular interval $\beta$.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Pantograph — Linear Scaling Proof

A symmetric pantograph has four bars forming a parallelogram: $OABC$. Tracing point $C$ yields scaled motion of point $A$ (tracer) on the fixed pivot $O$.

Let $k = \overline{OC} / \overline{OA} = \overline{OD} / \overline{OB}$ (similar triangles).

Position vectors in the plane:

$$
\vec{OC} = k \cdot \vec{OA}, \quad \vec{OD} = k \cdot \vec{OB}
$$

Since the parallelogram preserves orientation:

$$
\vec{CD} = k \cdot \vec{AB}
$$

So the motion of $C$ is a **scaled, rotated copy** of the motion of $A$, and any figure traced at $A$ appears at $C$ magnified by $k$ and rotated by $180^{\circ}$.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AI/ML-Enabled Robotic System — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Perception Sensors: RGB-D, LiDAR, IMU, Force Torque] --> B1[Layer 4: Perception and State Estimation]
    B1 --> B2[Layer 5: Planning A star, RRT, CHOMP]
    B2 --> B3[Layer 6: Learning and Reasoning RL, LLM, IL]
    B3 --> C1[Layer 3: Kinematics and Dynamics Solver]
    C1 --> D1[Layer 2: Low Level Control PID FOC]
    D1 --> E1[Layer 1: Physical Hardware Motors, Drives, Linkages]
    B1 -. CNN Object Detection .-> F1[Knowledge Base: Trained Models]
    B2 -. Path Library .-> F1
    B3 -. RL Policy .-> F1
    F1 -. Continuous Update .-> B1
    E1 --> G1[Physical Environment: Workspace, Objects, Humans]
    G1 --> A1
```

> **Reading guide:** The forward arrows show the **real-time control loop** (kHz to Hz). The dashed arrows show the **learning loop** (slow, offline or background).

## 4.2 DH Frame Assignment — 2-DOF Planar Arm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0[Base Frame: x0 y0 z0, origin at shoulder] --> F1[Frame 1: x1 y1, after rotation theta1 about z0, origin at elbow]
    F1 --> F2[Frame 2: x2 y2, after rotation theta2 about z1, origin at end effector]
    F0 -->|a1 = L1, d1 = 0, alpha1 = 0, theta1 variable| F1
    F1 -->|a2 = L2, d2 = 0, alpha2 = 0, theta2 variable| F2
```

> **Reading guide:** $a_i$ = link length, $d_i$ = link offset, $\alpha_i$ = twist angle, $\theta_i$ = joint angle. For this planar arm, only $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are variables; $a_1 = L_1$, $a_2 = L_2$, all other DH parameters are zero.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions per module, typically 3 marks each. Crisp definitions, no derivations.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*"Explain the role of Reinforcement Learning in robot motion planning. How does it differ from classical model-based control?"*

**Model Answer (Keywords for 3 marks):**
- RL learns an optimal policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ by maximising cumulative reward via the Bellman equation.
- No prior model of the environment is required; learning happens through trial-and-error.
- Classical control requires an exact dynamic model + hand-tuned gains.
- RL excels in **high-dimensional, contact-rich tasks** (walking, dexterous grasping).
- Limitations: sample inefficiency, sim-to-real gap, safety concerns.
- Typical algorithms: DQN, PPO, SAC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse RL with Supervised Learning. State clearly: RL has **no labelled dataset** — it learns from a **reward signal** that may be delayed.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*"Define the four Denavit–Hartenberg parameters. Why is the DH convention preferred over an arbitrary 4×4 transform between links?"*

**Model Answer (Keywords for 3 marks):**
- The four parameters are: **link length $a_i$, link twist $\alpha_i$, link offset $d_i$, joint angle $\theta_i$**.
- Only **two** of these are variables (one revolute or one prismatic), reducing the DOF search space.
- A general homogeneous transform has **6 DOF** — DH uses only **4** by constraining the axis convention.
- DH guarantees **systematic, unambiguous assignment** of frames for any serial chain.
- Universally supported in MATLAB, ROS, and CAD tools.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students mis-assign the $x$ and $z$ axes. Rule to memorise: **the joint axis is always $z_i$**; $x_i$ is along the common normal between $z_i$ and $z_{i+1}$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** 14-mark questions, 2 sub-parts of 7 marks each. Two choices A and B. Both choices are given — answer ANY ONE.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **velocity analysis of a Quick-Return Mechanism (Whitworth / crank-shaper type)** using the **Instantaneous Centre (IC) method**. Show all six instantaneous centres and derive the expression for the velocity of the ram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-bar linkage has link lengths $L_1 = 100$ mm (crank), $L_2 = 250$ mm (coupler), $L_3 = 200$ mm (rocker), $L_4 = 300$ mm (ground). Determine: (i) the type of mechanism by Grashof's law, (ii) the angular velocity of the coupler when the crank is at $60^{\circ}$ and rotates at $\omega_2 = 10$ rad/s, using the IC method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

1. Draw the Whitworth QRM: two cranks of equal length rotating in the same direction, connected by a slotted lever driving a ram. (1 mark)
2. State Kennedy's theorem: any three rigid bodies in plane motion have three ICs, and they are collinear. (1 mark)
3. Number the six ICs: $I_{12}, I_{23}, I_{34}, I_{14}, I_{13}, I_{24}$. (1 mark)
4. Use Aronhold–Kennedy triangle to locate the unknowns graphically. (2 marks)
5. Write the ram velocity: $V_{ram} = \omega_{34} \cdot \overline{I_{34} I_{13}}$. (2 marks)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Grashof's Law:**

$$
s + l = 100 + 300 = 400 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
p + q = 250 + 200 = 450 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $s + l < p + q$ (400 < 450), **Grashof's law is satisfied**. The shortest link is the ground (100 mm? No — shortest is 100 mm = crank, which is not the ground). Therefore it is a **Crank–Rocker mechanism** (input crank rotates fully, output rocker oscillates). (2 marks)

**(ii) Velocity Analysis (IC method):**

Set up the loop: ground = link 1, crank = link 2, coupler = link 3, rocker = link 4.

IC locations: $I_{12}$ at the crank pivot $O_2$, $I_{23}$ at joint $A$, $I_{34}$ at joint $B$, $I_{14}$ at rocker pivot $O_4$. (1 mark)

Apply Kennedy's theorem to triangles $(1,2,3)$, $(1,3,4)$, $(2,3,4)$ to locate $I_{13}, I_{14}, I_{24}$. (1 mark)

Once $I_{13}$ is found geometrically (or by coordinate method), the velocity of $B$ (on link 3) is:

$$
V_B = \omega_2 \cdot \overline{I_{12} I_{13}}
$$

The angular velocity of the coupler:

$$
\omega_3 = \frac{V_B}{\overline{I_{23} I_{13}}}
$$

For a 2D coordinate solution, set $O_2 = (0,0)$, $O_4 = (300, 0)$, $\theta_2 = 60^{\circ}$:

$$
A = (100 \cos 60^{\circ},\; 100 \sin 60^{\circ}) = (50,\; 86.6) \text{ mm}
$$

The position of $B$ is the intersection of circles (radius $L_2 = 250$ around $A$) and (radius $L_3 = 200$ around $O_4$). (2 marks)

Solving: $B \approx (290.6, 175.0)$ mm. The instantaneous centre $I_{13}$ is found by intersecting the line $O_2 I_{13}$ (perpendicular to $V_B$ direction at $A$) and the line $O_4 I_{13}$ (perpendicular to $V_B$ direction at $B$).

Numerical value: $\omega_3 \approx 3.8$ rad/s. (1 mark for the final answer with units)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of an AI/ML-enabled robotic pick-and-place system** for a manufacturing line. Identify the roles of perception, planning, and learning layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **2-DOF planar robot arm** with $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.4$ m, compute: (i) the end-effector position when $\theta_1 = 30^{\circ}$ and $\theta_2 = 45^{\circ}$, (ii) the joint angles required to reach the point $(0.6, 0.4)$ m using inverse kinematics. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks):**

1. Block diagram: Camera → CNN Detector → Pose Estimator → Grasp Planner (RL) → IK Solver → Joint Controllers → Gripper. (3 marks)
2. Role of perception: detects object type, 6D pose, grasp affordance. (1 mark)
3. Role of planning: computes collision-free trajectory using RRT* or CHOMP. (1 mark)
4. Role of learning: RL policy improves grasp success over time; imitation learning from human demos. (1 mark)
5. Mention ROS 2 as the integration middleware, Gazebo for simulation, and digital twin. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Forward Kinematics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
  &= 0.5 \cos 30^{\circ} + 0.4 \cos 75^{\circ} \\
  &= 0.5 \times 0.866 + 0.4 \times 0.2588 \\
  &= 0.433 + 0.1035 = 0.5365 \text{ m} \\
y &= 0.5 \sin 30^{\circ} + 0.4 \sin 75^{\circ} \\
  &= 0.5 \times 0.5 + 0.4 \times 0.9659 \\
  &= 0.25 + 0.3864 = 0.6364 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

So end-effector position is $(0.5365,\; 0.6364)$ m. (3 marks)

**(ii) Inverse Kinematics:**

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}
$$

$$
x^2 + y^2 = 0.36 + 0.16 = 0.52, \quad L_1^2 + L_2^2 = 0.25 + 0.16 = 0.41
$$

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{0.52 - 0.41}{2 \times 0.5 \times 0.4} = \frac{0.11}{0.4} = 0.275
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.275) = 74.04^{\circ} \quad \text{(elbow-down)} \quad \text{or} \quad -74.04^{\circ} \quad \text{(elbow-up)}
$$

Using elbow-down:

$$
\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y, x) - \text{atan2}(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)
$$

$$
\text{atan2}(0.4, 0.6) = 33.69^{\circ}
$$

$$
L_2 \sin\theta_2 = 0.4 \times 0.9614 = 0.3846
$$

$$
L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 = 0.5 + 0.4 \times 0.275 = 0.5 + 0.11 = 0.61
$$

$$
\text{atan2}(0.3846, 0.61) = 32.23^{\circ}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 33.69^{\circ} - 32.23^{\circ} = 1.46^{\circ}
$$

So joint solution (elbow-down): $\theta_1 = 1.46^{\circ}$, $\theta_2 = 74.04^{\circ}$. (4 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention for $\theta_2$**: Always state whether the elbow is up or down. The two solutions are equally valid — examiners dock marks if only one is given without justification.
> 2. **Units in Grashof problem**: Mixing mm with m loses 1 mark instantly. Convert all lengths to a single unit.
> 3. **DH parameter order**: Students frequently write $d_i$ before $a_i$ — KTU expects the order $a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i$.
> 4. **IC method**: Forgetting the line construction for the unknown IC costs 3 of 7 marks.
> 5. **ML questions**: Naming "TensorFlow" or "PyTorch" is not a substitute for explaining the *algorithm* (e.g., DQN, PPO).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- **AI vs ML vs DL:** AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL. Robotics uses all three.
- **RL Core Equation:** Bellman equation; policy $\pi$; discount factor $\gamma \in [0,1)$.
- **SLAM** = Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping — backbone of mobile robots.
- **CNN** = Convolutional Neural Network — used for vision-based grasping and object detection.
- **LSTM** = Long Short-Term Memory — used for predictive maintenance on time-series sensor data.
- **Grashof's Law:** $s + l \le p + q$ → classifies the four-bar as Grashof (at least one full-revolute link) or non-Grashof.
- **Quick-Return Ratio:** $Q = T_{cut} / T_{ret} > 1$ by definition.
- **IC Method:** Always 6 ICs for 4-bar; locate unknowns using Aronhold–Kennedy triangles.
- **Cam SHM:** Peak velocity $= \pi h \omega / 2\beta$; Peak acceleration $= \pi^2 h \omega^2 / 2\beta^2$.
- **Pantograph:** Output is a scaled, $180^{\circ}$-rotated copy of input; $k = OC/OA$.
- **DH Parameters:** Always assign $z_i$ along the joint axis; $x_i$ along the common normal; only 2 of $(a, \alpha, d, \theta)$ are joint variables per link.
- **2-DOF FK:** Trigonometric sum of two link vectors.
- **2-DOF IK:** Use law of cosines for $\theta_2$; atan2 formulation for $\theta_1$; two solutions (elbow up/down).
- **Jacobian:** Relates joint velocities to Cartesian velocities; $\dot{x} = J \dot{\theta}$.
- **Gripper force:** $F_{grip} = n m g / (2 \mu)$ for a two-finger gripper.
- **Safety factor $n$:** Typically **2 to 5** in industrial gripper design.
- **Project deliverables:** A typical KTU mini-project report must include: problem statement, design calculations, MATLAB/Python plots, simulation (ROS/Gazebo) screenshot, conclusions, and references.
- **Sample sizes for ML:** Grasping datasets (e.g., YCB, GraspNet) contain **1 M+ labelled images** for training robust CNNs.
- **Real-time constraint:** Low-level control at 1 kHz; perception at 30 Hz; AI reasoning at 1–10 Hz.
